File.Name: b624e_b624-027.jpg
Notes: 67540255 This is looking into the secret heart of (I think) a native Butterfly Mariposa Lily (Calochortus venustus) in the Liliaceae plant family - but cp. C. catalinae. They are beautiful and strange, like a mandala. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

File.Name: b624f_b624-049.jpg
Notes:
67540089 These are interesting new flowers of native Broad-leaved Milkweed (Asclepias eriocarpa) in the Asclepiadaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

The humble milkweeds are really our most advanced flowers, except maybe the orchids. They have sepals and petals like most flowers, but they also have another ring of unusual structures above them called the corona.

See my photo and explantaion at: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8108247

File.Name: b625a_b621-035.jpg
Notes:
67545721 The colors of summer... and wildfires!

This is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today working new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

Butterflies are still scarce in our mountains, but I find them every day. Our Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden is flowering - without butterflies! It's not right.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Golden Yarrow; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: b625b_b624-044.jpg
Notes: 67545639 This beautiful Wasp (Hymenoptera) is working summer flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess it's an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin) from the shape of its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

File.Name: b625c_b624-012.jpg
Notes: 67545524 This is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) collecting pollen from the stamens of a native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) flower in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. If you know about fly wing venation, you can spot the "spurious vein" in its wings. The fly's abdomen looks translucent. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

File.Name: b625d_b625-020.jpg
Notes:
67545446 Doesn't it cool you off just to look at this sweet-smelling Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family!

I appreciate that "yerba buena" means any local plant that smells like peppermint, but in California this is THE yerba buena, the original namesake of the city of San Francisco. It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It's not common in our mountains, though there is a large colony in our canyon. You can see a few flowers in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

The plant seems to be related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants in the Satureja genus, but it's been recently renamed as Clinopodium douglasii, which makes the relationship obscure. Those darned botanists!
ScientificName: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Satureja; Clinopodium douglasii
associatedTaxa: Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae; Satureja; Clinopodium douglasii

File.Name: b625e_b625-003.jpg
Notes: 67545309 I believe these are the very glandular new flower heads of Coastal Tarweed (Madia sativa) in the Asteraceae plant family. The pretty composite flowers open and close early in the morning, so I rarely see them. We also have the smaller Madia gracilis in our mountains, and some plants that seem to be intermediate between the two. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

File.Name: b625f_b622-016.jpg
Notes:
67545193 These are new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. They have two stamens and one blue-tipped pistil. They start out folded up tight like this, so that only heavy insects like Carpenter Bees (Anthophoridae) can lever them open. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

The leaves have a strong smell of camphor, like Vicks. They are widely used for "smudge sticks" - like natural incense. We rescued this plant from a slide along Paradise Road years ago. It's doing well in the garden.

File.Name: b626a_b626-010.jpg
Notes:
67550411 This is "Blue" butterfly despite the lack of colors.

I guess it is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil at a spring-fed wet place in the chaparral. Note its thin outstretched proboscis. It has blue upper wings, and it looks quite blue when it flies. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2011)

File.Name: b626b_b625-005.jpg
Notes: 67550290 This is a tattered Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. Butterflies have a tough life, though we don't appreciate it with all the flowers and sunshine. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

File.Name: b626c_b626-005.jpg
Notes:
67550238 This is a beautiful Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) perched on a fascicle of leaves of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family growing by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2011)

I didn't even try to figure out which of the many kinds of skipper this is - I just "skip her" to make life easy!

File.Name: b626d_b625-024.jpg
Notes:
67550175 These are fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on the rotted wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I guess it is genus Stemonitis. They look like fungi, but they are really animals! (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

For more info about these remarkable critters, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67474528

File.Name: b626e_b626-012.jpg
Notes: 67550067 This is a tiny native flower of Mimulus floribundus in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It is growing in a spring-fed wet place in the chaparral on ground burned by the Gap Wildfire three years ago, though I don't count it as a fire-follower. The glandular leaves feel distinctly slimy when you grasp them. It's uncommon in our mountains, like the habitat. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2011)

File.Name: b626f_b625-010.jpg
Notes:
67549944 Grasses have flowers?

Well yes, but they are wind-pollinated, so the flower structures are reduced to a minimum. This is a flowering native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the canyon. If you look close, you can see the stamens poking out from the spiklets. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2011)

Grasses are particularly difficult to photograph and not over-expose. I had to stop down 2-1/3 stops from what my camera thought was right.

File.Name: b627a_b627-031.jpg
Notes: 67556538 This is a perfect little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly "puddling" on dried up algae by the Santa Ynez River today. You can see the emerald iridescent speckles on the "eyes" of its hind wings. What can I say? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2011)

File.Name: b627b_b627-007.jpg
Notes:
67556354 This little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. It's pushing back with it's hind legs to open the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2011)

I guess it is a family Megachilidae bee because it has also been collecting pollen on its butt. It's forcing apart the flower to get to the nectar. If you look close, it has strange mouthparts that can disarticulate at a strange angle, almost like it's made for this task.
ScientificName: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: b627c_b626-001.jpg
Notes: 67556165 Here is a Giant Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera resting on leaves of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It has a long philosopher's face. (San Marcos Pass, 26 June 2011)

File.Name: b627d_b627-036.jpg
Notes:
67556091 Here is an amazing new flower of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) - our native Tiger Lily. It looks like it belongs in a garden, but this one is growing wild along Stagecoach Road. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2011)

I happened to get my first photo of a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly today. Flower and butterfly are made to be together. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/7999731

File.Name: b627e_b627-031.jpg
Notes: 67555864 These are remarkable new flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing on a sandy high place along the Santa Ynez River. They have an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2011)

File.Name: b627f_b627-028.jpg
Notes: 67555737 These are beautiful new flowers of perennial Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family that are still flowering in the woods along the "river trail" at the Sage Hill Campground today. Some flower stalks are 7 feet tall! It's been a good year for summer flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 June 2011)

File.Name: b628a_b628-066.jpg
Notes:
67562087 This beautiful Gorgon Copper (Lycaena gorgon) looks like any other "blue" butterfly from below, but she is remarkable above. The males are copper-colored above. I captured this one working flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family, but I also found them on the Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family growing nearby, and I believe that is their host plant. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

Today was a good day for butterflies - especially the Gossamer-Wing Butterflies in the family Lycaenidae - the Coppers, Blues, and Hairstreaks. I was getting worried, but a week of warm weather is finally bringing them out.

If you enjoy photos of local butterflies, you should check out all six of my photos today. I don't get to do this very often!
CommonNames: Gorgon Copper; Golden Yarrow; Longstem Buckwheat; Gossamer-Wing Butterflies
ScientificName: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Eriogonum elongatum; Polygonaceae; Lycaenidae
associatedTaxa: Lycaena gorgon; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Eriogonum elongatum; Polygonaceae; Lycaenidae

File.Name: b628b_b628-043.jpg
Notes: 67561938 Here is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly resting on a twig of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family today. This butterfly is female. She has tails like a hairstreak. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Canyon Gooseberry

File.Name: b628c_b628-035.jpg
Notes: 67561773 This is a Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly ovipositing on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. She has a thin dark "clouded" upper wing margin which is not visible in my photo. I kept trying to get around to her other side to show the clouded wings with backlight, but it didn't happen. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)
ScientificName: Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Colias harfordii; C. alexandra harfordii; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b628d_b628-028.jpg
Notes: 67561689 Here is a beautiful Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. She's a lady. A few minutes later I captured her laying eggs on another deerweed, but this is the better photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b628e_b628-027.jpg
Notes: 67561623 This is a handsome California Hairstreak (Satyrium californica) butterfly working flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has small "tails" like most hairstreaks. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b628f_b628-015.jpg
Notes: 67561556 Here is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly feeding on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. She's a female, quite brown on top. These butterflies are like ducks - flashy males and duller females. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b629a_b629-002.jpg
Notes:
67567842 "Rollin' In My Sweet Baby's Arms..."

Here are a mated pair of large Darkling Beetles (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera, probably genus Eleodes) - with legs entwined. Their nuptial bed is an old Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushroom in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2011)

The kids call these "Stink Beetles" - and harass them mercilessly. I prefer the old name "Pinacate Beetles". I look out for them.

It was cloudy and breezy today as a little front moved through. We actually had some drizzle this morning, unusual for June. But the overcast was perfect to capture these shiny-black beetles.
CommonNames: Darkling Beetles; Spring Agrocybe; Pinacate Beetles

File.Name: b629b_b628-019.jpg
Notes: 67567670 This is a different Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working the same flowers of native Turkish Rigging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family as my [Previous] photo. It has the same stout proboscis, but this fly doesn't hover. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b629c_b628-032.jpg
Notes: 67567527 I captured this tiny Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera dancing above the flowers of native Turkish Rigging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Their aim is amazing. I reckon it's holding himself down, not up! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b629d_b629-014.jpg
Notes: 67567395 This goofy Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) was standing up on a rock in the garden today. I got the photo - I wonder what it was up to? (San Marcos Pass, 29 June 2011)

File.Name: b629e_b628-045.jpg
Notes: 67567310 These are the amazing summer flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family, up close. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them here and there. These are growing near Arroyo Burro Pass in the mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b629f_b628-050.jpg
Notes: 67567270 These are beautiful summer-blue native flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing near Arroyo Burro Pass in the mountains. There are LOTS of these flowers here. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b630a_b630-055.jpg
Notes:
67573425 I believe this is a Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium) butterfly perched on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

We have three very similar Hairstreak butterflies in our mountains - the Hedgerow Hairstreak (Satyrium saepium), the Gold Hunter's Hairstreak (Satyrium auretorum), and the Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak (Satyrium tetra). I can't tell them apart, but I believe this is the Hedgerow Hairstreak because it has rusty orange upper wings - in another photo.

Today was a perfect day for photos - warm, but not hot and not too windy. It's heating up for the Fourth of July - not good, because people have a crazy notion to light fireworks, which cause wildfires. We'll be patrolling.
Locality: Satyrium tetra). I can't tell them apart
CommonNames: Hedgerow Hairstreak; Deerweed; Hairstreak; Hedgerow Hairstreak; Gold Hunter's Hairstreak; Mountain Mahogany Hairstreak; Hedgerow Hairstreak
ScientificName: Satyrium saepium; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium tetra
associatedTaxa: Satyrium saepium; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Satyrium saepium; Satyrium auretorum; Satyrium tetra

File.Name: b630b_b630-062.jpg
Notes: 67573276 This is another little Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly feeding on native flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I love the speckles on its tail eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

File.Name: b630c_b630-029.jpg
Notes:
67573225 Here is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today working new flowers of endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. It's a beautiful butterfly, and it doesn't mind me getting close with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

Butterflies have been scarce this year, but there are dozens (rather than hundreds) of these butterflies in the woods right now. This is their time.

File.Name: b630d_b630-054.jpg
Notes:
67573140 Black and white...

This is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the mountains. Sometimes the flowers are white, sometimes lavender. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011) There are few plants more beautiful than the native thistles - and few uglier than the non-native weedy thistles. If you are doing weed control in our mountains, be sure you can tell the difference. If unsure, leave it be!

File.Name: b630e_b630-026.jpg
Notes: 67572981 Here is a (another) remarkable dancing tiny Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera dancing above the flowers of endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. How can I resist capturing these photos day after day? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

File.Name: b630f_b630-016.jpg
Notes:
67572906 "Are you looking at me?"

This tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) with punk hair thinks he's hiding behind a flower of native succulent Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family - but I see him! (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

File.Name: b701a_b630-012.jpg
Notes:
67581556 Here is a wonderful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on the new flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. The Buddleia is flowering high up, so this is a "tele-macro" shot with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens from our shaky upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

My photos today show all three of our native swallowtail butterflies, working the same plant. This is our smallest and least common swallowtail - but it's still a large butterfly. It's name is a misnomer. It's a native butterfly that often uses non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae family for its host plant, though I guess it can use other plants in the Apiaceae. We always leave a few fennel plants just for the butterflies, and try to remember to trim off the flowers before they set seed.
Locality: Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae family for its host plant
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Buddleia davidii; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Buddleia davidii; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: b701b_b627-001.jpg
Notes: 67581487 This is my first photo of a Western Tiger Swallowtail (Papilio rutulus) butterfly this year. This is our largest swallowtail. It is working the new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. The Buddleia is flowering high up, so this is a "tele-macro" shot with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens from our shaky upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 27 June 2011)

File.Name: b701c_b628-008.jpg
Notes: 67581456 Here is a Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. The Buddleia is flowering high up, so this is a "tele-macro" shot with my long Tamron 200-500 mm lens from our shaky upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 28 June 2011)

File.Name: b702a_b702-021.jpg
Notes:
67584377 Here is another wonderful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on the flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon today. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2011)

I got all of today's photos on the native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing across the canyon. It's a wonderful butterfly flower.

I'm still waiting for Callippe Fritillary (Speyeria callippe) butterflies, which favor this plant. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/21594241

It was HOT today - maybe too hot for butterflies, though I found a few. It was 96 ∞F, about 36 ∞C. The word is that tomorrow will be even hotter - 100 ∞F, 40+ ∞C. It's not the best timing for a heat wave, with Independence Day coming up when yahoos love to set of fireworks. I hope they don't do it near our house!
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Callippe Fritillary
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Speyeria callippe

File.Name: b702b_b702-026.jpg
Notes:
67584172 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon. The butterflies have just two eye-spots under their remarkable wings.

These butterflies favor Gnaphalium "everlasting flowers" flowers in the Asteraceae plant family. I'll have a photo of the caterpillars tomorrow.
Locality:
67584172 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly working the new flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon. The butterflies have just two eye-spots under their remarkable wings.

These butterflies favor Gnaphalium "everlasting flowers" flowers in the Asteraceae plant family. I'll have a photo of the caterpillars tomorrow.
CommonNames: American Lady; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: b702c_b702-016.jpg
Notes:
67584079 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) has captured a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera. It's on a stem of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon. You can see the fly's long proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2011)

The spider is moving its prey. I nudged it a bit from the deep shade of a flower cluster, and captured the image as it moved away.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Bee Fly; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Bombyliidae; Diptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae

File.Name: b702d_b702-011.jpg
Notes:
67583913 I apologize that this photos isn't quite sharp, but this remarkable bee-wasp moves rapidly from flower to flower and is impossible to follow. All you can do is pick a flower to focus on and wait. I was lucky to get this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2011)

The flowers are native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon.

I believe it is in the genus Steniolia of the tribe of Sand Wasps in the Bembicini of the Apoid Wasps (Apoidea) of the Hymenoptera. I give thanks to Hartmut Wisch for helping me with a previous photo of this critter. I hope I got it right this time.

Look close under the knuckle of its left rear leg. This wasp seems to have an extra "dingus" below its second abdominal segment. I'm not sure what that is. It also appears to have pollen-collecting hairs on its rear legs.
CommonNames: Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Sand Wasps
ScientificName: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Steniolia; Bembicini; Hymenoptera

File.Name: b702e_b702-014.jpg
Notes:
67583536 I can't resist - this is another Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today working new summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the canyon. So pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2011)

This year there are dozens of these butterflies in the woods, but not hundreds like years past. Something happened to the checkerspot population a couple of years ago.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: b702f_b702-019.jpg
Notes:
67583450 "Sorry, this flower is taken."

This is another Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly working the summer flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon today. Look - there's an incoming Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the upper-right! She changed her mind when she saw the flower was already taken. (San Marcos Pass, 2 July 2011)
CommonNames: Anise Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Variable Checkerspot
ScientificName: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Papilio zelicaon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: b703a_b628-010.jpg
Notes:
67589367 This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working the new flowers of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. Look at all the pollen on her head - maybe from Salvia greggii in the garden? (San Marcos Pass, 30 June 2011)

I almost forgot about this photo, but it's a keeper!

It was actually a few degrees cooler than yesterday, rather than hotter. Not that I noticed any difference, but it's good to know.
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Buddleia davidii; Salvia greggii
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Buddleia davidii; Salvia greggii

File.Name: b703b_b703-004.jpg
Notes: 67589285 This isn't a Walking Stick insect (order Phasmatodea) - but it is a Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. You can see its spike-like proboscis folded under its head. It's perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This is the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2011)
CommonNames: Walking Stick; Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Phasmatodea; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Phasmatodea; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: b703c_b703-022.jpg
Notes:
67589163 This little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working the stamen of a flower of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden today. It's collecting pollen on its abdomen, so I suspect it is in the family Megachilidae. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2011)

These bees also work the same flowers for nectar - see my photo from last week at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67556354

File.Name: b703d_b703-010.jpg
Notes:
67588988 "Jumps on a man like a dog on a bone" - Holy Modal Rounders

The little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) on the right was quietly working a late flower of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, when the other wild bee jumped on it! I'm not sure if it's love play or territorial dispute - but the bees are different size, so I suspect they are male and female. I saw this happen several times. It happened too quick to be consummated. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2011)

File.Name: b703e_b703-035.jpg
Notes:
67588792 Here is a whole candelabra of new flowers and buds of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. It looks like they belong in a garden, but this one is growing wild a mile from home along the upper parts of San Jose Creek. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2011)

Perfect - except they should be full of swallowtail butterflies.

File.Name: b703f_b703-038.jpg
Notes: 67588700 These are new flowers of Humboldt Lily (Lilium humboldtii ssp. ocellatum) in the Liliaceae plant family - our native Tiger Lily. It looks like they belong in a garden, but this one is growing wild a mile from home along the upper parts of San Jose Creek. (San Marcos Pass, 3 July 2011)

File.Name: b704a_b704-007.jpg
Notes:
67595046 Here is a perfect male Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched on a dead stem in the woods by the river today. What can I say? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 July 2011)

Today was another hot day, so I went to the river for a dip and a few photos. I think it's too hot for butterflies, at least I didn't see them. It was muggy today - high humidity and high temps. There were thunderheads over the back country. I'll be glad when this heat wave is over!

File.Name: b704b_b704-006.jpg
Notes:
67594926 This is a beautiful green oh-so-green Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) in the Solanaceae plant family in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2011)

They are little predators. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/39512351

Note that its head is scooped out between the eyes. This is also so with dragonflies and damselflies (see my [Next] photo) and robber flies. I guess it helps them with depth perception to catch prey on the wing.

File.Name: b704c_b703-001.jpg
Notes:
67594746 The nymph of Tom Joad?

This is the beautiful ghost-colored nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) that I found today on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. I noticed the antennae poking out from under a leaf, and nudged it up for a photo.

I wonder if it's the nymph of a Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae) - the only white long-horned orthopteran I know. It's lying in a similar pose to the adults. (San Marcos Pass, 4 July 2011)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Nuttall's Snowberry; Snowy Tree Cricket

File.Name: b704d_b704-015.jpg
Notes:
67594546 This is beautiful native Red Thistle aka Cobweb Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. venustum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This one is growing on the dry flats along the river, though I've also seen them near the summits of our mountains. The only thing missing is butterflies! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 July 2011)

There are few plants more beautiful than the native thistles - and few uglier than the non-native weedy thistles. If you are doing weed control in our mountains, be sure you can tell the difference. If unsure, leave it be!

File.Name: b704e_b704-012.jpg
Notes:
67594488 Here are new flowers of native Mustang Mint (Monardella lanceolata) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing with the annual grasses on a sandy place along the Santa Ynez River. They have an intense peppermint smell that is shocking when you walk through a patch of it without knowing the source. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 July 2011)

I sometimes wonder if I'm the only person who knows they are here. They are growing on an old Chumash Indian midden near the river, in a place where no one walks but me. The plant is not mentioned in Jan Timbrook's recent book on Chumash Ethnobotany, but I think it should be.

File.Name: b704f_b704-021.jpg
Notes: 67594370 This interesting flowering plant is native Coastal Brookfoam (Boykinia occidentalis) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They are scarce in our mountains, like their habitat on live summer streams. I got this photo today where one of the few perennial summer streams empties into the Santa Ynez River. Don't ask - I won't say where it is! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 4 July 2011)

File.Name: b705a_b705-032.jpg
Notes:
67600778 It looks less like an ant as an adult, but it still moves in an ant-like way. If I'm right, this is Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on native flowers of glandular Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its thorax. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

It was cooler today, and breezy. The wing was coming from the ocean for a change, cooling us off.

File.Name: b705b_b705-036.jpg
Notes:
67600522 This was the damnedest thing...

I was focused on the large Blister Beetle in the family Meloidae of the insect order Coleoptera working flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Then this little "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera walked up and... stuck its proboscis into the beetles exposed abdomen!

The beetle would have let out a "yelp" if he could! The beetle took wing with the bug still attached. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

It all happened so fast, I'm still not sure what I saw - but I think that covers it. I guess this is an awesome new predator bug, at least new to me.

File.Name: b705c_b705-006.jpg
Notes: 67600383 These cute little Beetles (order Coleoptera) are "doing it" on a new summer flower of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. He has mating on his mind, but she seems mostly interested in feeding on the pollen. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

File.Name: b705d_b705-030.jpg
Notes: 67600212 This yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a Parasitic Wasp in the order Hymenoptera, though I don't know what kind. You can see the poor wasp's ovipositor below. This spider has had better luck capturing these interesting wasps than I have! The action is taking place on flowers of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

File.Name: b705e_b705-011.jpg
Notes: 67600148 Insect eggs - I don't know (or much care) which kind - on the flower of a Dudleya in the mountains, probably the Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family. It appears to be a successful hatch! (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

File.Name: b705f_b705-018.jpg
Notes: 67600029 Here are beautiful new flowers of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family - lots of them. With late rains and few caterpillars, they are having a great year. Our hummingbirds have abandoned our feeders, and I guess this hummingbird feast in the woods is why. (San Marcos Pass, 5 July 2011)

File.Name: b706a_b706-024.jpg
Notes:
67606543 Here are a mating pair of Mugwort Weevils - at least that's what they should be called.

They are tiny weevil beetles in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occur on the leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae family. They have strange anteater-like faces with the antennae down below the eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2011)

Here they are making babies - at least they're in the right position to do so. Something seems to be missing to consummate this wedding!

File.Name: b706b_b706-003.jpg
Notes:
67606371 This tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working a flower head of native Green Everlasting (Gnaphalium californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look at its full pollen baskets. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2011)

These hardly seem to be "real flowers", but these bees know that they are. The flower heads remind me of little fireworks or volcanoes - cool white exterior and sizzling yellow interior!

File.Name: b706c_b706-013.jpg
Notes:
67606180 Here is the first shy female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly of the summer trying to hide while working flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2011)

This is my first California Dogface butterfly photo of the summer, but NOT my first of the year. See my photo at:

File.Name: b706d_b706-035.jpg

File.Name: b706e_b706-016.jpg
Notes:
67606019 These are new female flowers of native Galium angustifolium in the Rubiaceae plant family - the same family as coffee.

We have many different species of Galium in our mountains, and they are a favorite of mine, though the flowers are quite plain and hard to tell apart. Most plants have leaves in whorls of four and four-petaled flowers. These lady-flowers have a fuzzy ovary below them. (San Marcos Pass, 6 July 2011)

Galium angustifolium stands out because it is a real self-supporting shrub. It is also "dioecious" which means it has male and female flowers on different plants. From a distance, the male flowers look yellow and the female flowers look white. These are the female seed-bearing flowers. Males and females happen to be growing right next to each other, and I've seen that before. (See my [Previous] photo.)

It's another one of those plants that grows in our canyon that could probably disappear without changing anything - though the diversity of our woods would be the poorer without them.
ScientificName: Galium angustifolium; Rubiaceae; Galium; Galium angustifolium
associatedTaxa: Galium angustifolium; Rubiaceae; Galium; Galium angustifolium

File.Name: b707b_b707-021.jpg
Notes:
67612342 I guess this is a handsome Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a Zinnia flower in the garden today. These butterflies are not common in our mountains, though I find them now and then. They look a lot like the Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterflies, but they have hooked antennae like other skippers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2011)

Garden tip - the old fashioned single-flower Zinnias are great butterfly flowers, but the hybrid double-flower varieties are no good at all.

67612244 This is an interesting Beetle (order Coleoptera) that I found working the new summer flowers of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family growing by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2011)

It's interesting because it sure seems to have a slender proboscis that's visible below its head. Hemiptera have sucking mouthparts, but Coleoptera should have chewing mouthparts. I just take the photos - I'll leave it to someone else to explain them!

   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67446837

It's nest is now 3 or 4 inches long - a long tube made of webbing and insect parts. If you look close, you can see the spider perched on the outside of its nest. (San Marcos Pass, 7 July 2011)

The spider has been working a flowering plant of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by our mailbox. Now the yarrow is finishing up, so it will be interesting to see what it does next. There is buckwheat growing nearby that is starting to flower.

File.Name: b708a_b708-008.jpg
Notes:
67618995 If you were to drive over San Marcos Pass on Highway 154, the last thing you would think of are native orchids growing in the summer-dry woods - but here they are!

This is the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. It's a real orchid growing in our summer woods! (San Marcos Pass, 8 July 2010)

You can see that the pedicel - the stem of the flower - is rotated 180 degrees in the process known as resupination. These flowers also have a long nectar spur. They are beautiful and elegant up close like this, but from a distance they look more like asparagus stalks than anything exotic!

I mostly find them in sunny places on the north-facing side of our mountains. I guess they want shade as seedlings and sun to flower. They need a few years to flower, and only last for 1 or 2 years.

Please forgive my cut-and-paste comments tonight. My Live Oak Music Festival friend Jigger just sent a DVD video of our Saturday night campground jam. I've been seriously distracted tonight!

File.Name: b709b_b709-016.jpg
Notes:
67623885 Seeing this Beetle (order Coleoptera) was like seeing an old friend today!

I don't know what these beetles are really, but I have my own name for them - it's a "Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle". I always find about this time of year on the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. It's a good-sized beetle, about an inch (2.5 cm) long. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2011)

Last summer I found it on July 15. Two years ago I found it on June 24. Three years ago I found it on July 18. It sure looks like a pattern!

I tried to move this beetle into better light, but it took wing before I could get the photo. Oh well, there's always tomorrow.

File.Name: b709d_b709-005.jpg
Notes:
67623598 This is an odd photo - or rather, the photo of something odd.

It shows another tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera), but it's filling it's pollen baskets with fungus spores from a "Live Oak Witches' Broom Fungus" infection of a Powdery Mildew, very likely Cystotheca lanestris. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2011)

This powdery mildew seems to attack this same small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree every year. It doesn't spread, and it doesn't seem to harm the tree, except for the galled branches. I'll post a photo of the whole infection tomorrow.

But why is the bee interested? It's not a one-shot occurrence - I've been seeing the bees on the tree every morning for a while.

For more info about this Powdery Mildew, see "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" (page 82ff.) which is available as a PDF from:
   http://nature.berkeley.edu/comtf/pdf/psw_gtr197.pdf

Notes:
67628725 Natty Dread comes to the woodlands?

Here are new dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The catkins look wind-pollinated, but they also attract a buzzing swarm of bees, so there must be more to the story. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

File.Name: b710b_b710-025.jpg
Notes: 67628596 This honeybee-size Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is snuggling into an amazing flower of native Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family. It brings the flower down, but not nearly enough to dust pollen on the bee's back. I wonder what insects these flowers are designed for! (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

File.Name: b710c_b710-027.jpg
Notes: 67628540 Here is a handsome Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly perched on a stick in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

File.Name: b710d_b710-010.jpg
Notes:
67628455 These are "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind.

This is the tent of little web-spinner Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caterpillars on Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. You can see the tiny caterpillars on the outside of the tent at the bottom of my photo. They may kill a branch or two of the plants, but they do not do too much damage. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

The little caterpillars spin their creepy webs on the host plants. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter until they pupate into adults next year. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them.

Tents now = butterflies next year!

File.Name: b710e_b710-004.jpg
Notes:
67628301 Here are first beautiful new galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. They are growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This native shrub is especially prone to forming galls. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

I've been looking for them, and these are the first I've found this year. Last year, I found the first new galls on 15 July 2010. I guess they're right on schedule!

Most of our Gall Wasps have a strange dual life cycle. A parthenogenic generation emerges in the early spring that is all females. They produce eggs in spring galls which are bisexual. In May-June, the males and females emerge from their galls, mate, and produce the new generation of summer galls which is showing just now. These galls will pass the summer and overwinter to produce a new generation of parthenogenic females next spring. And the cycle repeats.

Seems complicated, but sometimes nature is like that!
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

67628130 This is a "Live Oak Witches' Broom Fungus" infection of a Powdery Mildew, very likely Cystotheca lanestris, on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2011)

It's an Ascomycete fungal infection. This powdery mildew seems to attack this same small tree every year. It doesn't spread, and it doesn't seem to harm the tree, except for the galled branches, which will ultimately thin and die.

For more info about this interesting Powdery Mildew, see "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" (page 82ff.) which is available as a PDF from:
   http://nature.berkeley.edu/comtf/pdf/psw_gtr197.pdf

The real mystery to me is why the wild bees are interested in the spores! See my photo yesterday, at:

Today was cold and gloomy with morning marine fog until the afternoon - and we're at 2500 feet above sea level! I'll be busy for a few days. Its a good chance to go into my archives.

File.Name: b711d_b709-014.jpg
Notes:
67634180 These are galled flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. They are galled by tiny aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) - you can see a few scattered on the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2011)

The flowers should look like this:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67269578
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67435400
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67463389

This is so interesting! The galled flowers are not beautiful and barely recognizable. The aphids take them over and change them. But it's interesting that the galled flowers are still flowering, while the un-galled flowers are all done for the year. They must be specialized aphids, but who can tell by looking at them!

I reckon this is a plant gall in the works. Give them a few thousand generations, and they might produce a real structure - maybe one that benefits both plant and insect!

File.Name: b711e_b709-006.jpg
Notes:
67633886 Maybe you can help me identify this weed?

I'm sure it's a Morning-Glory in the Convulvulaceae plant family. It's a little annual vine with twining stems and hastate leaves like many morning-glories. The flowers are tiny, just 3-4 mm long. They have a small capitate stigma. They seem to be growing about six inches a day! All this points to the family Ipomoea. (San Marcos Pass, 9 July 2011)

But Ipomoea is a huge genus with over 500 species, including familiar garden flowers like "Heavenly Blues" and "Pearly Gates", and important veggies like Jicama and Sweet Potatoes. That's not much help.

This plant keys to Littlebell (Ipomoea triloba) in my books, but I don't believe it. That plant has larger flowers that are 1-2 cm long.

If you can help me identify this weed, please do! Should I pull it at once before it invades the woods, or keep it as a curiosity?

File.Name: b711f_b710-016.jpg
Notes:
67633685 This is another photo of dreadlock-like catkins of a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree, shot against the sky. It's in the same Fagaceae plant family as true oaks (genus Quercus), but this one has erect catkins rather than droopy, and chestnut-like spreading scales on the acorn cup. (San Marcos Pass, 10 July 2011)

You can see the new acorns below the catkins in my photo. I have a better photo in the archives at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/37595067

Lester, I'm not surprised that you don't recognize this tree. It's quite uncommon on San Marcos Pass, though we happen to have several large trees by our house. It mostly grows further north, in Big Sur and on into the Redwoods. Here it's a remnant tree - a remnant of wetter times, like the Huckleberries, Thimbleberries, and Madrones that grow with it. Climate change is nothing new after the last ice age!

It was another gloomy day in the fog at home in the mountains today, so I went to the river to find some sun. It was breezy with "fog winds" but not too bad.

This robber fly looks a lot like the fly in my [Previous] photo, but its larger. Maybe they are male and female?

67646486 I may as well start calling these Wild Bees (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily Bees" since this is the only place I see them.

(Yahoo, Julie let me off the hook on going to LA today. I'd rather be taking photos!)

File.Name: b713b_b713-007.jpg
Notes:
67646334 This is a remarkable summer wildflower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense. These are the first flowers I've seen this year. They weren't there 3 days ago. It's our finest summer wildflower. Everything comes in 3's with this flower - 3 sepals, 3 petals, 3 early stamens and 3 late ones, 3 carpels, a 3-parted stigma.

It's remarkable looking - and it's also remarkable because it's a lily that picks the middle of our bone-dry summer drought to flower in dry places!

Lynn - these first flowers are in the same place they were last year.

File.Name: b713e_b713-003.jpg
Notes:
67645713 This interesting critter has the unlikely name "Dog Vomit Slime Mold" - really! If you don't believe me, you can find it at Wikipedia:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fuligo_septica

This is NOT a fungus - for one reason, it can move. This is the slime mold Fuligo septica in the fascinating Myxomycetes class. This photo shows the mobile plasmodium stage becoming the solid lime-encrusted spore-bearing aethalium stage. It looks and feels like a toasted marshmallow, and it's about the same size. (San Marcos Pass, 13 July 2011)

It's more of a "them" than an "it". They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in a substrate like this oak log. But sometimes they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form this visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" that can crawl along like a slug. It finally dries and forms a mushroom-like "aethalium" structure to release tiny spores that will disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy for these critters' prime mission - to carry mother nature's seed to a new decaying log in the woods.

File.Name: b715a_b714-036.jpg
Notes:
67657564 Uninvited guest...

I came back from my usual walk in the woods yesterday to discover this cute RAT in our bird feeder! I'm still not sure how it got there.

I'm pretty sure this is a native Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes). It is white underneath, large ears, bulbous dark eyes - it all checks out, though I can't see if the tail is hairy. At least it's not scaly! The only feature that gives me pause is that I got this photo in the daytime. Aren't wood rats mostly nocturnal? (San Marcos Pass, 14 July 2011)

We seem to have a whole family of these wood rats in the wood pile under the bird feeder. *Sigh* Rats in the woodpile are better than rats in the walls. (H.P. Lovecraft reference!) We'll have to do some clean up real soon.

We actually had rats in the walls a dozen years ago. I finally set up a bowl with dog food in the living room, and waited up until 3:00 am. Over a few days, I picked off a whole family of them one by one with a pellet gun. Wood rats belong in the woods. They build large houses out of sticks, like beaver "lodges" in the woods. They are also called "pack rats" because they like to steal shiny things to add to their nests. (At least that's the myth.)

In one of the classic Donald Duck comics (#52, 1957), Carl Barks tells the story of "The Lost Peg Leg Mine" - where pack rats are the key to the story!

File.Name: b717e_b716-001.jpg
Notes: 67666533 This is a structurally perfect Fly (order Diptera) resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. I believe it is a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family, though it hardly matters. I suspect there's a name for every hair on its body. Think of this amazing detail the next time you swat a fly! (San Marcos Pass, 17 July 2011)

File.Name: b717f_b717-015.jpg
Notes:
67666485 This is a new summer wildflower of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. I've also heard it called the "Late-flowered Mariposa Lily" which makes sense.

This one is just starting to open, with a bud behind it. It's so remarkable that they chose to flower now, when the ground is bone dry with our usual summer drought - and they are so beautiful! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 17 July 2011)
CommonNames: Weed's Mariposa Lily; Late-flowered Mariposa Lily
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: b718a_b718-013.jpg
Notes:
67673436 I still don't know what this interesting Beetle (order Coleoptera) is, but it sure is handsome. It looks like a little puppy dog!

I call it a "Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle" since I always find about this time of year on the Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plants in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our creek. It's a good-sized beetle, about an inch (2.5 cm) long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

This year I first found it on July 9. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67623885

Last summer I found it on July 15. Two years ago I found it on June 24. Three years ago I found it on July 18. It sure looks like a pattern!

I guess this is a female. I saw another beetle on the mugwort with darker colors and a slender abdomen which I guess is the male. I don't know anything about them.

File.Name: b718b_b718-021.jpg
Notes:
67673288 This is a tiny Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera about to enter a flower of a native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) flower in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

If you look close, you can see how pollination happens with these deep-throated flowers. Look at the pollen on the very tip of the fly's proboscis! There is also pollen on its front legs. I can see in my photos that it uses its front legs to guide its proboscis into the flower. It's a good trick to do it all that fast!

The Prickly-Phlox is still flowering. The late rains were very good for flowers. There are still Farewell-to-Spring (Clarkia) and Chinese Houses (Collinsia heterophylla) flowers in the woods. But it's not so good for summer insects. For example I still haven't seen Ambush Bugs in the Phymatidae family of the order Hemiptera in the woods.
CommonNames: Bee Fly; Prickly-Phlox; Prickly-Phlox; Farewell-to-Spring; Chinese Houses; Ambush Bugs
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Clarkia; Collinsia heterophylla; Phymatidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Clarkia; Collinsia heterophylla; Phymatidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: b718c_b718-027.jpg
Notes:
67673052 Here is a slightly tattered Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

The early buckeye butterflies don't seem much interested in flowers, but they become more so as summer moves on. Maybe they use up their stores of "baby fat" and have to work for a living?
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: b718d_b718-035.jpg
Notes:
67673006 Here is a moth-like Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on a grass blade in the canyon. It's a real butterfly, with knobby antennae and soccer-ball eyes - though I admit it is kind of dull. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

This is a multi-brooded butterfly that seems to be always out in the woods. The spring forms are a bit more colorful than the summer forms:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/49854510

File.Name: b718e_b718-030.jpg
Notes:
67672928 This is an especially beautiful Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a flower head of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in the canyon. It has brilliant colors. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

There are too many kinds of these skipper butterflies - I choose just to "skip her". After I get the photo of course!

File.Name: b718f_b718-007.jpg
Notes:
67672694 This interesting Moth (Lepidoptera) has "bat ear" antennae! It must be just emerging from it's nest under a leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The nest reminds me of very fine "excelsior" or "wood wool" - the very fine wood shavings once widely used for packing, but not so common now. It felt solid. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

Maybe it's a male Western Tussock Moth - family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin? The females are wingless. They have beautiful larvae.
CommonNames: Moth; California Bay; Western Tussock Moth
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta

File.Name: b719a_b719-020.jpg
Notes:
67680457 Sometimes the telling makes it true. I lamented yesterday that there were no Ambush Bugs in the woods, and today I found one!

This is a handsome Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) perched on a new flower cluster of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. These are small but mighty predators that can bring down prey much larger than they are. I'm glad they've arrived! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 July 2011)

This has been a busy evening. I was all set to make do with three photos tonight, and then Fotolog went down. Well, here's my 3rd photo - a day late.

File.Name: b719b_b719-006.jpg
Notes:
67679484 This is a new nest of Yellow Jackets (genus Vespula in the order Hymenoptera) that is still under construction. You can see the wasp in the lower-left bringing up a load of dirt. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 July 2011)

These are aggressive wasps with a painful sting. They are the wasps that can get onto your hamburger between the time to pick it up and take a bite! I was nervous about getting this close, but they took no interest in me. Autumn is the time when they get real touchy.

The wasps are excavating, but I'm not sure if it's a new hole or an abandoned gopher hole. They are building a large nest of open-faced paper combs. Skunks dig them up in the fall, and you can appreciate how large they are - a foot or more in diameter sometimes!

This nest happens to be a few feet from a well-used horse trail by the river. Horses and riders beware!

File.Name: b719c_b719-017.jpg
Notes:
67679393 This is a beautiful native Dunn's Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) in the Campanulaceae plant family. It's uncommon, but only because it's habitat is so uncommon - perennial creeks that flow in our dry summer. I found this flower today where Lewis Creek empties into the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 July 2011)

I have a recent photo of the setting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67639662

File.Name: b720a_b720-045.jpg
Notes:
67684764 Look close!

This is an impressive Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) clutching it's prey on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2011)

The prey seems to be a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. These bugs are fingernail-size, so that gives some scale to my photo!

The first wave of summer flowers is just about done - the Deerweed, Yerba Santa, Golden Yarrow, etc. - and the predators are coming out to pick off the pollinators.

It's a good time to take a break. We're off to Portland, Oregon next week, hopefully on Monday. That gives me a few more days to "document the woods"!
ScientificName: Asilidae; Diptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Asilidae; Diptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: b720b_b720-050.jpg
Notes: 67684572 This is a true bug in the Reduviidae family of the insect order Hemiptera - probably the Bee Assassin (Apiomerus californicus). It's the size of a quarter. It's an impressive predator, though this one is struggling to get a hold in non-native Smilograss (Piptatherum miliaceum). You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2011)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus californicus; Piptatherum miliaceum
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus californicus; Piptatherum miliaceum

File.Name: b720c_b720-040.jpg
Notes:
67684413 These are tiny Mealy Bug (Pseudococcidae) insects in the order Homoptera on ripe fruit of Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Those white tubes are egg cases, so I reckon those spiny critters are the females. (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2011)

Look at the left of my photo. Could the fly-like critter be a male? It stayed here a long time, but it doesn't look anything like my other male Mealy Bug photos. Maybe it's a predator?
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/63201379
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/22562649

File.Name: b720d_b720-064.jpg
Notes:
67684201 This small fly (order Diptera) has met it's end on the spines of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. Did it come in for a landing too fast? (San Marcos Pass, 20 July 2011)

I believe it is a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family, though it hardly matters. Tiny dark ants have already found the carcass.

These cacti have been fatal for other insects - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/44014396

File.Name: b720e_b720-055.jpg
Notes: 67683962 Here is another photo of a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working a native summer-flowering Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I guess they have their appetite back! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 20 July 2011)
CommonNames: Common Buckeye; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

Notes:
67683904 Nano, if you can figure out what my "Mid-summer Mugwort Beetle" (order Coleoptera) is, I'll be grateful. This is close to a dorsal shot, and it happens to be a very nice photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 July 2011)

File.Name: b721a_b721-051.jpg
Notes:
67689623 Here is a another photo of the beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly ovipositing on a stem of (I guess) non-native Weedy Cudweed (Gnaphalium luteo-album) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden today. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

See my [Previous] photo for more info:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67689573

I was sitting almost too close to the butterfly, I captured it full-frame. I didn't go into the woods today expecting butterflies, but here they are.

File.Name: b721b_b721-044.jpg
Notes:
67689573 This is a beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly ovipositing on a stem of (I guess) non-native Weedy Cudweed (Gnaphalium luteo-album) in the Asteraceae plant family. These butterflies seem to favor all of our furry Gnaphalium plants. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

Note that she is only resting on a platform of four legs. As a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family, she is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs held up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

Despite my photos, butterflies have been scarce this year. This is a sign that the best is yet to come!
ScientificName: Vanessa virginiensis; Gnaphalium luteo-album; Asteraceae; Gnaphalium; Nymphalidae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa virginiensis; Gnaphalium luteo-album; Asteraceae; Gnaphalium; Nymphalidae

File.Name: b721c_b721-002.jpg
Notes:
67689417 Here is a handsome Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing on native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

It's an intimate moment - it's also the one time these butterflies hold still! I watched it for a while, fluttering from plant to plant, leaving eggs behind seemingly at random. "Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

File.Name: b721d_b721-017.jpg
Notes:
67689331 This is an Duskywing (genus Erynnis) butterfly working (like so many of my recent butterfly photos) native summer-flowering Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family.

It doesn't have white wing margins, so it must be either the Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) or the Afranius Duskywing (Erynnis afranius). Sorry, I don't know how to tell them apart. I hope they do! (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

Notes: 67689119 This is a threadbare Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly on a new flower cluster of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. Or is it a Lupine Blue (P. lupinus) which despite it's name prefers buckwheats? I guess I don't really care. I call them all "Acmon Blues". (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

File.Name: b721f_b721-009.jpg
Notes:
67688991 Our Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly caterpillars have made it to the next stage in just a week! Compare my photo of the "bird turd" stage from July 14:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67652254

This caterpillar has been devouring its usual host plant - non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. It's a native caterpillar that prefers a non-native host plant. We always leave a few fennel plants for the butterflies, and we try to remember to cut the flowers before they set seed. You can see a few aphid husks on the plant. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

File.Name: b722a_b721-028.jpg
Notes:
67695393 Here is another uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the canyon yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

It's a real native orchid growing in our dry summer woods. You can see that the pedicel - the stem of the flower - is rotated 180 degrees in the process known as resupination. These flowers have a long nectar spur. They look strange and embryonic, not like any other wildflower.

Today was sunny, but cool and windy, not a great photo day. Just as well. I've been busy backing up my computer to get ready for our trip to Oregon in a few days.

File.Name: b722b_b722-005.jpg
Notes: 67695338 Here is a tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. It's working a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family, and you can see it piercing the leaf with it's proboscis. (Look for the bright highlight under its head - the leaf is bleeding!) (San Marcos Pass, 22 July 2011)
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Wild Blackberry

File.Name: b722c_b722-009.jpg
Notes:
67695173 This is not beautiful, but it's interesting!

This acorn has been galled by some critter that is causing the Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) acorn cup to produce a sweet honeydew exudate under enough pressure to push the galled acorn out of its cup to the ground! The exudate is sweet and sticky like honey or molasses. Sure, I've tasted it. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 July 2011)

This might be the work of the Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) in the order Hymenoptera - though my book lists that species of wasp on Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) rather than Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). Either way, it's a remarkable gall wasp that can coax honey from the bitter oaks!
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Callirhytis carmelensis; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Callirhytis carmelensis; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: b722d_b722-011.jpg
Notes:
67694997 These are interesting native flowers of Sacapellote (Acourtia microcephala). This is an unusual composite (family Asteraceae) plant with heads of two-lipped mint-like flowers that are unlike any other composite I know. They are growing in the chaparral by the Bradbury Dam overlook near Lake Cachuma. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 July 2011)

If you're the fan of my photos who sent me email a month or two ago about this plant, please send again! I inadvertently lost your email and I would like to reply!

File.Name: b722e_b719-036.jpg
Notes: 67694904 Here is a beautiful Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera of the insect order Odonata) that I captured near the river on Wednesday night. She looks translucent. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 July 2011)

File.Name: b722f_b721-047.jpg
Notes:
67694810 Here is one last photo of my close encounter with the beautiful American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly perched on a stem of non-native Weedy Cudweed (Gnaphalium luteo-album) in the Asteraceae plant family in the garden yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 21 July 2011)

"Sorry this spot is taken."

She's perched on an already occupied caterpillar nest. I'm not sure if she's trying to oviposit or just waiting for me to go away. The caterpillars make these fuzzy domes in the Gnaphalium plants. I've found them coming out a few times, for example:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67652120

As a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family, she is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs held up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

File.Name: b723a_b723-032.jpg
Notes:
67700004 Here is a beautiful Tailed Copper aka Arota Copper (Lycaena arota) butterfly resting on a twig of native Canyon Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family today. This butterfly is female. She has tails like a hairstreak. I found her near Arroyo Burro Pass in the mountains today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2011)

I drove to the top of La Cumbre Peak today - 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara - looking for hill-topping butterflies, but I didn't find them. It's funny to find so few butterflies after a very wet spring, but that's the way it goes. Sometimes a dry summer (after a wet one) is better.

I've been distracted tonight by a showing of "Cool Hand Luke" on TCM. What a great cast and movie!
CommonNames: Tailed Copper; Arota Copper; Canyon Gooseberry

File.Name: b723c_b719-001.jpg
Notes:
67699789 This tiny white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has its breakfast fly on the new summer flowers of a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 23 July 2011)

This little spider is smaller than a flower - only a few millimeters across.

File.Name: b723d_b723-041.jpg
Notes:
67699702 Here is a different scarlet flower in the mountains.

This is the Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella (terrible name!) (Keckiella ternata ssp. septentrionalis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing near the top of La Cumbre Peak, almost 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara. It's a classic "hummingbird flower" - though I didn't see any hummers visiting today. This straggly shrub has long flowering stalks with leaves of threes. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2011)

This is our third "Penstemon" (including Keckiella) in the woods. It's less common, but only because it prefers the high mountain tops in our mountains.

We also have Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia):
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67600029

and Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius):
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67555737
Locality: including Keckiella) in the woods.  It's less common
CommonNames: Northern Blue-stemmed Keckiella; Climbing Penstemon; Scarlet Bugler

File.Name: b723e_b723-031.jpg
Notes:
67699556 This is looking close at amazing summer flowers of native perennial Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They seem to be flowering for a second time this year. I did no color correction on this photo, though I did rotate it a bit. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2011)

See my [Previous] photo for more info:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67699517

File.Name: b723f_b723-027.jpg
Notes:
67699517 A bit of late rain does wonders. We had almost 2 inches of rain in early June. The woods are dry now, but that late rain made a difference!

These are remarkable summer flowers of native perennial Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are not common in our mountains, but I find them here and there. These are growing near Arroyo Burro Pass in the mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 July 2011)

The thing is, they already flowered in June, and here they are flowering again in July! You can see the old (June) plant stems to the left and right. These are new plant stems, lower than the old - only 2-3 feet tall - but full of flowers.

Curiously, the plants near Knapp's Castle aren't flowering twice - only these near Arroyo Burro Pass. Fortunately the weeds aren't flowering twice, though the non-native Yellow Star Thistle (Centaurea solstitialis) sure is doing well along the mountain roads.
Locality: June) plant stems to the left and right.  These are new plant stems

File.Name: b724a_b724-003.jpg
Notes:
67704934 This is a little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) collecting pollen on an amazing blue flower in our garden. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2011)

The bee seems to be collecting pollen on its butt - maybe it's in the family Megachilidae?

I guess the amazing flower is Mountain Bluet (Centaurea montana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a relative of bad weeds, though this one doesn't seem to spread.

We originally planned to leave for Oregon tomorrow, but it's not going to happen. Maybe Wednesday or Thursday. That means I have a few more days to post photos!
Locality: Centaurea montana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a relative of bad weeds
ScientificName: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Centaurea montana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Megachilidae; Centaurea montana; Asteraceae

Notes:
67704807 This is another perfect American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly on a native plant of Fragrant Everlasting (Gnaphalium canescens) in the Asteraceae plant family. I can tell from another photo that she is ovipositing (laying eggs). But why would she oviposit on an already occupied caterpillar nest on the plants? Is she recycling? (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2011)

As a Brushfoot butterfly in the Nymphalidae family, she is perched on just four legs, with her two front legs held up in front in T-rex fashion. Many of our most distinctive butterflies share this trait of perching on four legs with two in the air.

File.Name: b724c_b724-041.jpg
Notes:
67704671 This is a tender moment between two mature Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly caterpillars on a branch of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. These caterpillars may be just about to transform into pupae, and then butterflies - if everything goes well. (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2011)

I can almost imagine what it's saying:
"Don't worry little bro - you made it through the hard part. Now we'll transform into something wonderful beyond your dreams!"

Or maybe it's:
   "Now I lay me down to sleep...
   I pray the Lord my soul to keep,
   If I shall die before I wake,
   I pray the Lord my soul to take."

(I imagine insects saying this every night.)

File.Name: b724d_b724-014.jpg
Notes:
67704498 This is the early "bird turd" stage of a caterpillar of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. It met a bad end. You can see a neat little wasp or fly (?) nest below the caterpillar. Caterpillars seem like such sitting ducks for predators and parasites, I'm surprised any of them survive. (Sitting ducks too!) (San Marcos Pass, 24 July 2011)

File.Name: b726b_b718-004.jpg
Notes:
67715777 Here is another little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of a native Phacelia that is quite happy on the dry edge of our garden. I think it might be the Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 July 2011)

These Phacelia flowers will never be considered beautiful because of the way they keep their old brown flowers on the stalks. But look closer. The older flower (up front) has stamens and pistil up front - to distribute pollen. In the newer flower that the bee is on, the stamens have pulled back, giving the pistils a chance to receive pollen. In the old brown flowers, both stamens and pistils are withdrawn. Many native flowers do a similar dance.
Locality: up front) has stamens and pistil up front - to distribute pollen.  In the newer flower that the bee is on

I apologize, I haven't been back to this place since last week. I wonder what the wasps are up to?

File.Name: b726f_b724-027.jpg
Notes:

These orchids are interesting. They have rather large leaves in the spring, which shrivel up completely by the time they flower. They only seem to last a year or two, but maybe new ones will come up nearby. It takes the new ones several years before they flower. I consider myself lucky every time I find one.

We got back from Oregon last night. I got these photos at our cabin, but this trip was mostly to help our kids move into their new house in Portland. Mission accomplished!

Julie and I have the notion to live in the Oregon cabin for a full year, starting next Spring. We'll see what happens...

This plant is very common in Oregon, but it's actually non-native. It forms the familiar brambles along the roads with delicious blackberries. When you get deeper in the woods, it disappears in favor of native blackberries like Rubus ursinus that are not so productive but more delicious when you can find them.

Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) and Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) are more native plants that are common in Oregon but scare in our mountains. I guess 1,000 miles north makes a difference!

Why a mushroom when today's photos are all critters? Well, there is an ant in the upper-right. And mushrooms really are more animal than plant - right?

67840801 The Slug Ouroboros! Well, the ancient myth has the "Worm Ouroboros" biting its own tail and enclosing the world. It's a yin-yang thing. Close enough.

I guess this "worm" is the same hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus or kin) terrestrial mollusk that we find at home in the rainy season, though these Oregon slugs sure looks different - they are mottled green, rather than bright orange. I think it's about to do something unmentionable that rabbits also do. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 12 August 2011)

Try a Google Image search to appreciate the color variation of these slugs:

I'm posting late, because we hosted a party last night for Dunn Middle School teachers.
