File.Name: b822a_b731-012.jpg
Notes:
67850689 Here are new flowers of a Western Coralroot Orchid (Corallorhiza mertensiana) in the Orchidaceae plant family. This orchid is saprophytic, without chlorophyll. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

I believe all orchids are mycotrophic because the orchid seeds are dust-like and carry no food supply for the emerging plant. They need a connection with a fungus to develop. But this orchid needs an additional myco-heterotrophic fungal connection to a tree because it has no chlorophyll of its own.

See my first photo today of Indian Pipes for more info and an interesting link:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67849918

I reckon I need one more day for my Oregon photos. Of course I've been taking new photo every day I reckon I need one more day for my Oregon photos. Of course I've been taking new photos every day since I got home!

File.Name: b822b_b730-004.jpg
Notes:
67850493 I believe this is a native Piperia transvera orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods near our water tank. The way the flower spurs are oriented horizontally is distinctive. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2011)

If you compare the Piperia orchid in my [Previous] photo, they are clearly different.

I took all these orchid photos with natural light, no flash. The trick is to catch the flower in a sunbeam with dark shadow behind.

File.Name: b822c_b814-040.jpg
Notes:
67850422 These are pretty summer flowers of a Piperia orchid in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods, but which kind? I guess this is Piperia elegens ssp. elegens. This subspecies (the species) doesn't grow in California. (Along the road to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2011)

If you compare the Piperia transversa in my [Next photo], you can see several structural differences: this orchid is greenish, not white; it has leafy bracts on the stem, but the next one doesn't; the spurs of the flowers run down the stem, rather than across; the spurs are longer; this one is growing above 1500 ft. elevation, and the next on is near 1000 ft. They are definitely different species.
Locality: Along the road to Twin Lakes
ScientificName: Piperia; Orchidaceae; Piperia elegens; elegens; Piperia transversa
associatedTaxa: Piperia; Orchidaceae; Piperia elegens; elegens; Piperia transversa

File.Name: b822d_b731-078.jpg
Notes: 67850339 These are beautiful new flowers of a White Bog Orchid (Platanthera dilatata var. leucostachys) in the Orchidaceae plant family. The white flower stalks grow several feet tall. This one is growing in a soggy meadow in the Oregon woods. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

File.Name: b822e_b811-003.jpg
Notes:
67850101 Here are new flowers of Rattlesnake Orchid (Goodyera oblongifolia) in the Orchidaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods. They are the most common of the mid-summer orchids near our cabin by far, appearing after most other wildflowers. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 11 August 2011)

Most years when we make our summer trip to Oregon, I have to hunt for orchids and Indian Pipes. This year they are just beginning to flower and are common. Wildflowers and butterflies are late in Oregon as they are at home. Despite "global warming", this summer has been relatively cool and wet.

File.Name: b822f_b731-006.jpg
Notes:

This oddball was once placed in its own Monotropaceae family, but now it's included within the Ericaceae plant family. They are white and do not contain chlorophyll like most plants. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

These flowers seem to be completely parasitic on forest trees. Or more likely they are myco-heterotrophs which are parasitic on the mycorrhizal fungi that are parasitic on trees. They have a fungal connection with tree roots in a three-way relationship mediated by an mycorrhizal connection between flower-fungus-tree. We only see them at all because they have to flower - like all other plants.

Maybe the whole forest is connected by fungi? See the interesting speculations by Paul Stamets:
   http://www.ted.com/talks/paul_stamets_on_6_ways_mushrooms_can_save_the_world.html

File.Name: b823a_b731-099.jpg
Notes:
67855030 This is a remarkable new flower of native "Pussy Ears" (Calochortus tolmiei) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's a beautiful Mariposa Lily in the Oregon woods at about 5,000 feet elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

This is another native plant that I've never seen before, though we've been hiking to Twin Lakes in August for many years. Like at home, Oregon plants are late this year.

Most of my photos today are from the short trail to Twin Lakes. It's one of our favorite trails in Oregon that runs through both wet and dry meadows on it's way to the lakes.

I might need one more day to finish off my Oregon photos and get back to photos from San Marcos Pass.

File.Name: b823b_b731-068.jpg
Notes:
67854907 These are spectacular new flowers of native Scarlet Gilia aka Skyrocket (Ipomopsis aggregata) in the Oregon mountains at about 5,000 feet elevation. So pretty! (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

This is another native plant in the Oregon woods that I haven't see for many years, though we've been hiking to Twin Lakes in August for many years. Like at home, Oregon plants are late this year.

File.Name: b823c_b731-055.jpg
Notes:
67854792 This is a beautiful flower of native Western Columbine (Aquilegia formosa) in the Ranunculaceae) family. It's almost too-pretty to be a native. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

It's curious that the Ranunculaceae are usually considered a "primitive" plant family. These flowers look anything but!

File.Name: b823d_b731-033.jpg
Notes:
67854717 These are new flowers of native Bear-Grass (Xerophyllum tenex) in the Liliaceae plant family. To me from central California, they look like little 2-3 foot tall yucca plants. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

Like my [Previous] photo, these are flowers I've never seen before, though we've been hiking to Twin Lakes in August for many years. Like at home, Oregon plants are late this year. It still feels like spring in the 5,000+ foot high Oregon meadows!

Hah - there's a bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working the flowers in the upper-left. Did you spot it?

File.Name: b823e_b731-075.jpg
Notes:
67854542 I guess this is the Great Camas (Camassia leichtlinii), but cp. C. quamash. It's in the Liliaceae plant family. There were lots of these growing in a wet meadow near Twin Lakes. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

The bulbs were an important food for Native Americans. Death Camas (Zigadenus) looks quite different, but the bulbs are said to look similar. They are poisonous. Know what you're eating!

It's interesting that I've never seen these before, though we've been hiking to Twin Lakes in August for many years. Like at home, Oregon plants are late this year.
ScientificName: Camassia leichtlinii; C. quamash; Liliaceae; Zigadenus
associatedTaxa: Camassia leichtlinii; C. quamash; Liliaceae; Zigadenus

File.Name: b823f_b814-056.jpg
Notes:
67854350 This is a conifer with a berries rather than cones!

It's a fruit of a Pacific Yew (Taxus brevifolia) tree in the Oregon woods. These small conifer trees are never common, though they are scattered here and there, mostly along creeks or rivers - though we have one near our cabin which is about a mile from the river. (Near Umpqua Hot Springs, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 14 August 2011)

The fruit are said to be poisonous to humans and horses. The strong wood is prized for bows. The bark is the source of Taxol which has some use treating breast cancer. Fortunately the drug is now synthesized as Paclitaxel and the trees are safe.

(The hot springs were way too crowded on a Sunday, but we found a beautiful camp nearby along the river to hang out for a while. That's where I found the yew with berries.)
Locality: The hot springs were way too crowded on a Sunday

File.Name: b824a_b813-008.jpg
Notes:
67859804 These are ripe seeds in an open seed pod of some kind of Wild Iris (genus Iris, family Iridaceae) plant family growing in the Oregon woods. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 13 August 2011)

That's enough of my Oregon photos, though I have lots more. I've been taking lots of new photos at home since we got home a week ago.

File.Name: b824b_b731-060.jpg
Notes: 67859684 This is some kind of Wallflower (Erysimum) mustard in the Brassicaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods above 5,000 feet elevation. It's a native, not a weed. Like all our weedy mustards, it has 6 stamens - 2 short and 4 long - but it sure looks different from the weeds! (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

File.Name: b824c_b731-095.jpg
Notes:
67859461 These are impressive large native flowers of Pacific Rhododendron (Rhododendron macrophyllum Ericaceae ) in the Ericaceae plant family growing in the Oregon woods at about 5,000 ft. elevation. (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

This shrub is said to flower in early spring. I've never seen them before in flower, though we've been hiking to Twin Lakes in August for many years. Like at home, Oregon plants are flowering late this year.

I was losing my light as the sun got low. I didn't have much choice with flowers before I lost the sun completely!

File.Name: b824d_b731-105.jpg
Notes: 67859322 Here are handsome native succulents growing on the edge of a cliff in Oregon. I'm sure it is genus Sedum in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family, but I'm not sure what species. I didn't realize this group was so complicated! (Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 31 July 2011)

File.Name: b824e_b730-033.jpg
Notes:
67859213 Here is a strange rust fungus(?) growing up the stems of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Oregon woods. It's like the huckleberries have pulled up their socks. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 July 2011)

We also have this native shrub at home in the Santa Barbara mountains. It rarely sets fruit at home, and I notice that the Oregon plants rarely set fruit also.

File.Name: b824f_b813-003.jpg
Notes:
67859134 These are strange tumor-like galls that I found on Himalayan Blackberry (Rubus discolor) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's growing along a dusty dirt road in the Oregon woods. I don't think I've ever noticed the galls before. I reckon it's NOT an insect gall. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 13 August 2011)

Himalayan Blackberry is not a native plant in Oregon, but it's what everyone there means by "Wild Blackberries" - and they are delicious! It forms great brambles along roads and low spots.

File.Name: b825a_b817-006.jpg
Notes:
67864587 Here is a large Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly catching some morning light from a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. So beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2011)

I'm catching up with new photos from home after our Oregon trip. Lots of butterflies - in fact they've been the most noticeable insects after a summer with few insects at all. It looks easy in my flog, but I've been out grubbing for photos almost every day!

File.Name: b825b_b817-044.jpg
Notes:
67864494 This is a newly emerged Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly on a stick in the woods. The cocoon is just a few inches away, and its host plant of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family is a few feet away. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2011)

These butterflies seem so vulnerable to parasitic wasps in the chrysalis stage, it's a wonder any survive. I'll post another photo of a failed chrysalis tomorrow.

File.Name: b825c_b818-004+7.jpg
Notes:
67864337 I happened to capture both of our white-fringed "Spread-wing Skippers" (subfamily Pyrginae) with white trim on the trailing edge of the hindwing - on the same day, in the same pose. Both are "puddling" - sucking moisture from our gravel driveway. It made sense to put the photos together:

* The top photo shows the Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly. It is charcoal black, and not very common in our mountains.

* The bottom photo shows the Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly, which has more brown in its wings. It is common.

I love these sad names, both Latin and common! (San Marcos Pass, 18 August 2011)
CommonNames: Spread-wing Skippers; Funereal Duskywing; Erynnis funeralis; Mournful Duskywing

File.Name: b825d_b819-022.jpg
Notes:
67864197 This is a tattered Great Basin Wood-Nymph (Cercyonis sthenele) butterfly perched on dead grass along my trail. Its eyespots are hardly recognizable. This butterfly has a floppy flight like a moth. It rarely lands for long and doesn't visit flowers. They are hard to capture in a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2011)

I have a recent photo of its cousin C. pegala from Oregon:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67831780

File.Name: b825e_b820-017.jpg
Notes: 67864096 Here is a pretty Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly perched on a new flower of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon. For some reason, I rarely find insects on these flowers, but here they are. I've been seeing these butterflies in the woods, but I haven't got a photo since last March. (San Marcos Pass, 20 August 2011)

File.Name: b825f_b823-011.jpg
Notes:
67863983 This is a pretty female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working a new late season composite flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has a soft-looking pussy cat face. (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2011)

I'm catching up with new photos from home after our Oregon trip. Lots of butterflies - in fact they've been the most noticeable insects after a summer with few insects at all. It looks easy in my flog, but I've been out grubbing for photos almost every day!

File.Name: b826a_b825-004.jpg
Notes:
67871199 Here is a handsome Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly ovipositing on the non-native Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) in the Solanaceae plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2011)

It seems an odd choice for a host plant. One of my butterfly books says categorically that they use legumes, but another book says they use a "wide variety of plants, especially legumes." The moral - have several books, and trust what you see!

I think you can see one of its tiny eggs below the tip of its abdomen on the leaf margin in the lower-left of the photo!

I'm posting late today because we had another small dinner gathering at our house last night.

File.Name: b826b_b819-031.jpg
Notes: 67871147 Here is a beautiful Blue butterfly in the subfamily Polyommatinae in the Lepidoptera perched with wings open on a plant stem in a clump of flowering California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub. I can't be sure what kind of butterfly it is without seeing the bottom wings, but it's probably an Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) or maybe a Lupine Blue (P. lupinus). What rich colors it has! (San Marcos Pass, 19 August 2011)
CommonNames: Blue; California Buckwheat; Acmon Blue; Lupine Blue
ScientificName: Polyommatinae; Lepidoptera; Eriogonum fasciculatum; Plebejus acmon; P. lupinus

File.Name: b826c_b822-007.jpg
Notes:
67871063 This is a pretty Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly perched on a leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family down by the creek in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2011)

I haven't got a photo of these butterflies for a few years. We used to have a small oak tree in the canyon which was a perfect perch for them, but an over-zealous fire crew cut it down during a wildfire a few years ago. Keep those fire crews busy or they do some damage!

These butterflies usually stay near the willows and cottonwoods along the creek. The very similar California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterflies are more common in the woods. They have a dark border around the orange wing-tips. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67485626

File.Name: b827a_b827-013.jpg
Notes:
67873302 This is a perfect American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterfly on a native plant of summer-flowering Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I didn't use flash for this photo - it's a butterfly in the sun with deep shade behind. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2011)

We had a few drops of real rain last night from tropical weather. Today has been very hot. I measured 98 ∞F (37 ∞C) on the porch this afternoon. Just a week ago I was wishing it would warm up!
CommonNames: American Lady; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella

File.Name: b827b_b825-006.jpg
Notes:
67873284 This is a beautiful lady Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterfly on a non-native Tomatillo (Physalis ixocarpa) in the Solanaceae plant family in the garden. The fruit of this plant are the essential ingredient in chile verde and much of my favorite food! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2011)

She's been busy ovipositing on this plant. I've seen her for three mornings now doing it. See my photo yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67871199

File.Name: b827c_b826-018.jpg
Notes: 67873242 Here is a male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working a non-native flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. You can see a bit of its hidden "dog face" in the back-lit wings - but he only really shows it in the air. I'm still trying to get that perfect photo! (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2011)

File.Name: b827d_b827-023.jpg
Notes: 67873209 This is a late-season Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working the last of the native summer-flowering Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Or is it a Western Tiger Swallowtail (P. rutulus) butterfly? As summer goes on, the swallowtails get harder to tell apart. It definitely has some color on its wings. Either way, it's pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2011)
CommonNames: Pale Swallowtail; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Western Tiger Swallowtail
ScientificName: Papilio eurymedon; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; P. rutulus

File.Name: b827e_b822-024.jpg
Notes: 67873150 I've been seeing this different Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly in the garden this week. Here it is perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. Maybe it's an Umber Skipper (Poanes melane)? It's said to be our largest Grass-Skipper, which sound right. (San Marcos Pass, 22 August 2011)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: b827f_b826-004.jpg
Notes:
67873096 This is a humble Cabbage White (Pieris rapae) working non-native flowers of Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. It's a weedy butterfly working a weedy flower. Both butterfly and flower are introduced species. This is nobody's favorite butterfly, but they have their charms, and here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2011)

This was the only butterfly that I saw in urban Portland, Oregon.

File.Name: b828a_b823-003.jpg
Notes:
67878424 Here is a handsome white-faced Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new composite flowers of Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. Note the strange "double antennae". (San Marcos Pass, 23 August 2011)

I believe this critter might be genus Copestylum in the subfamily Eristalinae. See the BugGuide page at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/9909

I've been showing enough butterflies for a while, so today's photos are all real flies!

File.Name: b828b_b828-026.jpg
Notes: 67878322 Here is a beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a new flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family today. You can see the spurious vein in its wings if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2011)

File.Name: b828c_b827-047.jpg
Notes: 67878225 I'm pretty sure this is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera with a long proboscis. It's working new flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2011)

File.Name: b828d_b827-038.jpg
Notes:
67878169 Judging from the figured wings and long ovipositor, I guess this pretty fly is a female Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera). She's perched on immature flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. I wonder if she'll make galls? (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2011)

This flower is another one of our yellow-flowered late-season composite bloomers that keep the insects alive at the end of our hot, dry summer.

File.Name: b828e_b825-007.jpg
Notes:
67878018 This is a small Long-legged Fly (family Dolichopodidae in the order Diptera) with its tiny breakfast meal. They are little predators. (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2011)

Note that its head is scooped out between the eyes. This is also so with dragonflies and damselflies and robber flies. I guess it helps them with depth perception to catch prey on the wing.

File.Name: b828f_b824-020.jpg
Notes:
67877932 My first guess (see the update below) is that this is a large (bee-sized) "Horse Fly" in the family Tabanidae of the order Diptera. But I'm sure it's not a real Horse Fly (Tabanus punctifer) - they have never shown any interest in my flesh. Female Horse Flies have a nasty bite! Maybe this is a male? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 23 August 2011)

This one is working flowers of Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family - a shrub that only grows on the highest peaks of our mountains.

Update 29 August 2011 - D'oh, this is NOT a horsefly (Tabanus). The antennae are wrong for one thing. I now believe it's a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae. No wonder it never tried to bite me! I've been wrong about this fly before.

See the images at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/9910/bgimage
Locality:
Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family - a shrub that only grows on the highest peaks of our mountains.

Update 29 August 2011 - D'oh
CommonNames: Horse Fly; Horse Fly; Golden Fleece; Mexican Cactus Fly
ScientificName: Tabanidae; Diptera; Tabanus punctifer; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Tabanus; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae
associatedTaxa: Tabanidae; Diptera; Tabanus punctifer; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Tabanus; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae

File.Name: b829a_b829-023.jpg
Notes:
67883165 Here is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) getting a drink from the river today. She kind of skimmed the water, with the characteristic "bent-back" posture she usually has when working flowers. Look - you can see her beak in the water! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2011)

I went to the river today for photos and a swim. It's not much of a river at this time of year and with two dams upstream. I can step across it. But it is still flowing in this one place, and the hummers were drinking here. I sat quietly for a while, and they came back...

Hah - today was the first day of "teacher stuff" before school starts next week. I'm officially retired now, so I don't care!

File.Name: b829b_b829-036.jpg
Notes:
67883061 This is a beautiful large Dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) perched on a Cattail (family Typhaceae) leaf by the river. Maybe it's a Flame Skimmer (Libellula saturata) or kin? I saw it on its favorite perch and didn't think I could sneak up on it, but it came right back to pose for my photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2011)

Look at the way its wings are buttressed to give them strength. That is great engineering.

File.Name: b829c_b829-040.jpg
Notes:
67882967 I found this handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) munching a leaf of native Cattail (family Typhaceae) by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2011)

The leaf was overhanging the river. The hopper would make great trout food if it slipped!

File.Name: b829d_b829-015.jpg
Notes:
67882830 I guess this pretty critter is a Harlequin Bug (Murgantia histrionica) in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera. My books say they prefer Mustard (Brassicaceae) plants, but this one is working a composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 August 2011)

The Lepidospartum squamatum is just starting to flower on the river wash! I usually get a huge number of photos on this shrub. But so far there are no Metalmark (family Riodinidae) butterflies. All insects are late this year.
ScientificName: Murgantia histrionica; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Brassicaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Metalmark; Riodinidae
associatedTaxa: Murgantia histrionica; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Brassicaceae; Lepidospartum squamatum; Lepidospartum squamatum; Metalmark; Riodinidae

File.Name: b829e_b829-010.jpg
Notes:
67882662 This is a large black Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera. This one is working some kind of succulent plant that is just starting to flower in the garden. To get some detail on the bee like those amazing eyes, I had to overexpose by one full F-stop. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2011)

They are slowly destroying the fascia boards on our roof with their nests, but I leave them be.

File.Name: b829f_b829-007.jpg
Notes:
67882537 Is "beautiful fly" an oxymoron? Judge from this photo...

I'm pretty sure this beautiful fly is a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae, order Diptera) despite the absence of bristles. Studying the pictures at bugguide.net, I'm pretty sure this critter is genus Gymnosoma. It's perched (posing for me!) on a leaf of non-native Spearmint (Menthe spicata) in the garden. It was working the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2011)

The adult flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: b830a_b830-042.jpg
Notes:
67887877 I found this beautiful Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) on our well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. There were four of them - all fighting and flirting, though the American Lady (Vanessa virginiensis) butterflies seemed to be pushing them around! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)

You would think Monarchs would be common here, since thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away... but they are actually scarce. We don't see them until autumn, which I guess is now. The butterflies were acting "frisky" - I hope they lay eggs on one of the three kinds of milkweed in the garden!

We had a mixed flock of Warblers (birds) come through the garden this morning, so I put my "big glass" on my camera - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens. I couldn't capture the warblers, but I did get the rest of my photos today.
Locality: birds) come through the garden this morning
CommonNames: Monarch Butterfly; Butterfly Bush; American Lady
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Buddleia davidii; Vanessa virginiensis; Warblers
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Buddleia davidii; Vanessa virginiensis; Warblers

File.Name: b830b_b828-005.jpg
Notes:
67887763 There's a butterfly mosh pit in the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. I saw Western and Pale Swallowtails, Monarchs, American Ladies, Buckeyes, and lots of Skippers!

I also saw this beautiful Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)

I wonder if it's the same butterfly that I found newly emerged last week? See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67864494

File.Name: b830c_b830-039.jpg
Notes:
67887655 This is a large bee-sized black fly on Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. There were lots of them. I used to think they were gentle "Horse Flies" in the family Tabanidae, but now I know better.

I now believe this is a Mexican Cactus Fly (Copestylum mexicanum) in the family Syrphidae. It's not a typical Syrphid Flower Fly by any means, but if you know about fly wing venation, you can spot the "spurious vein" in its wings. We do have lots of cactus plants in the garden around our house. (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)
CommonNames: Butterfly Bush; Horse Flies; Mexican Cactus Fly; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Buddleia davidii; Tabanidae; Copestylum mexicanum; Syrphidae

File.Name: b830d_b830-008.jpg
Notes:
67887480 This is an Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) caught in the air working a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)

It almost looks like an Allen's-Rufous Hummer, but that's just the light reflected from the flower. Sure - the flower is over-exposed. Who cares?

File.Name: b830e_b830-007.jpg
Notes:
67887404 Here is a perfect Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) caught in the air above a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)

This is not a classic "hummingbird flower", but she was working it anyway! See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: b830f_b830-014.jpg
Notes:
67887329 This is another Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working flowers of a furry-leaved succulent in the garden this morning. (I don't know what it is.) Look - it has the same "bent-back" posture as the hummer in my [Previous] photo. I love the curve of its wings! (San Marcos Pass, 30 August 2011)

We had a small mixed-flock of Warbler birds come through the garden this morning! There were Black-throated Gray Warblers (Dendroica nigrescens)(?), Wilson's Warblers (Wilsonia pusilla), and another that I didn't recognize. They were gathering around our bathtub-water garden. I guess the autumn migration has begun.

I put my "big glass" on my camera - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens. I couldn't capture the warblers, but I did get these nice hummers! I'm sure my big lens scares the birds. I need a screen.
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Warbler; Black-throated Gray Warblers; Wilson's Warblers
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Dendroica nigrescens; Wilsonia pusilla
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Dendroica nigrescens; Wilsonia pusilla

File.Name: b831a_b824-027.jpg
Notes:
67892574 I'm sure this is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus or kin) because of the spines on the back of its thorax. It's captured a tiny fly (order Diptera) for lunch. (San Marcos Pass, 24 August 2011)

Today's photos are all predators. It's that time of year when the predators appear to pick of the other insects before winter kills them all.

It was sunny enough, but cool and breezy today - a nice change from the heat we've been having. It wasn't a good day for photos, just as well as I had work to do that I've been putting off.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; fly

File.Name: b831b_b826-030.jpg
Notes:
67892455 Here is a perfect Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera perched on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. I'm sure that's its shed skin underneath the leaf. It's been perched on the same plant ever since I got this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2011)

Look close - you can make out its thin proboscis under its head.
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Wood Mint
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Stachys bullata; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Stachys bullata; Labiatae

File.Name: b831c_b824-010.jpg
Notes:
67892398 Here are a mating pair of Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera). Their nuptial bed is made of flowers of Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family - a yellow-flowered composite shrub that only grows on the highest peaks of our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 24 August 2011)

It odd that I've found very few of these predator insects on flowers at home - even on the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) where they are perfectly camouflaged. It's been a strange year for local insects.
CommonNames: Ambush Bugs; Golden Fleece; California Buckwheat
ScientificName: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum
associatedTaxa: Phymatidae; Hemiptera; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Eriogonum fasciculatum

File.Name: b831d_b825-018.jpg
Notes:
67892267 This is the chrysalis of an Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly caterpillar - but it's met a bad end. You can see several holes that parasitic wasps have made into the chrysalis, and the outline of a wasp at the bottom. This caterpillar will never become a butterfly - but many wasps will be born! (San Marcos Pass, 25 August 2011)

I didn't notice the tiny wasp at the bottom when I got the photo. The chrysalis is on a plant of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. We control the plants, but we always leave a few for the butterflies. I posted a photo of the butterfly it would have been yesterday:


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67887763
Locality: Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. We control the plants

File.Name: b831e_b817-030.jpg
Notes:
67892183 Here is a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) with a large black Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera. It's perched on a spent flower head of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) shrub in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 17 August 2011)

I know the feeling - *blah*. The bee should have little "X"s through its eyes.
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Carpenter Bee; Woolly Blue Curls
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Trichostema lanatum

File.Name: b831f_b826-019.jpg
Notes: 67892119 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a petal of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. If you look very close, you can see that it has its very-tiny breakfast prey. (San Marcos Pass, 26 August 2011)

File.Name: b901a_b901-003.jpg
Notes:
67897771 I'm pretty sure this is a bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in the garden today. It's working flowers of English Lavender (Lavendula angustifolia) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The fly was actually hanging upside down. I flipped the photo so it would look more "natural". That "seventh leg" hanging down between its two front legs is its impressive proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2011)

Tonight was the back-to-school Potluck at my good ol' Dunn Middle School - where I taught for 28 years before officially retiring last June. I went to the potluck to visit good friends - and I am teaching a skimboard class this trimester! I got home late. Three photos will do tonight.

File.Name: b901b_b901-015.jpg
Notes: 67897736 I'm pretty sure this is a beautiful little humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera with a long proboscis and graceful legs. This one is working new late-summer flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2011)

File.Name: b901c_b901-022.jpg
Notes: 67897689 I call this a "Steel Bridge Fly". It's a large Bee Fly - family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera - that I always seem to find at this time of year perched on the steel guard rails of the little bridge across our creek. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2011)

File.Name: b902a_b902-014.jpg
Notes: 67902405 Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a stamen of (I think) "Bluebeard" (Caryopteris x clandonensis) in the Lamiaceae (or by some accounts Verbenaceae) plant family in the garden this morning. Look close - you can see its proboscis touching the stamen to pick up pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 2 September 2011)
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae; Verbenaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae; Verbenaceae

File.Name: b902b_b827-008.jpg
Notes:
67902347 This is the caterpillar of a Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterfly. I found it looking pensive on the end of a stem of its food plant, non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. It must be ready to become a chrysalis. I came back a day later, and it was gone from view - hidden away to work some magic. (San Marcos Pass, 27 August 2011)

I imagine its saying the prayer all insects must say as they get ready for their big transformation:


   "Now I lay me down to sleep...
   I pray the Lord my soul to keep,
   and if I die before I wake,
   I pray the Lord my soul to take."

Parasitic wasps make this a real prayer! See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67892267

File.Name: b902c_b828-029.jpg
Notes:
67902220 These are frisky Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterflies in the woods. I guess that's the female in the lower-right, working a nearly-spent flower head of native summer-flowering Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2011)

Look how the lady is still feeding but wagging her tail! (What is it about sex and food?) The male in the air is interested. He kept diving in and out, but finally flew away.

The Monardella hypoleuca has been flowering in our canyon since June! It's never had such a run, but I still haven't seen any Fritillary (Heliconiinae) butterflies which favor them.
Locality: What is it about sex and food?)  The male in the air is interested.  He kept diving in and out
CommonNames: Grass-Skipper; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella; Fritillary
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Heliconiinae
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca; Heliconiinae

File.Name: b902d_b901-012.jpg
Notes: 67902087 Here is a handsome Harford's Sulphur (Colias harfordii aka C. alexandra harfordii) butterfly perched on a leaf in the woods. You can recognize the butterfly by the dark margin on its upper wings that is barely visible in this photo. They are pretty common, but camera-shy. I don't get many photos of them. (San Marcos Pass, 1 September 2011)

File.Name: b902e_b829-013.jpg
Notes:
67901989 This is a tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera perched on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. You can see her proboscis folded under her head. (San Marcos Pass, 29 August 2011)

What is that little structure that hangs down her forehead from between her antennae?
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b902f_b828-012.jpg
Notes: 67901917 This is a momma Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her egg sack from a potential predator - me! She is perched on an unusual native plant of Rough-leaved Aster (Aster radulinus) in the Asteraceae plant family. This uncommon plant is growing - and flowering! - on bone dry soil in a place that was cleared for a firebreak a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 28 August 2011)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster radulinus; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster radulinus; Asteraceae

File.Name: b903a_b902-014.jpg
Notes:
67906241 Here is a beautiful emerald green nymph of a Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's not quite a winged adult yet, but its close. It was perched on a native shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family that is just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

I'm pretty sure of the ID because this "teen" Katydid still has some colors from its "baby" stage. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67389507

I think it might grow up to be a Fork-tailed Bush Katydid (Scudderia furcata) or kin. I have an old photo of the adult at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/15318240

(Note to self - get new katydid photos!)
CommonNames: Bush Katydid; Coyote Bush; Fork-tailed Bush Katydid
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Scudderia furcata
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Scudderia furcata

File.Name: b903b_b826-002.jpg
Notes:
67906030 I believe this is the brown form of the Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera. This one is not quite an adult because of those short wings. I reckon it has one more molt to go before it can join the nighttime chorus jam! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

Our common Oecanthus crickets are "thermometer crickets" - just count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 to get degrees Fahrenheit! I have no idea if that really works, but they do chirp faster on warm nights.

File.Name: b903c_b826-026.jpg
Notes:
67905923 This is a rather large Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera). It was perched on a sandstone rock in the garden this morning, in pretty deep shade. I waited for the sun to get to it, but it disappeared by then. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

I believe our spring cicadas only make a clicking noise, but these late-summer cicadas add to the nighttime chorus of crickets that we're hearing every night. I'm at a disadvantage with the cicadas though. Their chorus is almost the same pitch as my tinnitus - that constant ringing in my ears that comes from too many years of too-loud music. Be warned! (On the other hand, a constant chorus of cicadas isn't bad!)

File.Name: b903d_b903-024.jpg
Notes:
67905818 The native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family is just starting to flower, and this tiny humpbacked Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera was there to celebrate the opening with its long proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

Coyote Bush is a humble shrub that many people consider a weed, but its late-season flowers are important for the insects. The shrubs are "dioecious" with male and female flowers on different plants. This plant is male - staminate, with stamens. I'm happy to see it.

File.Name: b903e_b903-012.jpg
Notes:
67905732 This is a handsome Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly working the composite native flowers of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look at the iridescent sparkles on her wing spots! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

We have many different yellow composite flowers in our mountains that serve to keep the insects alive at the dry end at the dry end of the year.

File.Name: b903f_b903-002.jpg
Notes: 67905696 This is a tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working the same flowers of (I think) "Bluebeard" (Caryopteris x clandonensis) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden this morning as my [Previous] photo. What a beautiful workplace these little bees have! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)
ScientificName: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Caryopteris x clandonensis; Lamiaceae

File.Name: b904a_b904-038.jpg
Notes:
67912019 This is a remarkable tiny Moth (Ledpidoptera) working flowers of a native Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's definitely a "microlepidoptera" - only a few millimeters long! It looks metallic silver, though my photo shows it as gray. It has remarkable spiny hind legs which it raises in alarm - I guess to frighten off potential predators like me. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 September 2011)

*Maybe* this is a Chrysoesthia linguacella or C. drurella moth in the family Gelechiidae - the Twirler Moths?

I drove to the top of La Cumbre Peak today - 4,000 feet above Santa Barbara - looking for hill-topping butterflies. I didn't find them, but I did find a few other interesting critters.
ScientificName: Ledpidoptera; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Chrysoesthia linguacella; C. drurella; Gelechiidae
associatedTaxa: Ledpidoptera; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Chrysoesthia linguacella; C. drurella; Gelechiidae

File.Name: b904b_b904-009.jpg
Notes: 67911976 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly perched on flowers of a Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another yellow-flowered composite shrub that only grows on the highest peaks of our mountains, though we have other species of Ericameria in our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 September 2011)
ScientificName: Strymon melinus; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Ericameria
associatedTaxa: Strymon melinus; Ericameria arborescens; Asteraceae; Ericameria

File.Name: b904c_b904-030.jpg
Notes:
67910431 Here is a mating pair of Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) on flowers of Golden Fleece (Ericameria arborescens) in the Asteraceae plant family - a yellow-flowered composite shrub that only grows on the highest peaks of our mountains. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 4 September 2011)

It must be a feat of cooperation for these mighty predators to "hook up" like this. They "do it" a lot. See my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67892398

File.Name: b904d_b904-050.jpg
Notes: 67910378 This is a goofy-looking net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera perched on a leaf in the woods today. If you let one jump from your hand, you can feel some force! With that grill-like front end, they remind me of tiny toy trucks. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2011)

File.Name: b904e_b904-044.jpg
Notes: 67910266 This is the mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) hanging upside down on a plant while her kids frolic nearby. I don't think many spiders exhibit maternal behavior like this. (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2011)

File.Name: b904fe_b904-043.jpg
Notes:
67910211 This is a nursery of tiny spiderlings. The Mama was sitting nearby - see my [Next] photo.

They are tiny Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae). The nest was on an unusual native plant of Rough-leaved Aster (Aster radulinus) in the Asteraceae plant family - but I don't think it matters to them! (San Marcos Pass, 4 September 2011)
ScientificName: spiderlings; Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster radulinus; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: spiderlings; Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; Aster radulinus; Asteraceae

File.Name: b905a_b905-036.jpg
Notes:
67915477 Getting ready for the late night music jam!

This is a male Snowy Tree Cricket (Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera. I found it perched on a spent leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2011)

These males are the main music-makers in our late-night chorus jam. They are "thermometer crickets" - just count the chirps in 15 seconds and add 40 to get degrees Fahrenheit! I have no idea if that really works, but they do chirp faster on warm nights. I like the cross-veins on its large wings.
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b905b_b905-010.jpg
Notes:
67915291 Here is a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working new late-summer flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 September 2011)

I was looking for Metalmark (family Riodinidae) butterflies, but they still aren't here. It will be interesting if they skip this year. How will the population continue?
ScientificName: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Metalmark; Riodinidae
associatedTaxa: Junonia coenia; Lepidospartum squamatum; Metalmark; Riodinidae

File.Name: b905c_b905-008.jpg
Notes:
67915198 I believe this is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working new flowers of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. This is a tight crop on a windy day. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 September 2011)

It's always windy at the river!

File.Name: b905d_b905-003.jpg
Notes:
67915149 This isn't the best photo - but look close!

It's a little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. It's carrying a large load of pollen.

Now look at the remarkable mouthparts under its head. I think this outdoes the Alien in the movies! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 September 2011)

File.Name: b905e_b905-012.jpg
Notes: 67914998 Here is one of the many bees and wasps (order Hymenoptera) working the flowers of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. I have no idea what this is. I guess it's a Wild Bee (family Apidae), though I can't make out pollen baskets. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 September 2011)

File.Name: b905f_b905-019.jpg
Notes:
67914845 Here is one of the last flowers (note the buds) of the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 5 September 2011)

It's a beautiful large shrub that has an odd distribution. I find it in the foothills in Santa Barbara; along the Santa Ynez River at Paradise; and in the remote back country by Pool Rock - but not in between. It's interesting that all these places are arranged nearly north-south across our east-west mountains.

File.Name: b906a_b824-042.jpg
Notes:
67920281 This is a perfect Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) in the order Hymenoptera working flowers of horticultural Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden this morning. It's got full pollen baskets. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2011)

Honey bees are so common, that it's hard to remember that they are not native to California. Before there were honey bees, there were lots of wild bees. There still are - but they are harder to find.

File.Name: b906b_b906-026.jpg
Notes:
67920211 This is a Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working the flowers of a small non-native succulent plant with long flower stems. Julie thinks it's a small Aloe plant, and that's good enough for me. Look how the bee fills the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2011)

Julie has these plants growing in containers on the front porch where I like to rest and drink a cold beer after the day's hike. I've been noticing the same bee coming around to the same flowers day after day. I finally got a photo.

File.Name: b906c_b906-017.jpg
Notes:
67919957 This is another Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - probably the Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus) though you can't see its "spurs" in this photo. It's perched on the new late-summer flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today.

Is this a "threat pose" as a potential predator (me) gets too close? See my [Previous] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2011)

I went back to the river today to meet with local film-maker Michael Love who is looking for insect shots for a new film he is making. We found some, but it was "too darned hot" to go very long - 98∞F in the shade, and we were out in the sun!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b906d_b906-022.jpg
Notes: 67919790 This is a remarkable Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. I believe it is a Four-spurred Assassin Bug (Zelus tetracanthus) because of the spines on the back of its thorax. This one is perched on new flower clusters of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2011)
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Four-spurred Assassin Bug; Coastal Goldenbush
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Zelus tetracanthus; Isocoma menziesii; Asteraceae

File.Name: b906e_b828-009.jpg
Notes:
67919533 This is the husk of a large Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) on a plant stem in the woods. The back is split wide open where the adult crawled out. These adult cicadas are part of the evening music jam in the woods. What a opportunity - to leave your old life behind in a husk and start over as a new adult! (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

I vaguely recall an old science fiction story that described what metamorphosis feels like. I did some snooping on the web, and I believe the story is "The Rose" by Charles L. Harness (1974). I remember it as being remarkable. I'll read it (again) too!

File.Name: b906f_b906-002.jpg
Notes:
67919308 These are beautiful new native flowers of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. These are the late-season flowers that keep the hummingbirds going in the fall. Look close - you can see pollen grains on the female stigma of the flowers, and more on the male anthers above. (San Marcos Pass, 6 September 2011)

The flowers are in our garden, but they native plants washed out by rainfall a few years ago that we rescued. We give them a bit of summer water, but not much.

File.Name: b907a_b905-031.jpg
Notes:
67925202 "Pleased to meet you..."

This is the same Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) as my [Previous] photo. I'm sorry it stepped into the shade, but it's still a keeper photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2011)

Today's photos are all grasshoppers. I don't know much about their taxonomy, so every grasshopper looks different to me! I think the most interesting thing about them is the color of their wings when they take wing. Alas - it's impossible to capture.

The crickets and cicadas are going at it tonight. It was another very hot day. We almost got to 100 ∞F on the Pass, and it got up to 106.5 ∞F in the Santa Ynez Valley! That's too hot for butterflies and photographers. Tomorrow we go to the beach.

This is "fire weather". I'm trying not to think about it too much, but we are ready.

File.Name: b907b_b905-032.jpg
Notes: 67925120 This is an adult Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a stem of the native shrub Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe this is the same grasshopper as my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2011)

File.Name: b907c_b905-018.jpg
Notes: 67925065 I found this beautiful gray Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a twig by the river. The colors seem to be very good camouflage in this summer-drab landscape. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2011)

File.Name: b907d_b905-029.jpg
Notes: 67925040 Here is another handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a stem of the native shrub Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This shrub is just starting to flower. This grasshopper is still immature - note the short "trainer wings". (San Marcos Pass, 5 September 2011)

File.Name: b907e_b819-003.jpg
Notes:
67924959 I found this handsome Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a stem of the native shrub Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family a few days ago. Note the short "trainer wings" - it's still immature. (San Marcos Pass, 3 September 2011)

It looks a lot like the grasshopper in my [Next] photo, but there are subtle differences in the colors and wings. Maybe they are a potential mating pair?

File.Name: b907f_b906-007.jpg
Notes:
67924913 I found this Short-horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera) perched on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family down by the river yesterday. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 6 September 2011)

Today's photos are all grasshoppers. I don't know much about their taxonomy, so every grasshopper looks different to me! I think the most interesting thing about grasshoppers is the color of their wings when they take wing. Alas - it's impossible to capture.

File.Name: b908a_b908-027.jpg
Notes:
67929748 I believe this tiny critter is a Black Scavenger Fly in the family Sepsidae of the order Diptera. I only noticed it because I was already focused on a tiny (but larger) bee on a nearby flower. They may be common for all I know, but this is the first one I've noticed.

It was a good day for photos of flies! It was a few degrees cooler than yesterday, but then the winds picked up. I'm glad I got my photos early in the day.

This cricket is not quite an adult because of those short wings. I reckon it has one more molt to go before it can join the nighttime chorus jam!

We had to drive south to Oxnard today, but we made the best of it and walked along the beach around the point past the river mouth of the Ventura River. This is "California Street" - now "C-Street" - where I used to hang out when I was a surfer dude back in the 60s!

We got back late and I'm tired. These three photos will do just fine.

It has a spicy taste and makes great peppermint tea when dried. It's not common in our mountains, though there is a large colony in our canyon - where we got the cuttings to start our own colony.

File.Name: b911a_b907-003.jpg
Notes:
67943278 This is one of our most interesting insect galls, though it doesn't have any visible structure like the galls I've been showing today. I believe this is the gall (or at least a sign of the gall) of a Mottled Acorn Gall Wasp (Callirhytis carmelensis) - or kin - in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The gall wasp larvae is making changes in the oak's acorn to produce real honey under pressure that will ultimately force the immature acorn from its cup. "Live Oak honey" - sounds like an oxymoron if you've ever nibbled an acorn, but here it is! It really does taste sweet. Note the little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on the lowest acorn! (San Marcos Pass, 7 September 2011)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.

My photos today are all plant galls. Some of them go back a few weeks - I've been saving them for a rainy day, which was today! Well, it was cool and drizzly at least. This is unusual weather for September!

File.Name: b912b_b912-020.jpg
Notes:
67947873 This is a remarkable Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera). It looks a bit like the Common Blow Fly or Blue Bottle Fly. But these are trash flies, and I found this critter in the woods. And what gives with that great yellow mouth and impressive proboscis??

I did some browsing at BugGuide, and I now believe this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). See the images at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/36836/bgimage

Yes it is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This one is working the flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 September 2011)

This fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feed on already dead tissue. But it actually has some use. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds.

I obviously don't know what I'm talking about! Visit Wikipedia for a start, and follow the links. Interesting stuff!
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cochliomyia
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Common Blow Fly; Blue Bottle Fly; Secondary Screwworm; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
