67966888 I found this handsome Agaricus mushroom in the garden today! I have no idea what kind it is, or whether it's ok to eat. That's not the point. This is the first new mushroom of the year - in September! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)

It feels like autumn today - though the autumnal equinox is still a week away. We were in the stratus cloud gloom until after 3:00 pm, and the temperature barely got out of the 50s for most of the day. I can't remember when I last wore long pants. (It feels funky after months with shorts!) Sure it will warm up next week, but I hope this is an omen for a rainy fall!
Locality: It feels funky after months with shorts!)  Sure it will warm up next week

File.Name: b916b_b916-021.jpg
Notes: 67966809 I believe this is the tiny larvae of a Green Lacewing (Chrysopidae) in the insect order Neuroptera. It's taking apart another insect nest on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf and eating the eggs. If you look close, you can see its impressive mandibles! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)

File.Name: b916c_b916-017.jpg
Notes: 67966741 This is a beautiful Lepidoptera caterpillar on flower stems of native Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of butterfly it will become. I think it looks like a Checkerspot with those "radar tower" spines on its back - maybe Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii), though the timing seems wrong. I can't find any photo on he Web. Whatever it is... here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae; Chlosyne gabbii
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae; Chlosyne gabbii

File.Name: b916d_b916-013.jpg
Notes: 67966453 I guess this is an interesting Lepidoptera chrysalis on a Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family leaf. It seems to have eyes on its back. It sure looked like a bird turd when I found it today. I had to get very close before I realized it was something else! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)

File.Name: b916e_b916-004.jpg
Notes: 67966444 Here is an impressive Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) bundling some unfortunate prey on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family in the woods today. The spider looks something like the Cat-Faced Spider - Araneus gemmoides. It has sad world-weary eyes - lots of them! (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Wild Blackberry; Cat-Faced Spider
ScientificName: Araneidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Araneus gemmoides
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Araneus gemmoides

File.Name: b916f_b916-027.jpg
Notes:
67966318 These are beautiful ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family in just the right light. The trees are related to avocado, and it show in the fruit, which look like little avocado "pups". (San Marcos Pass, 16 September 2011)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so - and the fruit are even more so than the leaves. But hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when roasted - rich, like bitter chocolate. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56886172

File.Name: b917a_b917-016.jpg
Notes:
67970584 "I didn't know they could do that!"

This is an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) sticking its mighty proboscis into a flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family rather than another insect! These are consummate predators, so this is a surprise. Maybe it's a male, and only the females are predators? Or maybe it's just hungry? Or it's gone vegan? (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

Today was another gloomy autumn day, though it cleared earlier than yesterday. The National Weather service promises that tomorrow will be warmer. Summer isn't over yet!

File.Name: b917b_b917-025.jpg
Notes:
67970461 Of course that's a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) at the bottom of my photo, but look close on top to see the pissed-off looking Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera)!

I guess bees are a bit too much for these predators. This photo gives a good scale for the little Ambush Bugs - see my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Ambush Bug; Ambush Bugs

File.Name: b917c_b917-009.jpg
Notes: 67970401 This is a beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. There are so many kinds of these tiny flies that I can't tell them apart, even looking at my photos side-by-side! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

File.Name: b917d_b917-021.jpg
Notes:
67970373 This is another honeybee-like (and honeybee-size) Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

Maybe this fly is genus Eristalis? The larvae of these flies have the lovely name "Rat-tailed Maggots" because they develop a long breathing tube from their abdomen! See the web page at:
   http://www.canacoll.org/Diptera/Staff/Skevington/Syrphidae/Eristalis/Eristalis.htm
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Eristalis
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Eristalis

File.Name: b917e_b917-051.jpg
Notes:
67970330 I've been showing the male "staminate" plants of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family, but this is the female seed-bearing "pistillate" plant which is just starting to flower in the canyon. The flowers are quite different. From a distance, these shrubs look white (female) rather then yellow (male).

The Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) are still interested. The flowers must produce some nectar, but probably not much - the bees move from flower to flower so fast that it's hard to grab a photo! This one is a bit over-exposed. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

File.Name: b917f_b917-034.jpg
Notes: 67970268 Here is another goofy-looking Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. Maybe it's family Issidae, genus Neaethus? It was perched on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I guess it's a gravid female. She looks large with eggs. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Neaethus; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: b918a_b918-024.jpg
Notes:
67974528 Here is another photo of our most impressive wasp - a Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's one of the world's largest wasps - as big as a dragonfly and almost as large as a small hummingbird! I believe this is a female because of the coiled antennae. In this photo you can see her brilliant blue iridescent colors. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

It was a good day for photos at the river today, though it was breezy - it's always windy there! The morning gloom has cleared out, and it almost feels like summer again.

File.Name: b918b_b918-013.jpg
Notes:
67974404 This is our most impressive wasp - a Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's one of the world's largest wasps - as big as a dragonfly and almost as large as a small hummingbird! I believe this is a female, though she has straightened out her antennae. (They should be coiled.) This one is working new composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

I haven't seen any Tarantula Spiders (Aphonopelma) out yet this year, but they usually come out of their burrows in the fall to find mates. The female wasps battle and try to sting a tarantula to paralyze it. Then they drag the poor spider into their nest and lay an egg on it. Think of the movie "Alien"...

They have beautiful blue iridescent colors, but like hummingbirds, you have to have the sun just right to see it. See my [Next] photo.
CommonNames: Tarantula Hawk; Scale Broom; Tarantula Spiders
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Aphonopelma

File.Name: b918c_b918-035.jpg
Notes:
67974265 Love play among the butterflies!

These are Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies. One of them is working the new late-summer flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today, but the other is flying 'round and 'round and won't give her any rest. I think they are in love! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

I assume that's the female below, and the pursuing male in the air. That's how it works with us, but I might be wrong with butterflies!

File.Name: b918d_b918-022.jpg
Notes:
67974184 I've been waiting for this critter! This is a handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working a composite flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

There should be dozens or hundreds of them right now working these flowers, but I only saw this one today. I was happy to see it! Flowers and butterflies are both late this year.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b918e_b918-092.jpg
Notes:
67974127 Here is a pretty Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

The butterfly was actually hanging upside down. I flipped the photo so it would look more "natural".

File.Name: b918f_b918-007.jpg
Notes:
67974074 This is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her precious egg sack. She is perched on a flowering stem of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

All of my photos today are taken on this late-season flowering shrub. It attracts much life at the end of the year!

File.Name: b919a_b918-042.jpg
Notes:
67979759 Butterflies in love!

Here are a pair of Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies that seem to have finally settled down to discuss their affairs. A moment later, they fluttered off together. It's sweet how the lower one's antenna is placed between the other's. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

This action is taking place inside a thicket of new composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. For the earlier stage of this story, see my photo with the same two butterflies:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67974265
Locality: Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. For the earlier stage of this story

File.Name: b919b_b915-032.jpg
Notes: 67979627 This is a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin, though there are other species. It's perched on a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You gotta love those eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

File.Name: b919c_b918-026.jpg
Notes:
67979579 Here are a pair of little needle-thin Damselflies (suborder Zygoptera) in the insect order Odonata. Both are perched on native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

My question is are they the same species or different? Sure the top one is paler than the bottom one, so maybe they are female and male? They hover around the flowers like little cargo helicopters. You can almost see them swimming in the air. It's just a different Reynolds number, right?

File.Name: b919c_b918-085.jpg
Notes: 67979462 This is a beautiful Wasp (Hymenoptera) working new flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. I don't know what it is, but those amazing eyes remind me of a wasp I've found before in the Sand Wasp group - tribe Bembicini of the Apoid Wasps (Apoidea/Sphecidae). All I know is that it is beautiful! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

File.Name: b919d_b918-067.jpg
Notes: 67979289 I believe this is a beautiful Paper Wasp in the genus Polistes of the family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera - but I'm not sure of the species. There are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" - all handsome. This one is working new flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

File.Name: b919f_b912-032.jpg
Notes:
67979073 This is something magical!

It's the egg-sized silk cocoon of a native Ceanothus Silk Moth (Hyalophora euryalus) on the ground under a Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the (Rhamnaceae) plant family. I'm not sure if that's the exit hole of the moth or some horrible (but fascinating!) parasite. (San Marcos Pass, 17 September 2011)

The silk cocoons feel and sound like touching the paper cone of a stereo speaker. It's a magical sound, and Native American Chumash shamans made rattles from the cocoons. The large adult moths are uncommon, and the cocoons are a rare find. I've been collecting them for years. I have a basket full, and I plan to make my own rattle when the time is right. I'll be looking for the adult moths on the windows at night.

I have an old photo of the adult moth here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/33199599

File.Name: b920a_b920-025.jpg
Notes:
67984276 This is a beautiful critter in the insect order Neuroptera perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) by the creek today. Look at its beautiful eyes - a green film over an orange core. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

My first thought was Brown Lacewing in the family Hemerobiidae, but now I'm not sure - the wings don't seem right. There are also Green Lacewings (Chrysopidae) that are brown, and many more. Whatever it is, it is handsome and interesting. The antennae are as long as its wings.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Brown Lacewing; Green Lacewings

File.Name: b920b_b920-022.jpg
Notes:
67984104 This is a beautiful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera) dancing over the flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

Fennel is an invasive weed in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies. And then we try to remember to cut the seed heads so they don't come up everywhere!
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: b920c_b920-015.jpg
Notes:
67984057 This is a handsome West Coast Lady (Vanessa annabella) butterfly on a flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. It looks a lot like our other "Painted Lady" butterflies (V. cardui and V. virgeniensis), but there are small differences - like four small "eyes" under the hindwings rather than two large ones. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

Like the Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii), Mexican Sunflower seems to be a magnet for interesting butterflies in the woods!
CommonNames: West Coast Lady; Mexican Sunflower; Butterfly Bush
ScientificName: Vanessa annabella; Tithonia rotundifolia; V. cardui; V. virgeniensis; Buddleia davidii
associatedTaxa: Vanessa annabella; Tithonia rotundifolia; V. cardui; V. virgeniensis; Buddleia davidii

File.Name: b920d_b920-021.jpg
Notes:
67983944 I'm pretty sure this is a Flesh Fly (family Sarcophaga in the order Diptera) - "Robust legs and ... bright red compound eyes are the hallmarks for these large flies." I'm sorry that it's face isn't quite in focus, but look closer at the three bright red Mites (Acari) hitching a ride on this fly - and the mites are in focus! The mites match the color of its eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

This fly has front legs, but it has them folded under it's body. Maybe the mites bother it?

File.Name: b920e_b920-031.jpg
Notes:
67983850 Even mighty predators have to (or get to) mate!

Here is a pair of loving Ambush Bugs (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) getting intimate in a mating pose. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

They are small but mighty predators. I found another one today that had captured a Grass-Skipper butterfly, but it was down an embankment and I couldn't get the photo. (I tried.) See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/51894766
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55733081

File.Name: b920f_b917-040.jpg
Notes: 67983664 This is a another little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It seems different from the other syrphids I've been posting. For one thing, it keeps moving it's abdomen in and out in a sinuous motion. It's a pretty little fly. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

File.Name: b921a_b921-008.jpg
Notes:
67989062 This is another small [b]Mayfly[/b] (order [i]Ephemeroptera[/i]) that I found perched on the side of our house this morning. Note that the [Previous] mayfly had two tails, but this one only has one. Maybe they are male and female, but I don't know which. The creek is far away and mostly dry, so I reckon this mayfly hatched from one of our water garden tubs. It has a proud bearing! (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2011)

I thought it would be easy to distinguish one and two tailed mayflies, but all the web pages talk about two and three tailed mayflies. I don't know which is which, but I reckon I would tie a "Blue Dunn" fishing fly to match this hatch!

This photo gives an idea of the Depth of Field I usually work with - not much!

File.Name: b921b_b921-003.jpg
Notes: 67989010 This is a small [b]Mayfly[/b] (order [i]Ephemeroptera[/i]) that I found perched on the side of our house this morning. The creek is far away and mostly dry, so I reckon this mayfly hatched from one of our water garden tubs. Note that this mayfly has two tails, and see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2011)

File.Name: b921c_b918-044.jpg
Notes:
67988852 I [heart] true bugs!

This little Plant Bug (family Miridae) in the order Hemiptera - probably genus Lygus - is perched on flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river. It has a heart on its back! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

File.Name: b921d_b919-001.jpg
Notes: 67988667 This is a tiny black Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) "butts up" in a flower of Basil (Ocimum basilicum) in the garden. You can see the "pollen baskets" on its hind legs that identify it as a bee. (San Marcos Pass, 19 September 2011)

File.Name: b921e_b920-019.jpg
Notes:
67988601 Here is the tiny larvae of a Green Lacewing (family Chrysopidae or kin) in the insect order Neuroptera prowling around flowers of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

Fennel is an invasive weed in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the Anise Swallowtail (Papilio zelicaon) butterflies. And then we try to remember to cut the seed heads so they don't come up everywhere. Looks like it's time to trim this one!
ScientificName: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon
associatedTaxa: Chrysopidae; Neuroptera; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae; Papilio zelicaon

File.Name: b921f_b918-071.jpg
Notes:
67988505 This is the same nest of fierce Yellow Jacket Wasps (genus Vespula in the order Hymenoptera) that I photographed a while ago. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67715442

The wasps have moved a lot of dirt, and rocks since then! I reckon the small gravel rocks around the hole were all brought up by the wasps. The soil around the hole has an organic look - like H.R. Giger's sets for the movie "Alien". (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 September 2011)

I always believed these wasps take over a gopher hole, but I believe they made this one themselves!

File.Name: b922a_b922-015.jpg
Notes:
67993585 This is another photo of the uncommon Queen Butterfly (Danaus gilippus)! This is a desert species that sometimes makes it to the coast in autumn. It looks a lot like a Monarch (Danaus plexippus), but the colors are different. See my [Previous] photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

It's another butterfly whose caterpillars eat milkweed leaves, which contain toxins that are unpalatable to birds. They advertise the fact with these bright aposematic colors. native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river near the Sage Hill campground.

We had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society meeting tonight, but I left early after helping to set up just to post these photos. Just my luck - fotolog is down tonight. It seems like fotolog has been down a lot lately...
Locality:
Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river near the Sage Hill campground.

We had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society meeting tonight
ScientificName: Danaus gilippus; Danaus plexippus; aposematic; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Danaus gilippus; Danaus plexippus; aposematic; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b922b_b922-021.jpg
Notes:
67993561 The same day as the King, I found this Queen Butterfly (Danaus gilippus)! This is a desert species that sometimes makes it to the coast in autumn. It looks a lot like a Monarch (Danaus plexippus). The spots are the same, but the colors are different. Compare my [Previous] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

This is an uncommon butterfly in our coastal mountains, but there are a few reports on the Web - mostly by my butterfly-friend Nick Lethaby, who noticed a Queen in Goleta on September 13. This is the first one I've ever seen.

My photo is deep in a thicket of new composite flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river near the Sage Hill campground. Even though it's not far from the river, it feels like a desert here.
Locality: Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash of the river near the Sage Hill campground. Even though it's not far from the river
ScientificName: Danaus gilippus; Danaus plexippus; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Danaus gilippus; Danaus plexippus; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b922c_b922-054.jpg
Notes:
67993526 This is a Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - the King. It's working new flowers of Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This plant doesn't look like a typical goldenrod. It grows in wet places, like this bank along what's left of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

You might think Monarchs would be common here, since thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away in trees by the coast... but they are actually scarce. We don't see them until autumn, which is now with the Autumnal Equinox tomorrow.

This is the King, but the Queen is more unusual. See my [Next] photo!
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae

File.Name: b923a_b922-012.jpg
Notes:
67996703 This is a beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - working a composite flower head of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river yesterday. I love those eyes! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

Today was an interesting weather day. We had tropical weather with a few rain drops from the south. It was overcast most of the day, which flip-flopped between gale-force winds and dead calm. We are actually under a "Red Flag Warning" because of the threat of dry lightening strikes, but so far there's no lightning. I'm not worried.

Today is also the Autumnal Equinox for those of us living north of the equator. It's the official first day of autumn here, and it feels like autumn. We're losing the sun at our house on the north side of the south-facing Santa Ynez Mountains. What will winter bring?
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b923b_b922-061.jpg
Notes:
67996623 Here is a remarkable Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae) in the order Hymenoptera working new flowers of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the river. This plant is a relative of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis), but it's different. It's endemic to Southern California. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

I know, this photo is over-exposed - but still pretty cool! I wonder what it does with that long ovipositor in our woods?

Sara Allen Plummer Lemmon was one of the early naturalists who settled in Santa Barbara in the 1890s. It was Sara Lemmon who became the leader of the movement to have the California Poppy adopted as the California state flower.
CommonNames: Ichneumon Wasp; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Ichneumonidae; Hymenoptera; Baccharis plummerae; Asteraceae; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: b923c_b922-056.jpg
Notes: 67996404 I guess this is a handsome Meadowlark Dragonfly (genus Sympetrum, family Libellulidae, order Odonata) - but I really don't know. It's perched on a leaf by the river hanging over water. I had to get my shoes wet to get this photo! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011)

File.Name: b923d_b922-047.jpg
Notes: 67996199 This is an impressive Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. I reckon it's an Argiope spider - maybe the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata), though this doesn't look silver at all. It does have those impressive spikes on the back of its abdomen. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 22 September 2011) You can see a bit of its impressive stabilimentum - the crisscross band of silk through the center of the web. There's still debate about why so many spiders make it.

File.Name: b923e_b922-003.jpg
Notes: 67996126 This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has a breakfast fly - I'm sure it's a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera. File.Name: b923f_b923-002.jpg
Notes:
67996088 This is a tiny Spider in a small flower cluster of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family by the creek. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, and I won't try to guess. (San Marcos Pass, 23 September 2011)

Today was an interesting weather day. We had tropical weather with a few rain drops from the south. It was overcast most of the day, which flip-flopped between gale-force winds and dead calm. We are actually under a "Red Flag Warning" because of the threat of dry lightening strikes, but so far there's no lightning. I'm not worried. I got this photo under cloudy skies.

File.Name: b924a_b924-021.jpg
Notes:
68000529 This Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) is snuggling into a new flower of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The beautiful flowers close early in the day, by 10 or 11 unless it's foggy. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

We had a bit of heavy rain last night - for about 3 minutes. We got 0.025 inches of rain in our gauge. Not much, but it's a start on the new rain year!
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

Notes: 68000413 I was trying to get focus on these small flowers of Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family today... when I noticed this tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

File.Name: b924c_b924-046.jpg
Notes:
68000361 This is a late-season flower of Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family growing in the mountains. It's a curious plant. Most of its relaties grow in wet places, but this late-summer species flowers along dry trails roads in the mountains no matter how dry it has been. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

This photo shows the whole family - bud, flower, seed pods (from inferior ovary), and opening seeds.

File.Name: b924d_b924-011.jpg
Notes:
68000273 Like a fly in a furnace!

This is another golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a flower head of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. You can see it taking pollen on its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

My camera always over-saturates yellow and orange, but it sure is pretty!

File.Name: b924e_b924-047.jpg
Notes:
68000175 This is the pretty gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It has clown colors! (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

Remember - if it looks like a fruit, but it's growing on an Oak, and it's not an acorn... then it must be a gall!
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: b924f_b916-024.jpg
Notes:
68000150 A green apple on an Oak tree?

No, oaks have acorns. This is the gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It is growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.

When insects get scarce in the mountains, there are always new galls to photograph! They nicely fill the gap between summer insects and winter mushrooms.
Locality:
Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.

When insects get scarce in the mountains
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae

File.Name: b925a_b925-012.jpg
Notes:
68004829 This beautiful Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly appeared just when the sun broke through the gloom today. I guess butterflies and sunshine go together like cheese and wine. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

If you don't like spiders, stay away from my [Previous] three photos today - otherwise enjoy them!

It was cool and cloudy and windy for most of the day as our first real storm front from the north moved in. This is a dry front, but the seasons are a'changin'! This is our typical see-saw autumn weather pattern. It's expected to turn hot again next week.

File.Name: b925b_b925-003.jpg
Notes:
68004799 Here is another beautiful Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on staminate flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon he owns these flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

Today was a good day for crab spider photos but a lousy day for photos in general. It was cool and cloudy and windy for most of the day as our first real storm front from the north moved in. This is a dry front, but the seasons are a'changin'!

This is our typical see-saw autumn weather pattern. It's expected to turn hot again next week.

File.Name: b925c_b925-006.jpg
Notes:
68004753 This is another little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on staminate flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

Uh, do you notice something missing? This spider is missing its left two forelegs - a major bummer for a critter which pinches its prey! I guess losing limbs is an occupational hazard for these spiders who regularly grab prey larger than they are and try to hang on.

I show another crab spider's solution to this problem at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/8328780

File.Name: b925d_b925-025.jpg
Notes:
68004617 This is the inside of a flower head of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden today. Did you notice the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) at the bottom of the photo? Now that's good camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

I apologize for the color - this is what my Nikon D300 does with bright yellow-orange, a bit over-saturated. I actually toned it down a bit.

File.Name: b925e_b923-007.jpg
Notes:
68004513 These are the smallest Ants I've ever noticed! They are doing something on a single flower head of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't know what kind of ant they are, but they do have a waist of two segments with a postpetiole. Tiny as they are, they look just like ants! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

This is a tight crop. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The ant on top is 248 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the ant is 23.6x248/4288 = 1.36 mm - less than 1/16 inch across!

File.Name: b925f_b923-003.jpg
Notes:
68004355 I believe this is the brown form of the Western Tree Cricket (Oecanthus californicus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera. It's snuggling into a native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. This must be a female with her ovipositor evident. Maybe like the proverbial ostrich, she believes she is invisible if her head is hidden! (San Marcos Pass, 25 September 2011)

Like most musicians, these crickets work the night sift and sleep during the day. I believe these crickets make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (also genus Oecanthus) which pulse. There's a sound sample at:
   http://entnemdept.ufl.edu/walker/buzz/586a.htm
CommonNames: Western Tree Cricket; Mugwort; Snowy Tree Crickets
ScientificName: Oecanthus californicus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oecanthus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus californicus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Oecanthus

File.Name: b926a_b926-048.jpg
Notes:
68009269 Here is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (maybe Pepsis mildei?, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's peacefully working flowers of native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) on the dry wash by the river, but they are hell with tarantula spiders! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2011)

This is one of the world's largest wasps - as big as a dragonfly and almost as large as a small hummingbird! I believe this is a female because of the size and coiled antennae. In this photo you can see her brilliant blue iridescent colors. They seem clumsy on the flowers - too big! See my photo from September 18 for more info:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67974404

Yesterday was cloudy and cool. Today was pleasant and clear. Tomorrow is forecast to be hot. That's our autumn in a nutshell - it swings back and forth.
ScientificName: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Pepsis mildei; Pompilidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: b926b_b926-082.jpg
Notes:
68009160 I believe that this is an interesting California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica or kin) dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata. I got this photo on the summer-dry floodplain along the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2011)

It's an uncommon dragonfly. Real dragonflies hold their wings out in parallel when they rest. Damselflies fold their wings in along their body. This one is in between. They may be common, but I don't see them very often.

File.Name: b926c_b926-018.jpg
Notes:
68009097 "Are you looking at me?"

Here I'm making camera-to-eye contact with a little Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera) in the insect order Odonata. I love those split-level eyes! Sure, it's a tight crop. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2011)

File.Name: b926d_b926-070.jpg
Notes:
68009008 This is a handsome Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly in the woods by the river today. You can't see much of the flower it's working, but it is Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 September 2011)

I haven't seen a Mylitta Crescent since August 20, and it looks newly emerged. Butterflies have been scarce this year, so it's always a pleasure to find new ones - even ones I've already found!

Insects have to hunt now to find the open flowers in the dense flower heads of mostly spent flowers on these plants.

File.Name: b927e_b927-009.jpg
Notes:
68013121 There are crickets hiding everywhere!

This is a male Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera. This one is trying to hide under the petals of a flower of a composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2011)

These are the "thermometer crickets" that sing with a pulsing call that speeds up on warmer nights. Sometimes they create beautiful jams, but mostly they stay in synch with each other. Now how do they do that?

These pretty flies are predators. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67878018

File.Name: b928f_b927-020.jpg
Notes:
68017739 I haven't seen many aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) lately, but here is a new batch on the undersides of the leaves of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2011)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The aphid on the bottom is 113 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the aphid is 23.6x113/4288 = 0.62 mm - really tiny!

It's fun using a digital SLR as an expensive micrometer, but I can only do it if I know the magnification or focus distance. Unfortunately, my Nikon camera doesn't save that data as part of the EXIF data file. Or maybe it does? My camera must know how far away the focus point is, and there are strings of "nonsense" numbers in the EXIF info that might be the answer. It would be a valuable hack to figure this out!

File.Name: b929a_b929-005.jpg
Notes:
68022346 This a a perfect little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a cactus spine in the garden this morning. It looks like a studio shot, but it isn't. This fly perched so quietly that I was able to use the self-timer function of my camera to get the photo without my usual jitters! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2011)

If you compare my [Previous] photo, you can see how different these syrphid flies are.

The weather is changing. It was breezy and cooler today, with a bit of morning fog. There's a chance of rain coming up!

File.Name: b929b_b926-012.jpg
Notes:
68022276 This is a different photo of the same little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) that I posted here a few days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68008845

It's working a composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. You can see how different it is from the syrphid fly in my [Next] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2011)

File.Name: b929c_b921-012.jpg
Notes:
68022126 Nappy time for hummers!

I caught this little Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) taking a nap in a native shrub of Coffee Berry (Rhamnus tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family hanging over our bathtub water pond at about 10 am.

It sat here quietly for maybe twenty minutes, slowly opening and closing its eyes. I guess even the hyper hummingbirds need a nap sometimes! (San Marcos Pass, 26 September 2011)

I've been using my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens trying to capture the pretty new birds in the woods - mostly warblers. Not much luck so far. I find it much easier to photograph butterflies than birds!

File.Name: b929d_b929-011.jpg
Notes:
68021918 This is a little Moth (Ledpidoptera) trying to hide under a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family - until I nudged it into the sunlight. It's just a "little brown moth" (LBM), but what pretty eyes it has! (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2011)

I like its dog-like snout - or is that bat-like?

File.Name: b929e_b929-003.jpg
Notes:
68021850 I found this odd spider on a spent flower of composite Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 29 September 2011)

It's odd because I'm pretty sure this is an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae, not the usual Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I find on these flowers. But here it is. It seems to have enlarged palps. Maybe it's a male looking for a female?
CommonNames: Mexican Sunflower; Orb-Weaver Spider; Crab Spider

(San Marcos Pass, 27 September 2011)

See my [Previous] photo for more info.

File.Name: b930a_b930-015.jpg
Notes:
68026682 Here is a mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her precious egg sack against a potential prey - that darn photographer, me! You can see her chelicerae tipped with small fangs below (actually above, in my photo) her eyes, with pedipalps to the sides. Her nest is on a flowering stem of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2011)

Green Lynx Spiders have impressive mothering instincts. They guard their eggs for many weeks, and keep an eye on the spiderlings after they emerge.

I had to do something in town late today, so three photo will do just fine tonight.

Update 1 Oct. 11: I found another Green Lynx Spider with a slender abdomen guarding an egg sack by the river today. See my [Next] photo. I now believe this spider with the fat abdomen is Peucetia longipalpis.
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; Oxyopidae; chelicerae; pedipalps; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: b930b_b930-012.jpg
Notes: 68026573 This is a bright yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a staminate flower cluster of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon the spider owns these flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2011)

File.Name: b930c_b930-003.jpg
Notes:
68026532 These are the odd "flowers" of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look close to see the well-camouflaged Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the upper-right patiently waiting for an insect to come by. (San Marcos Pass, 30 September 2011)

Mugwort appears to be wind-pollinated, but there must be more to it. I take this spider as proof that insects are interested in the flowers!

File.Name: ba01a_ba01-035.jpg
Notes:
68030931 The mating game...

These are frisky Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterflies with love on their minds! I guess that's the female at the bottom of the pile. She's being coy, concentrating on food. Then there's not one but two males fluttering above her trying to win her affection! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

Of course I found this action on the flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river - like most of my photos today.

File.Name: ba01b_ba01-061.jpg
Notes: 68030878 This is a beautiful Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly working new flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. It was windy enough to lift up its tails, but somehow I got the perfect photo! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

File.Name: ba01c_ba01-078.jpg
Notes:
68030855 I found this frog today in a little pool that was cut-off from the main pond of the Santa Ynez River. (It doesn't flow much at this time of year.) I guess it's about 2 inches long - definitely larger than a tree frog. I couldn't get closer than this crop. The water looks milky - I suspect it's a sulfur spring. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

What kind of frog is it? I hoped it might be endangered California Red-legged Frog (Rana draytonii), but I'm afraid those large webbed hind feet give it away as an immature American Bullfrog (Lithobates catesbeianus) - the mortal enemy of Red-legged Frogs! Bullfrogs are an introduced species in California, and they cause real environmental damage.

(I'll send my photo to someone who knows to make sure.)
Locality: Lithobates catesbeianus) - the mortal enemy of Red-legged Frogs! Bullfrogs are an introduced species in California
CommonNames: frog; California Red-legged Frog; American Bullfrog

File.Name: ba01d_ba01-067.jpg
Notes: 68030746 This is an impressive Thread-Waisted Wasp (family Sphecidae) in the order Hymenoptera - maybe genus Prionyx. It's working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

File.Name: ba01e_ba01-046.jpg
Notes:
68030667 I'm not sure what exact species this pretty Paper Wasp is, but I'm pretty sure it is genus Polistes in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. There are a number of these wasps with "desert colors" - all handsome. This one is working new flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

This is an odd view of this critter. The wasp has it head buried in a flower. But it's a great view of that little triangle of ocelli (simple eyes) between the compound eyes!

File.Name: ba01f_ba01-019.jpg
Notes:
68030585 This is a different Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans in the family Oxyopidae) guarding her precious egg sack. (See my [Previous] photo.) I found this one on the dry flood plain by the river today. It's perched on a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

This is definitely a female, with epigynum evident. What's curious is the size of her abdomen. The spider in my [Previous] photo had an abdomen like a grape, but this one is quite slender. Maybe the [Previous] spider is actually Peucetia longipalpis?
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Lepidospartum squamatum; epigynum; Peucetia longipalpis

File.Name: ba02a_ba02-043.jpg
Notes:
68035209 This Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae has captured a sad-looking Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) in the order Hymenoptera. The bee looks resigned to its fate. This is happening on a flowering native shrub of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

I believe the spider is P. longipalpis not P. viridans because of the large grape-like abdomen.

This bee will never get to deliver those full pollen sacks. All the summer pollinator insects will soon disappear as winter approaches, and the spiders and other predators will pick off what they can. It's nature's way.

The weather is changing. It sounds like we have real winter weather coming our way, starting tomorrow night. There's a 90% chance of heavy rain on Wednesday! You have to appreciate that we have a Mediterranean summer-dry, winter-wet weather pattern. It's been a long time since we've had real rain!
CommonNames: Green Lynx Spider; Honey Bee; Coyote Bush

File.Name: ba02b_ba02-061.jpg
Notes:
68035085 Look close - there's a lot happening in this photo.

Starting at the top-center, a little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) has captured a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) - much larger than he is. You can see the deadly proboscis of the ambush bug. This bee will never get to deliver those full pollen sacks.

Now look at the bottom-center of the photo, and spot the tiny Freeloader Fly (family Milichiidae of the order Diptera). The bee is still warm, and they already found it. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

It's definitely predator season in our mountains. All the handsome pollinator insects will disappear soon as winter approaches, and the spiders and other predators will pick off what they can. It's nature's way.
CommonNames: Ambush Bug; Honey Bee; Freeloader Fly

File.Name: ba02c_ba02-065.jpg
Notes:
68034946 "Be careful little wing - some flowers have claws!"

This is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a staminate flower cluster of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I reckon this spider owns these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

File.Name: ba02d_ba02-036.jpg
Notes:
68034901 There's been a blessed event (many of them!) in the nest of the Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia longipalpis, not P. viridans) in the family Oxyopidae that I showed two days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68026682

New spiderlings are hatching! Mama spider is nearby, but I could fit her in the photo. I'll try again when she is better situated. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2011)

File.Name: ba02e_b926-002.jpg
Notes:
68034819 This is a classic orb-web - the web of an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. This one is a few feet across, large but not huge. It is constructed high in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, shot from our upstairs porch with my my Tamron 200-500 mm lens. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2011)

It's remarkable that these webs can withstand not only thrashing insects, but also winds blowing through the branches. Spider webbing is amazing material!

The light has to be just right to bring out the details of a spider web. I haven't quite figured out the details, but I know it when I see it!
CommonNames: orb-web; Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ba02f_b924-056.jpg
Notes:
68034694 Here is a yellow mass of California Goldenrod (Solidago californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's one of our most dependable late summer flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 24 September 2011)

My photo is a bit overexposed, but still nice. This is for my friend Lester, just to prove we have them in California too. See Lester's photo of Wisconsin goldenrod at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/lester_hunt/92316170

File.Name: ba03a_ba03-043.jpg
Notes:
68039610 This is a handsome Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). This is the first one I've seen on the Pass this summer! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

This one is working a pistillate (seed bearing) composite flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The red background is autumn leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family.

The first winter rain is coming after our usual summer drought! Clouds are moving in, the temperature and the barometric pressure are falling, and the National Weather Service gives a 100% chance of heavy rain on Wednesday, maybe starting tonight!
Locality:
Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family.

The first winter rain is coming after our usual summer drought! Clouds are moving in

Notes:
68039492 Here is my favorite autumn predator - an Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera) - probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). They remind me of shogun-era samurai warriors! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

This one is perched a staminate flower cluster of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - just waiting for prey.

See my photo yesterday to see what they can do:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68035085

File.Name: ba03c_ba03-028.jpg
Notes:
68039426 This is a tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. If you look close, you can see that it has its proboscis out on this stem of Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

You have to be in just the right place to notice these tiny insects. They may be common, I don't know. I rarely notice them, but today I was lucky.

File.Name: ba03d_ba03-058.jpg
Notes:
68039362 These are the galls of Clasping Twig Gall Wasps (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The galls contain the wasp larvae, which control the abnormal plant growth. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

The wasps know the trick of coaxing honeydew from the bitter oaks, which attracts Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera). The ants get a meal and also defend the wasp larvae from predators. Complicated, but nature is often like that.

The galls remind me of muffins fresh from the oven. These are the first ones I've seen this year on the Pass. I'm glad the population hasn't totally vanished.
Locality: family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera). The ants get a meal and also defend the wasp larvae from predators. Complicated
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak; Ants

File.Name: ba03e_ba03-024.jpg
Notes:
68039142 This is the nest of another spider in the woods. It's a long silk tube decorated with the heads of its prey!

I believe it is an unusual (though locally common) Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. If you look close, you can see a bit of the spider at the bottom of its nest. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

File.Name: ba03f_ba03-018.jpg
Notes:
68039082 This Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae has wrapped a large prey. I'm pretty sure the spider is a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin. I believe the unfortunate "package" is a Tiger Moth (tribe Arctiini) in the Noctuoidea superfamily of the Lepidoptera. I've seen very few tiger moths at the windows at night this year. Maybe this is why? (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

It's definitely predator season in our mountains. All the summer insects that delight and bedevil us will soon disappear for winter, and the spiders and other predators will pick off what they can.

File.Name: ba04a_ba04-021.jpg
Notes:
68044251 Here is mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia longipalpis, cp. P. viridens) in the family Oxyopidae. She's keeping watch over her brood of tiny spiderlings. Her nest is on a flowering stem of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2011)

This is the same dutiful mama spider I showed a few days ago guarding her nest:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68026682

We had some rain for my birthday today! Just 0.05 inch of rain so far, but it's a start. The forecast is still for heavy rain tomorrow.

Doing the garden, digging the weeds,
Who could ask for more.
Will you still need me, will you still feed me,
When I'm sixty-four.

File.Name: ba04b_ba04-005.jpg
Notes:
68044160 Our first rainy day of the new rain year needs a rain photo!

These are multicolored berries of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family - holding some rain drops. That's a ripe berry on the right and an unripe one on the left. The birds love the berries - but they taste terrible (to me), and are probably a potent laxative. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2011)

We had our first winter storm today! Just 0.05 inch of rain so far, but it's a start. The forecast is for heavy rain tomorrow.

File.Name: ba04c_ba01-007.jpg
Notes:
68044085 This is a beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) in the order Diptera. It's getting pollen from flowers of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family growing on the edge of our garden. This is a native plant that seems quite happy with summer water. (San Marcos Pass, 1 October 2011)

See my [Previous] photo.

It's a native plant that seems quite happy with summer water. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

File.Name: ba04e_ba02-029.jpg
Notes:
68043969 At first, this fly looks like another Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) - but that's not right. It is (sparsely) bristly and has large "calypters" - those large white lobes at the base of the wing.

I'm sure this is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) in the order Diptera. Browsing at Buguide, I guess it is subfamily Phasiinae, possibly genus Cylindromyia. This one is working staminate flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

The adult flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: ba04f_ba01-080.jpg
Notes:
68043746 This is a real Horse Fly (genus Tabanus) in the family Tabanidae of the order Diptera. It's perched on dried algae on a rock outcrop by the river. It has interesting eyes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 October 2011)

There are many different kinds of horse fly - 108 taxa according to BugGuide! They have a painful bite. Yes, there is a horse trail nearby - but I don't know that they always go together.

File.Name: ba05a_ba03-034.jpg
Notes:
68048672 This is a large, satisfying acorn of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. This acorn will make some squirrel very happy! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

We have several different "scrub oaks" in our mountains. If you compare the acorn cup of this oak with my [Previous] photo, you can see the difference.

We had about 1-1/2 inches of rain today! It's a good start to the new rain year.

File.Name: ba05b_ba03-053.jpg
Notes:
68048628 A mandala of acorns...

These are new acorns of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are a sign of autumn and a good sign for all the local critters that depend on acorns for food. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

File.Name: ba05c_ba03-032.jpg
Notes: 68048545 Here is a different late-season Stephanomeria wildflower still going in the autumn woods. (See my [Previous] photo.) This is the native composite Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. (It might also be S. virgata, but this one is only about one foot tall.) This flower looks like it's wearing striped stockings on its styles! (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae; S. virgata
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria; Stephanomeria exigua; Asteraceae; S. virgata

File.Name: ba05d_ba03-021.jpg
Notes:
68048475 Here is another Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a new flower of the late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 October 2011)

The beautiful flowers close early in the day, by 10 or 11 unless it's foggy. I find them on road cuts in the mountains. Where did they grow before there were roads?
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria

File.Name: ba05e_ba02-020.jpg
Notes:
68048425 I thought all caterpillars (Lepidoptera) were done as we slide into autumn, but I found this one working what's left of a late-season flowering plant of Allophyllum glutinosum in the Polemoniaceae plant family. I don't know what it will become. (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

I just discovered that I have never posted a photo of a flower of Allophyllum glutinosum. (I'm shaking my head.) I'll make up for it tomorrow.

File.Name: ba05f_ba04-015.jpg
Notes:
68048261 This is a close shot of some of the spiderlings in my [Previous] photo. They are Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia longipalpis, but cp. P. viridens) in the family Oxyopidae. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2011)

Looking at all these spiderlings, it's interesting to think that every male and female couple have to produce just two offspring in their lifetime to replace them to maintain a stable population...

File.Name: ba06a_ba06-021.jpg
Notes:
68053234 These are winged "alate" Termites (order Isoptera) about to fly off into the unknown. They do this every year after our first winter rain, but I don't always find where they are coming from. These termites are emerging along the chip seal road in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2011)

I'm tired after doing Hike Club this afternoon with a bunch of middle school kids. Three photos will do just fine.

File.Name: ba06b_ba06-026.jpg
Notes:
68053173 This is an enormous Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. Well, it SEEMS enormous because it's just a fly. It's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. I found this one working the flowers of a pistillate Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2011)

It seems like the "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae!

I know this isn't the best photo. The fly was several feet away, this is a tight crop, and it was windy. But these large flies have been scarce this year, so I better show it while I can! See my photos last year for a better shot:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64666413
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64541286

File.Name: ba06c_ba06-006.jpg
Notes: 68052924 I guess this is a little "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family of the order Diptera. I apologize that the fly isn't in better focus, but the real interest is that relatively huge bright red Mite (Acari) hanging under its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 6 October 2011)

File.Name: ba07a_ba07-006.jpg
Notes:
68057056 Here are the first new flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae family. These are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear, almost perfectly timed with the first rain. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

We have a summer-dry and winter-wet Mediterranean climate here in central California. I'm still finding late-season flowers at this dry end of the rain year, but these pretty flowers mark the beginning of a new year. They are overlapping this year.

File.Name: ba07b_ba07-008.jpg
Notes:
68056947    "Birds do it, bees do it
   Even educated fleas do it
   Let's do it, let's fall in love..."
  (after Cole Porter of course)

These are calyptrate muscoid flies. I guess they are Cluster Flies in the genus Pollenia of the family Calliphoridae, but I'm not sure. They are our most common "house fly" in the mountains. Here they are exchanging genetic material to insure that there will be lots of little flies in the future! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

File.Name: ba07c_ba07-021.jpg
Notes:
68056817 This is a little Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) literally putting his head in the lion's mouth!

The lion in this case is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flowering shrub of composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). The spider twitched under my lens when the ant touched, but he didn't go for it. "Yuck - no more ants!" (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

File.Name: ba07d_ba07-013.jpg
Notes:
68056680 This is a beautiful little Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae wrapping its prey - maybe a tiny Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) insect in the order Homoptera. I got this photo today with the sun behind, with the exposure -1.3 EV stops. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

I don't understand that drop of liquid on its right pedipalp.

File.Name: ba07e_ba07-032.jpg
Notes: 68056560 This is an impressive Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae, with the afternoon sun behind. I'm pretty sure it's a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin, though I really don't understand the common name. It's munching some prey, but I can't make it out. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

File.Name: ba07f_ba06-010.jpg
Notes: 68056504 It seems like resurrection - this crusty summer-black Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) has already revived after our first winter rain just two days ago! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2011)

File.Name: ba08a_ba08-020.jpg
Notes:
68060973 I found this odd little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) guarding a female pistillate (seed-bearing) flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. Its front legs seem way too big for its body! (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011) I've been trying to post photos while Turner Classic Movies is showing "Gunga Din" (1939) on television. It was one of my Dad's favorite movies, after "The Quiet Man" (1952). I think I agree with his choices.

It was warmer today, and it's forecast to get hot next week. That's our autumn in a nutshell - it swings between hot and cold, and wet and dry.
Locality:
1952). I think I agree with his choices.

It was warmer today

Notes: 68060894 This little golden Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working a composite flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's an little-known insect on a little-known plant. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011)

File.Name: ba08c_ba07-018.jpg
Notes: 68060827 Here is another little Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae) in the order Diptera. Browsing at Buguide, I guess it is subfamily Phasiinae, possibly genus Cylindromyia. This one is perched on a female pistillate (seed-bearing) flower of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011)

File.Name: ba08d_ba08-001.jpg
Notes: 68060783 This is a goofy-looking Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. Maybe it's family Issidae, genus Neaethus? It was perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I've been seeing a lot of them - whenever I stop and look close. They have net-like wings and amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011)
