File.Name: ba08e_ba08-006.jpg
Notes:
68060728 Me and my shadow...

I believe this is the Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. Its abdomen is attached to its thorax by a slender petiole. It's the most common wasp by our house. I don't bother them, and they've never bothered me. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011)

File.Name: ba08f_b915-002.jpg
Notes:
68060630 This is a pretty little Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird in a native shrub of Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. They've been eating the berries - and also acting like flycatchers and eating the winged termites in the air after our first rain. (San Marcos Pass, 20 September 2011)

They stop here on their migration south to Mexico and Central America, and we see them again in the spring as they fly back north.

This isn't the best photo - I'm better with butterflies than birds! I'm posting because it might be the mystery bird that feather61 found in Guanaja, Honduras:
   http://www.fotolog.com/feather61/46736702
ScientificName: Dendroica townsendi; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Dendroica townsendi; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: ba09a_ba09-005.jpg
Notes:
68065394 This is a perfect Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) snuggling into a new flower of native Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family growing on the edge of our garden. This is a native plant that seems quite happy with summer water. It just keeps on making flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2011)

This was the first photo I got this morning, and its a keeper. The weather is turning warm again, and it's expected to get hot by mid-week. The local insects took a hit from the rain and cold last week.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: ba09b_ba09-011.jpg
Notes: 68065288 This is another pollen-dusted Yellow Jacket-type wasp (family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera) - but it's not a true yellow jacket. It's snuggling into a flower cluster of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. I'm not sure if this is the same wasp as my [Previous] photo or different. What do you think? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba09c_ba01-042.jpg
Notes:
68065250 "It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it!"

This is a Yellow Jacket-type wasp (family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera) - but it's not a true yellow jacket. It's trying to remove all the pollen its picked up from this native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba09d_ba09-022.jpg
Notes:
68065194 I'm the only person I know who get excited about finding a new fly in the woods - but I do!

This is an interesting fly (order Diptera) that I found today working late-summer flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. I don't know what it is, but those big red eyes and golden abdomen seem distinctive. It has patterned wings and closed wing-cells. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba09e_ba08-002.jpg
Notes:
68065072 "Only a fly, but one more is gone
Leaviní nobody to sing his sad song
Leaviní nobody to carry him home
Only a fly, but one more is gone"

(after Bob Dylan, "Only a Hobo")
http://www.mojvideo.com/video-bob-dylan-only-a-hobo/464c3179eee2bf7147e6

This is a dead fly (order Diptera) that I noticed on a Raspberry (Rubus idaeus and/or R. strigosus) leaf in the garden yesterday. Maybe it's a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family. I wonder what killed it? It's only a fly, but one more is gone. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2011)
ScientificName: Diptera; Rubus idaeus; R. strigosus; Anthomyiidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rubus idaeus; R. strigosus; Anthomyiidae

File.Name: ba09f_ba09-046.jpg
Notes:
68064904 So that's why they call them Cat-Faced Spiders!

This is an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae with a Yellow Jacket-type Wasp (family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera) prey. I'm pretty sure that the spider is a "Cat-Faced Spider" (Araneus gemmoides). Look close and you can see the cat face on the lower part of the spider's abdomen, between the front legs of the spider! I've always wondered about the name, and now I see it! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spiders; Orb-Weaver Spider; Yellow Jacket; Cat-Faced Spider

File.Name: ba10a_ba10-029.jpg
Notes:
68069980 This is a perfect Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found today on a flowering shrub of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). Maybe it is genus Phidippus? (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

I got photos straight down, and from the side - but I reckon this is the keeper. It's munching a breakfast fly, maybe a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera)? This spider is rather large for a jumping spider - a centimeter or so long.
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Coyote Bush; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Salticidae; Baccharis pilularis; Phidippus; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Baccharis pilularis; Phidippus; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: ba10b_ba10-022.jpg
Notes:
68069847 I guess this is butterfly number 8?

Here is a brilliant male California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly, resting on the ground. (I've been chasing it for a while!) The males are much more colorful than the females - compare my [Previous] photo. I haven't seen these butterflies for a while, and today I captured photos of both male and female. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

This is the official State Butterfly of California, but I reckon only lepidopterists know why! The real beauty of the male is the upper side of their wings, and they always fold their wings up when they rest. I'll keep trying to get that perfect photo. Here are a few past attempts:


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65588110
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/64875864
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52912484
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/52872460

File.Name: ba10c_ba10-020.jpg
Notes:
68069725 This female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly is totally "into" this flower of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum - aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. I don't think I've ever seen any butterfly working these classic Hummingbird flowers, but I guess her proboscis is long enough to reach the nectar. After a minute or two, I worried that she might be caught by a spider. I nudged her, and she flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

The female Dogface is a pale copy of the male - right down to a hint of a "figure 8" on its hind wing. See my [Next] photo for the male.
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: ba10d_ba10-001.jpg
Notes:
68069596 This is a beautiful Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly. I spotted it last night flying high around the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree off our upstairs deck. I noticed it again this morning and got the photo. It's a 500 pixel crop from my 4288 pixel original. I got it from about 20 feet away with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens. This butterfly looks like it's been around. I wonder where it's been? (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

These seem be mostly an autumn butterfly in our mountains. I've found them before in September, October, November, January, and (the oddball) in June.

File.Name: ba10e_ba10-011.jpg
Notes: 68069417 This a remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin, though there are other species. I've shown it before working flowers, but lately I've been seeing them flying close to the ground. They have amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

File.Name: ba10f_ba10-034.jpg
Notes:
68069332 This is a different Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera. It's really tiny - I thought it was an aphid at first. It's perched on a plant stem of Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. Once I noticed this one, I saw several others on the same shrub. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

Sara Allen Plummer Lemmon was one of the early naturalists who settled in Santa Barbara in the 1890s. It was Sara Lemmon who became the leader of the movement to have the California Poppy adopted as the California state flower.
CommonNames: Planthopper; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis

File.Name: ba11a_ba11-009.jpg
Notes:
68074370 Spiders aren't the only late-season predators in our mountains. This is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Maybe it's a Spiny Assassin Bug in the genus Sinea? You can see its impressive proboscis. It also has a large bright red Mite (Acari) hanging from its side as a hitchhiker or tick-like parasite. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

It was breezy and hot today - and we will have more of both tomorrow. I'm glad we had real rain a week ago to take the edge off the wildfire danger, but this is still fire weather.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak; Spiny Assassin Bug; Mite
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Sinea; Acari; fire weather
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Quercus agrifolia; Sinea; Acari; fire weather

File.Name: ba11b_ba11-014.jpg
Notes:
68074122 This is another Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides), an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - though you can't see the cat face here. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68064904

It has an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) for prey - in fact it had 4 Honey Bees all neatly wrapped. This is happening in a flowering shrub of native composite Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

It's definitely predator season in our mountains. All the summer insects that delight and bedevil us will soon disappear for winter, and the spiders and other predators will pick off what they can.
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spider; Orb-Weaver Spider; Honey Bee; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Araneus gemmoides; Araneidae; Apis mellifera; Baccharis pilularis
associatedTaxa: Araneus gemmoides; Araneidae; Apis mellifera; Baccharis pilularis

File.Name: ba11c_ba11-004.jpg
Notes:
68074037 I found this neat cluster of little dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushrooms coming up from a potted plant in the garden. Their caps are as thin as tissue paper and more fragile. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

I got this photo in deep shade. I had errands to do in town for a while. When I came back the pot was in the light, but the little 'shrooms were totally shriveled up. They have a short time - but the fungal mycelium lives on.

File.Name: ba11d_ba11-005.jpg
Notes: 68073932 Here are lots of Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) in the order Homoptera lined up like cattle at a feeding trough on the Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa) milkweed in the garden. I don't know how they find the garden milkweeds every year, but they do. They're pretty, so we leave them be. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

File.Name: ba11e_ba11-019.jpg
Notes: 68073854 This little Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working a composite flower of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. These pretty flowers are real survivors - still flowering at the end of our summer-long dry season. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

File.Name: ba11f_ba10-009.jpg
Notes:
68073769 These bright red ripe berries of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family look so good... but they have a horrible bitter taste. The plants have opposite leaves, with the uppermost pair fused distinctively at the base to surround the stem. It's a handsome plant that's having a good year in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2011)

Most web sites just repeat the claim that the berries are "edible but bitter" - isn't that an oxymoron? Even the birds seem to ignore them until there's nothing else.

File.Name: ba12a_ba12-001.jpg
Notes:
68079121 This is a beautiful native late-season flower of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Onagraceae plant family. The pollen grains are remarkable, and it looks like this one has been pollinated. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2011)

It's been a busy day. First I took Julie down to town early to catch a ride to San Clemente Island for a few days of restoration work. Then it was hot and windy at home. The wind died down just about when I had to drive to school to resin skimboards and attend a board meeting of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. I'm retired, but these are still busy days! I just had time for this one photo of a flower near our garden.

File.Name: ba12b_ba07-015.jpg
Notes:
68079096 Here is a little Convergent Lady Beetle - aka "ladybug" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. This one is on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the canyon by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2011)

These ladybugs aren't converging in the woods by the thousands yet, but this is the first one I've noticed in a while. Maybe it's a sign of a rave to come.

See my photo from 17 October 2009:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55640438
ScientificName: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Ribes amarum
associatedTaxa: Hippodamia convergens; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Ribes amarum

File.Name: ba12c_ba09-044.jpg
Notes:
68079065 This is another photo of the fierce Yellow Jacket Wasps (genus Vespula in the order Hymenoptera) that I found a while ago. See my previous photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67988505
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67715442

I always believed these wasps take over a gopher hole, but I believe they excavated this hole themselves. The soil around the hole has an organic look - like H.R. Giger's sets for the movie "Alien". There might be thousands of wasps in that underground nest, but they have never bothered me as I get my photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba12d_ba09-038.jpg
Notes: 68079024 These are interesting new flowers of the native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't know much about them, but here they are. It's another of the late-season composite flowers in our woods. There are lots of them! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba12e_ba09-040.jpg
Notes:
68079006 Sometimes a flower doesn't look like a flower!

These are new "flowers" of Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a composite related to sunflowers, though you have to study the flowers to see the relationship. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba12f_ba09-031.jpg
Notes: 68078991 Here is a new seed head of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It's just starting to get the feathery plumes on its seeds that give it its Spanish name barba de chivo, "Goat's Beard". This plant was traditionally used medicinally to treat sores, and maybe as a hair tonic. "If it grows such wonderful hairs on its seeds, it must work on my head!" (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2011)

File.Name: ba13a_ba13-004.jpg
Notes:
68083284 This tattered veteran is a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly perched on an equally tattered leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. This butterfly must have stories to tell! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2011)

It's was very hot today - 104 ∞F at the river, that's 40 ∞C! I took my my after-school Hike Club kids to the beach this afternoon. Yes, I'm tired and these three photos will do just fine today.

File.Name: ba13b_ba13-016.jpg
Notes:
68083224 This tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) is working the last flowers of Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2011)

Yes, the Monardella hypoleuca still has a few flowers - which is remarkable since that I found the first flowers on June 21. That's almost 4 months of flowers on these native plants!
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Coyote Mint; White-leaf Monardella
ScientificName: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca
associatedTaxa: Apidae; Hymenoptera; Monardella hypoleuca; Lamiaceae; Monardella hypoleuca

File.Name: ba13c_ba13-009.jpg
Notes:
68083164 I believe this is an unusual (though locally common) Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. It has a pretty random web, with a neat nest hanging in the middle of it like an upside down thimble. It decorates its nest with the bodies of its prey, and (oddly) its own shed skin. This is Viking stuff! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2011)

I found this spider outside its nest in a native flowering shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family.
CommonNames: Coneweb Spider; Desertshrub Spider; Coyote Bush

File.Name: ba14a_ba14-027.jpg
Notes:
68087678 After I got my [Previous] photo, the slender snake dived down into the muck at the bottom of this little frog pond cut-off from the main pond of the Santa Ynez River. After about 5 minutes, it came up for a breath - poking out of the muck. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

I like this photo! It shows what's underwater, what's out of the water, and reflections of what's above.

So what kind of snake is it? I first assumed it was a Garter Snake (genus Thamnophis) because it was swimming. I went through every snake in my "Check-list of Amphibians and Reptiles of Santa Barbara County, California" (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 1974) trying to match them at CaliforniaHerps.com, but no luck.

I'm better with Leps than Herps! The best photo-match I found is a Western Yellow-bellied Racer (Coluber constrictor mormon). It's not especially known as a water snake, but could that be it?
Locality: Coluber constrictor mormon). It's not especially known as a water snake
CommonNames: snake; Garter Snake; Western Yellow-bellied Racer
ScientificName: Thamnophis; Leps; Herps; Coluber constrictor mormon
associatedTaxa: Thamnophis; Leps; Herps; Coluber constrictor mormon

File.Name: ba14b_ba14-020.jpg
Notes:
68087614 I found this slender snake (suborder Serpentes of class Reptilia) swimming in a small frog pond cut-off from the main pond of the Santa Ynez River. (The river doesn't flow much at this time of year with two dams upstream.) Look at that bulge in its middle - I'm sure it's what's left of a frog! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

So what kind of snake is it? I first assumed it was a Garter Snake (genus Thamnophis) because it was swimming. I went through every snake in my "Check-list of Amphibians and Reptiles of Santa Barbara County, California" (Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History, 1974) trying to match them at CaliforniaHerps.com, but no luck.

I'm better with Leps than Herps! The best photo-match I found is a Western Yellow-bellied Racer (Coluber constrictor mormon). Could that be it?
Locality: Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History
CommonNames: snake; Garter Snake; Western Yellow-bellied Racer
ScientificName: Serpentes; Reptilia; Thamnophis; Leps; Herps; Coluber constrictor mormon
associatedTaxa: Serpentes; Reptilia; Thamnophis; Leps; Herps; Coluber constrictor mormon

File.Name: ba14c_ba14-034.jpg
Notes:
68087524
   "You get a line and I'll get a pole,
   We're all goin' down to the crawdad hole..."

Crayfish, crawfish, or crawdads - call them what you like - are fresh water decapod crustaceans like little lobsters. Tasty too!

I assumed this critter was alive and took photos of it under water. It never moved, and I finally picked it up. It still didn't move, so I placed it on the edge of the pond and got a photo. I don't know what's happening. I have no idea what species this is. The full-size photo is fabulous. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

File.Name: ba14d_ba14-010.jpg
Notes:
68087410 This is an impressive Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. I guess it's the Silver Argiope (Argiope argenata) spider with those impressive spikes on the back of its abdomen. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

I'm more interested in the prey that it's wrapping. I'm sure from the shape that this is a little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae of the order Hemiptera). I guess it's fitting that the predator becomes prey.
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Silver Argiope; Ambush Bug

File.Name: ba14e_ba14-037.jpg
Notes: 68087303 Here is a beautiful skinny Dragonfly (suborder Anisoptera, order Odonata) perched on a Cattail (family Typhaceae) leaf by the river today. It's holding its wings out pretty flat which confused me, but I'm pretty sure this is a Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae, probably the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica). It has a body like a damselfly. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)
CommonNames: Dragonfly; Cattail; Spreadwing Dragonfly; California Spreadwing
ScientificName: Anisoptera; Odonata; Typhaceae; Lestidae; Archilestes californica
associatedTaxa: Anisoptera; Odonata; Typhaceae; Lestidae; Archilestes californica

File.Name: ba14f_ba14-029.jpg
Notes:
68087190 This worn Lorquin's Admiral (Basilarchia lorquini) butterfly posed for me on a twig by the river today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

It looks a lot like the California Sister (Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly in my [Previous] photo, but the orange wing-tips go right to the edge of its forewings without a dark border. These butterflies usually stay near the willows and cottonwoods along a creek or river.
Locality: Adelpha bredowii ssp. californica) butterfly in my [Previous] photo

File.Name: ba15a_ba14-005.jpg
Notes:
68091157 "Tangled up and blue" - and pissed!

I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's tangled up in a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) web on a cactus plant in the garden. I didn't realize they have such impressive claw-like mandibles! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2011)

I was trying to decide if I should help it or not... wasp or spider?

I seem to remember I first got this question from the Italian movie Mondo cane (1962) that I saw in high school - a turtle is stuck on its back, should you flip it over? It's become a classic question, for example in the movie Bladerunner and even Futurama.

While I was pondering, the wasp got free.
Ethical problem solved!

I was five photos into today's batch when I realized they were all photos from our garden. That made it easy to pick the sixth photo.
Locality: 1962) that I saw in high school - a turtle is stuck on its back
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Linyphiidae; Mondo cane; Bladerunner; Futurama
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Linyphiidae; Mondo cane; Bladerunner; Futurama

File.Name: ba15b_ba15-001.jpg
Notes:
68090926 This is a handsome little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking for prey on a leaf in the garden this morning. You can't sneak up on these spiders - they have eyes everywhere! (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. Its a tight crop. The spider is about 700 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the spider is 23.6x700/4288 = 3.85 mm, a bit over 1/8 inch. I thought more like 3/16 inch, but foreshortening can explain that. It's tiny!

File.Name: ba15c_ba15-003.jpg
Notes:
68090897 It looks like the furnaces of hell!

This Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has captured a fly (order Diptera) - probably a Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae). It's perched on a late composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. A moment later it cast the husk of the fly away like trash. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; fly; Syrphid Flower Fly; Mexican Sunflower

File.Name: ba15d_ba15-027.jpg
Notes: 68090818 Look close! There's a little Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) stretched out on the middle petal of this healing flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the garden today. This is a female cricket. The noisy males have broader wings. I seem to find crickets everywhere hiding right now. They work the night shift and need to catch some sleep during the day. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)

File.Name: ba15e_ba15-005.jpg
Notes:
68090766 I found another Spreadwing Dragonfly in the family Lestidae of the order Odonata - probably the California Spreadwing (Archilestes californica). I found it in our garden perched on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. What amazing eyes it has, like precious stones. This interesting insect is like a cross between a real dragonfly and a damselfly. It holds its wings out, but a bit to the side. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)

By chance, I happened to photograph one of these at the river yesterday. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68087303
ScientificName: Lestidae; Odonata; Archilestes californica; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Lestidae; Odonata; Archilestes californica; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: ba15f_ba13-020.jpg
Notes: 68090675 Here is a large Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - the King. It's working late flowers of well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden. (The shrub seems to be catching its second wind!) I got this photo with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens from our shaky upstairs deck. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2011)

File.Name: ba16a_ba16-021.jpg
Notes:
68095786 This is a cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla) by the frog pond near our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch. Or is it a Baja California Treefrog (Pseudacris hypochondriaca)? I don't really care. The frog pond is dry for summer, but there is a little seep that keeps this one part damp. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

I tried to get a photo of the one green tree frog I saw, but it disappeared in the green grass. These brown frogs (same species) were easier to find. Isn't that how natural selection works?

About our old cabin - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59854701

I happened to photograph a frog, an insect, a spider, a flower, a mushroom, and (bending the rules) the wind! Not a bad haul of photos today!
Locality:
same species) were easier to find. Isn't that how natural selection works?

About our old cabin - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59854701

I happened to photograph a frog
CommonNames: Pacific Tree Frog; Baja California Treefrog

File.Name: ba16b_ba16-039.jpg
Notes: 68095718 Here is an impressive adult Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's antennae go on for quite a ways out of the frame. I flushed this one out of the grass, and it landed in a nearby Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

File.Name: ba16c_ba16-045.jpg
Notes:
68095682 Wow - this Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides or kin) has captured an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

If you look close above the bee's wing, you can see what I believe is a tiny Freeloader Fly (Milichiidae) of the order Diptera. These tiny flies act as kleptoparasites, feeding on bodily fluids of the prey. It's unknown whether they just lick the prey or actually penetrate it.

The Wikipedia page about these flies is interesting:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Milichiidae
Locality: Milichiidae) of the order Diptera. These tiny flies act as kleptoparasites
CommonNames: Cat-Faced Spider; Honey Bee; Freeloader Fly
ScientificName: Araneus gemmoides; Apis mellifera; Milichiidae; Diptera; kleptoparasites
associatedTaxa: Araneus gemmoides; Apis mellifera; Milichiidae; Diptera; kleptoparasites

File.Name: ba16d_ba16-026.jpg
Notes: 68095527 I found this surprising wildflower of native Spanish Clover (Lotus purshianusFabaceae plant family growing at the summer-dry frog pond near our old cabin by the Kinevan apple orchard today. It's surprising because these flowers mostly finished two months ago. There's a little seep here with a bit of summer water for flowers - and frogs! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

File.Name: ba16e_ba16-042.jpg
Notes:
68095348 I found these baseball-size "Giant Puffball" (genus Calvatia) mushrooms today in the old Kinevan apple orchard. Maybe they are Calvatia formosa? I split one open so you can see the yellowish spore mass inside. Maybe edible or maybe bitter-tasting. Want to try it? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

They are growing on bare ground with old plants of Dove Weed or Turkey Mullein (Eremocarpus setigerus) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see a few green seedlings that have sprouted after our rain two weeks ago!
Locality: Eremocarpus setigerus) in the locally uncommon Euphorbiaceae plant family. If you look close
CommonNames: Giant Puffball; Dove Weed; Turkey Mullein
ScientificName: Calvatia; Calvatia formosa; Eremocarpus setigerus; Euphorbiaceae
associatedTaxa: Calvatia; Calvatia formosa; Eremocarpus setigerus; Euphorbiaceae

File.Name: ba16f_ba15-014.jpg
Notes:
68095194 You can't photograph the wind unless you photograph something in the wind.

This is bad grass! These are flowering stems of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) bent over from the wind yesterday. It is pretty, especially in the wind, but...

I believe this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. It grows along roads like other weeds, but it's also getting established in the woods and along the river. I've seen it get worse over the years. It's deceptive, because it really does look like a native grass if you don't look too close. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)

Kill it if you see it. Don't let it get established!

File.Name: ba17a_ba16-009.jpg
Notes:
68100149 Here is the remarkable nymph of a Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. You can see its impressive proboscis folded under its head. It seems late in the year to find this nymph, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2011)

I got home late tonight, so three photos will do just fine.

File.Name: ba17b_ba16-012.jpg
Notes: 68100112 This is a perfect little golden-faced Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). It's taking pollen from a late composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. Its proboscis looks like an elephant's trunk! (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2011)

File.Name: ba17c_ba16-025.jpg
Notes:
68100034 Here is another photo of the same little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla - or is it P. hypochondriaca?) as my [Previous] photo. This is by the summer-dry frog pond near our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

It was funny - there was a green treefrog, but I lost it in the grass and missed the photo. This tan frog was easy to find in the grass, but I never would have found it on the sand if I hadn't been tracking it. I believe these frogs are slow color-shifters. They can pick a color to match their surroundings.

File.Name: ba18a_ba18-050.jpg
Notes:
68104603 This is a large Monarch Butterfly (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) - the King. It's working late flowers of a Salvia in the Lamiaceae plant family in our garden today. I think it might be "Salvia 'Indigo Spears'". It's a deep blue salvia hybrid that blooms all summer for us. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

You might think Monarchs would be common here, since tens of thousands of them overwinter a dozen miles away in trees by the coast... but they are actually scarce. They are having a good year. I see one or three every day, but maybe the same ones. Oddly, they show no interest in our milkweeds.
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Salvia; Lamiaceae; Salvia
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Salvia; Lamiaceae; Salvia

File.Name: ba18b_ba18-032.jpg
Notes:
68104548 This is another little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking for prey on a leaf in the garden this afternoon. Julie looked at this photo and said "It looks like a cute puppy dog with 8 eyes!" (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

Six eyes are easy to see, but it has small eyes on the side between the medium-sized eyes - the posterior median eyes (PME). Maybe the function of these small eyes is unknown? See the often-quoted image by David Hill:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Jumping_spider_vision_David_Hill.png

They see very well - jumping spiders often make eye contact with me, but not this one. They have simple eyes, not compound like insects, so they must have highly developed retinas behind those lenses. See Wikipedia at:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jumping_spider

I have another photo of (I think) the same kind of spider at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68090926
Locality:

File.Name: ba18c_ba18-006.jpg
Notes:
68104424 I believe this is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) that I found perched on my trail today. They seem to wander quite a ways from water. Sure, this is over-exposed and cropped too-tight - but it's still a keeper. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

I've found these dragonflies several times in mid-winter, hanging onto plants in our canyon. See my photo (and follow the links for more) at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66052982

File.Name: ba18d_ba18-022.jpg
Notes: 68104258 I first found this pretty little caterpillar munching a late-season flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I got this photo about an half-hour later. It had finished munching and was just hanging - maybe inspecting its work? I'm not sure if it's a butterfly or moth, but it is definitely a Lepidoptera. It seems late in the season for caterpillars! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

File.Name: ba18e_ba17-003.jpg
Notes:
68104197 This is the same large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I've shown before on a composite flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68090897

It decided to move today - just a few feet to a nearby Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus, though it had to "cast a line" to manage it. Here it is on the cactus. What next? (I guess it's ready to lay eggs.) (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2011)
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Mexican Sunflower; Prickly Pear

File.Name: ba18f_ba16-007.jpg
Notes:
68104108 Here is another photo of the remarkable nymph of an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera that I showed in my [Previous] photo. It's resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. It has amazing colors and texture - like candy. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2011)

Note the cute little "training wings". I reckon it has just one more molt to go until it "comes out" as an adult.

File.Name: ba19a_ba14-014.jpg
Notes:
68108886 This is a pollen-dusted Yellow Jacket-type wasp (family Vespidae in the order Hymenoptera) - but it's not a true yellow jacket. It's working a flower cluster of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 14 October 2011)

I have a photo of probably the same kind of wasp at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68065288

I took my skimboard class to the beach today to try out their boards. We had some problems with the resin last week in the 104 ∞F heat, but everything worked fine!

I heard an uncommon little Northern Saw-whet Owl in the woods tonight. I haven't heard it since last spring. The seasons are definitely changing!
CommonNames: Yellow Jacket; Scale Broom; Northern Saw-whet Owl

File.Name: ba19b_ba16-004.jpg
Notes:
68108807 "Just a fly" - but what perfect form and structure!

Look close, and you can see that this fly is grooming its long proboscis between its front legs. I believe it is a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family of the order Diptera. It's perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) on the wild edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

File.Name: ba19c_ba15-007.jpg
Notes:
68108727 "Just a fly" - but what perfect form and structure! I'm sure there's a name for every hair on its body.

Maybe it's a Flesh Fly (family Sarcophaga) in the order Diptera. I found it perched on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 15 October 2011)

File.Name: ba19d_ba18-027.jpg
Notes:
68108671 Here is a beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working staminate (pollen-bearing) flowers of composite flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

As I recall from previous years, the Coyote Bush usually all flowers at once. But this year different shrubs are flowering at different times. It's a boon for us photographers.
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Coyote Bush

File.Name: ba19e_ba16-018.jpg
Notes: 68108549 These are the strange "flowers" of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) in the Viscaceae plant family. It's growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. KIds - bits of mistletoe with berries bring a premium price at Christmas time! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2011)

associatedTaxa: Phoradendron villosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ba19f_ba18-013.jpg
Notes: 68108400 My part of the world is not known for fall colors, but we have some plants that oblige. These are red autumn leaves of Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Enjoy the color - don't touch! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

File.Name: ba20a_ba20-049.jpg
Notes:
68113253 I believe this is another Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata). I found it perched on a California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family by our mailbox, with the white and brown flowers behind. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2011)

I found another on of these dragonflies in the woods a couple of days ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68104424

These are our winter dragonflies. See my photo (and follow the links for more) at:

I had a hard time picking a lead photo today - they are all interesting! I like the background of this one, like an oil painting.

File.Name: ba20b_ba20-034.jpg
Notes: 68113197 This is a magical opening flower of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family. These new flowers make perfect mandalas as they unwind. It's interesting that they start yellow and turn pure white when they open. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2011)

File.Name: ba20c_ba20-031.jpg
Notes:
68113143 Here are magical flowers of Toloache (Datura wrightii) in the Solanaceae plant family in a dry field by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2011)

The plant is related to the infamous Jimson Weed (D. stramonium) that grows across the country - but it's different. Current opinion is that it's not really native, but was introduced from Mexico in Pre-Colombian times by Native Americans for ceremonial purposes. It's a common weed locally, but it's dangerous unless you get "Don Juan" himself to guide you. Be careful little wing!

The flower has been an artists subject many times, notably in the paintings of Georgia O'Keeffe. Try a web search on "georgia o'keeffe jimson weed":
   http://www.google.com/search?q=georgia+o%27keeffe+jimson+weed
Locality: D. stramonium) that grows across the country - but it's different. Current opinion is that it's not really native

File.Name: ba20d_ba20-002.jpg
Notes:
68112940 Here is a wonderful Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) hiding under the curled leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. What a wonderful color its wings have. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2011)

This is a male cricket - the noisy ones. The females have narrower wings. I seem to find crickets everywhere hiding right now. They work the night shift and need to catch some sleep during the day - like most working musicians I know.
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Cricket; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ba20e_ba20-054.jpg
Notes:
68112820 This is a small Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) working a female pistillate (seed-bearing) flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. The ants don't seem to be munching the flowers. I guess they are getting nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 20 October 2011)

You can see that this native ant has one dorsal bump on its petiole. I don't know what kind of ant it is.

File.Name: ba20f_ba20-020.jpg
Notes:
68112740 It was too windy along the river for photos today, but I tried. This is an impressive Wasp in the order Hymenoptera - maybe a Thread-Waisted Wasp (family Sphecidae)? It was moving to much in the wind for me to get sharp focus. But look closer!

This wasp has a hitchhiker! Look on the wasp's left hind leg - screen-right - near my insert. It looks a bit like an ant, but I blew up the cropped image and I can clearly make out a long proboscis folded under its head. I'm sure this is a tiny ant-mimic "true bug" (order Hemiptera) that's getting a free ride on the wasp. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2011)

8+ years of finding photos in the woods for fotolog - and I still regularly get surprised!

File.Name: ba21a_ba21-015.jpg
Notes: 68117528 These are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. They're real nuts, with a soft covering - though they are small, only pea-size. They taste pretty good, maybe a bit bitter. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011) Now look closer! There's a little brown Crab Spider (Thomisidae) sitting on the left nut. I wonder what he hopes to catch sitting there?

File.Name: ba21b_ba21-023.jpg
Notes:
68117483 New mushrooms in the woods!

I believe this is a large white Hygrophorus sordidus mushroom. I found it growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees along our driveway today. It's a hefty mushroom that is edible but not choice. The new mushroom year has begun! Just in time - insects are getting scarce. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011)

I found this in the woods last year on October 18:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65148272

File.Name: ba21c_ba21-026.jpg
Notes:
68117417 New mushrooms in the woods!

I believe this is Russula subnigricans or kin. It's quite large, 6-8 inches broad. It has Russula vibes - and the stem snaps like chalk, a sure sign of this genus. It develops reddish stains when bruised, and it's not beautiful like so many mushrooms are. I found these growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees along our driveway today. It's not particularly poisonous (and tastes mild), but it's not very good either. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011)

According to my book, this mushroom is a chantrelle-indicator - and we found lots of delicious Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) in the same place last year. (My book is: David Arora, Mushrooms Demystified, 1986)

We had our first real rain a couple of weeks ago, and the mushrooms are starting. Everything that happens next in the woods depends on the weather - wet or dry?

File.Name: ba21d_ba20-018.jpg
Notes:
68117223 This is a handsome Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found on a flower cluster of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry wash by the river yesterday. Maybe it is genus Phidippus - possibly the Cardinal Jumper (Phidippus cardinalis)? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 20 October 2011)

It was windy. I got this photo while I was holding the branch steady with my left hand. Not too bad!
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Scale Broom; Cardinal Jumper

File.Name: ba21e_ba21-018.jpg
Notes:
68117113 Here is a different large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. I'm not sure what it is, but it is not a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) which are so common in the woods right now. It's darker and more dangerous looking. This one is sitting in a seed head of non-native Fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) in the Apiaceae plant family by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011)

Fennel is an invasive weed in our mountains, but we always leave a few for the butterflies. We try to remember to cut the seed heads so they don't come up everywhere, but it's almost too late to trim this one.
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Foeniculum vulgare; Apiaceae

File.Name: ba21f_ba18-051.jpg
Notes: 68116980 These are spider webs stretching from our roof to the oak trees about 15-20 feet away. I got this photo from the upstairs porch when the light was just right. You can see a few webs in the tree. The spiders must wait for the wind to pull out their silk webs that far. Julie called them "spider zip-lines" which sounds good to me! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

File.Name: ba22a_ba22-005.jpg
Notes:
68122173 As a gentleman with a beard, I appreciate whiskers on other critters - especially if they are bright orange!

This is a remarkable Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera). It looks a bit like the Blue Bottle Fly, but that's a trash fly, and I found this critter in the woods.

I believe this fly is the native "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). It is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This one is working the flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

I've found this fly before. For interesting info, see my comments at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67947873

It was windy today, but this one flowering Coyote Bush was somewhat out of the wind, so I spent some time with it to see what I could find. There were lots of interesting flies!
CommonNames: Blow Fly; Blue Bottle Fly; Secondary Screwworm; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Calliphoridae; Diptera; Cochliomyia macellaria; Calliphoridae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: ba22b_ba22-026.jpg
Notes:
68121233 I found this impressive fly (order Diptera) with bristles and a white abdomen working male staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. I suspect it is a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

If it is a Tachinid Fly, the adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: ba22c_ba22-013.jpg
Notes:
68121146 This is a handsome little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working male staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

As familiar as flies are, you can see that my flies today are very different from each other.

File.Name: ba22d_ba22-024.jpg
Notes:
68121113 This is a furry bee-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working male staminate (pollen bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

It was windy today, and these flies are very camera-shy. I couldn't get a shot that shows the fly's face, but you can see that it's different from the fly in my [Previous] photo. We have lots of these Syrphid flies!

File.Name: ba22e_ba21-034.jpg
Notes: 68121042 Here is a beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) getting pollen from a flower of Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden. Look close - you can see pollen grains on its proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011)

File.Name: ba22f_ba02-011.jpg
Notes: 68120887 Remember that scene from "The Exorcist" (1973) when actress Linda Blair rotates her head all the way around? This fly is doing something similar! It's a little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a leaf in the garden. It's grooming itself. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2011)

File.Name: ba23a_ba23-022.jpg
Notes:
68124952 This is a tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working a flower of horticultural Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden. It's dusted with pollen, but you can see that it is more interested in the nectar in the flower cup. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2011)

*Sigh* I just got word last night that Donna, the Dunn Middle School science teacher, took a fall off her steps and broke her hip. I've been asked to substitute for her. I'm recently retired, and out of the habit of getting up in the dark. Naturally, I got up this morning and started to round up my old notes.

Then I got word this afternoon that they only need me from Wednesday - Friday this week. A reprieve! I went out for some new photos, but it was breezy. I got a few.

Between anxiety about teaching tomorrow and a fire in the mountains, three photos will do just fine tonight!

File.Name: ba23b_ba23-018.jpg
Notes: 68124837 Here is another immature Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. They have fabulous "candy colors" - anything but what you would expect from a slow-moving predator! You can see its impressive proboscis folded under its head. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2011)

File.Name: ba23c_ba23-030.jpg
Notes:
68124773 We had some excitement in the woods this afternoon!

I heard fire trucks drive by, then I saw a fire bomber join the fray. We have no view from our house in the canyon, but I suspected the worst. Finally we saw smoke, so Julie and I drove up the road into the mountains to get a view.

It turned out to be a small fire near Arroyo Burro Pass near a semi-official shooting range. There have been several fires here over the years. The forest service tried to shut it down last year, but people went there anyway, and they finally reopened the gates this summer. This is the result. This place has burned so many times before, I don't think it was too bad.

This is a large helicopter doing a water drop. It might well be a Sikorsky S-64 Skycrane. There were several fire crews on the scene. Maybe in a slow wildfire year, they are looking for some action?
