File.Name: ba24a_ba24-022.jpg
Notes:
68129313 A pretty mandala of flower and cricket...

This is an odd Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) feeding on a flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2011)

The cricket is odd because it's feeding on pollen from the flower. Usually these crickets are active at night and rest during the day.

It's time to cut back to three photos a day, at least for this week when I'll be busy teaching at Dunn Middle School.

Also I was busy half-watching a really spooky movie on Turner Classic Movies - "Carnival of Souls" (1962), apparently made for $33,000. Highly recommended. It's public domain. You can download it from the Internet Archive at:
   http://www.archive.org/details/CarnivalofSouls

File.Name: ba24b_ba24-002.jpg
Notes: 68129188 I found this odd caterpillar on a spent Sedum succulent in the garden this morning. From my book on caterpillars, I believe it is a Hairstreak (subfamily Theclinea) or Blue (subfamily Polyommatinae). The real question is: what's it doing active now, so close to winter? (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2011)

File.Name: ba24c_ba23-024.jpg
Notes:
68128998 This is a tiny Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera). It's been working a flower of horticultural Yarrow (Achillea) in the garden - looking for nectar, not pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2011)

I got this photo a few moments after my [Previous] photo. Now the bee is trying to clean off its remarkably complicated proboscis.

"It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it.

File.Name: ba25a_ba24-003.jpg
Notes: 68133446 These are Wild Turkeys (Meleagris gallopavo) under the oaks at Paradise down by the river. They were introduced from the East Coast many years ago, and now they are well-established. Note the new grass after our early October rain! Look - it's eating at the left end and pooping tomato-like "frass" at the other end. I guess this proves that the tomato really is organic! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2011)

File.Name: ba25b_ba25-005.jpg
Notes:
68133263 We found this caterpillar in our dinner tomato last night. Too bad - it's a fine "heritage tomato" that we had plans for, but this critter got first dibs. (I used flash for this photo.)

Look - it's eating at the left end and pooping tomato-like "frass" at the other end. I guess this proves that the tomato really is organic! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2011)

File.Name: ba25c_ba25-006.jpg
Notes:
68133163 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera near the river yesterday. They nest under our eaves at home. I don't know where they nest here by the river.

It's working flowers of a native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. These flowers are finally winding down for the year, but there still are a few. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2011)

There's nothing like a cloudy day to bring out the detail of these flowers!
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: ba26a_ba26-013.jpg
Notes:
68137843 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly resting on an old seed head of Cheat Grass in the field at school today. It was really interested in the non-native Australian Saltbush (Atriplex semibaccata) growing nearby. It was windy, but there's a cushion of air close to the ground where the wind isn't so bad. (Dunn School, Santa Ynez Valley, 26 October 2011)

This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. It looks beautiful up close like this, but if you don't know to look close, it looks very plain - if you notice it at all.

*Sigh* I put in a full day teaching at Dunn Middle School today as a substitute for Donna, who broke her hip in a fall. It is fun, but I'm tired! I remember why I was ready to retire after 28 years!
CommonNames: Western Pygmy Blue; Cheat Grass; Australian Saltbush

File.Name: ba26b_ba22-014.jpg
Notes: 68137680 This is a handsome Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly working new staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. That patch of blue on its forewing is so beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

File.Name: ba26b_ba24-018.jpg
Notes: 68137585 Here is a sweet little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) climbing over the mountainous terrain of a flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the garden. You can see the "spurious vein" on its upper wing if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2011)

File.Name: ba27a_ba27-002.jpg
Notes:
68141882 This is a handsome Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis) butterfly working flowers of some kind of garden Daisy in the Asteraceae plant family. (Or maybe it's the White Checkered-Skipper (P. albescens) - they are very similar.) I got this photo in the school garden today between classes. These butterflies mostly fly close to the ground. (Dunn School, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 October 2011)

I have found this butterfly at home before, but it's not common. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/38916120

One good thing about substitute-teaching this week at Dunn Middle School is finding new butterflies in the lowlands. Yes, I am having great fun with the kids, once I get over the shock of getting up in the dark!

File.Name: ba27b_ba22-009.jpg
Notes:
68141697 This is a perfect Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working staminate (pollen-bearing) flowers of composite flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

These butterflies have a way of striking perfect poses for the camera, and they let me get close.

I also clearly saw an uncommon Fatal Metalmark (Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly on the same plant, but I couldn't get the photo. I'll try again this weekend.
Locality: Calephelis nemesis californica) butterfly on the same plant
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Coyote Bush; Fatal Metalmark
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Calephelis nemesis californica

File.Name: ba27c_ba22-002.jpg
Notes: 68141629 Here is a fallen oak leaf caught in a spider web in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2011)

File.Name: ba28a_ba28-007.jpg
Notes:
68145858 This is an immature "true bug" in the order Hemiptera on a plant of native Horseweed (Conyza canadensis. It's a weedy-looking composite in the Asteraceae plant family that's still flowering after most native flowers are done for the year. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2011)

The bug looks like a Harlequi Bug (Murgantia histrionica) in the family Pentatomidae, though it only seems to have 4 antenna segments instead of the expected 5. But so do most of the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/247819/bgpage
It might be that the immatures are different.

I'm finished with my classroom stint as a substitute science teacher, at least for a while. It would be nice to get out tomorrow for more photos, but our local Natural History Society is holding the annual Plant Sale, and I agreed to work. Maybe I'll get some photos at the sale!
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Conyza canadensis; Asteraceae; Murgantia histrionica; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Conyza canadensis; Asteraceae; Murgantia histrionica; Pentatomidae

File.Name: ba28b_ba28-015.jpg
Notes:
68145677 A cricket rendezvous?

Here are male and female Western Tree Crickets (Oecanthus californicus or kin) in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera on a spent flower of Purple Coneflower (Echinaceae purpurea) in the garden. That's the male on the flower with wide wings, and the female stretched out on the petal below with narrow wings. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2011)

They were originally both stretched out on nearby petals of the flower. The male woke up and started moving around as the sunlight was failing.

Crickets sleep all day and work the night shift. They are the songsters of our woods in the fall. I believe these brown crickets make a steady trill, unlike the Snowy Tree Crickets (also genus Oecanthus) which pulse.
CommonNames: Western Tree Crickets; Purple Coneflower; Snowy Tree Crickets
ScientificName: Oecanthus californicus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Echinaceae purpurea; Oecanthus
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus californicus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Echinaceae purpurea; Oecanthus

File.Name: ba28c_ba28-002.jpg
Notes:
68145588 These are beautiful ripe berries of Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or S. albus ssp. Laevigatus) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. There's some confusion about the name because our plants seem to be intermediate in height between the species. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2011)

Some websites report that the white berries are edible, but I have to assume that the authors never tasted them! They taste terrible. Even the birds seem to leave them on the plants. There are reports of Chumash native Americans using the stems for baskets.
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis or S. albus; Laevigatus; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis or S. albus; Laevigatus; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: ba29a_ba29-008.jpg
Notes:
68150283 This hummingbird is taking it easy while working this flower!

I believe this is an immature male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2011)

Light is getting scarce at home, and I had to look up this morning for a photo-op. Then I went off to work at the annual plant sale of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society. It went well.

It's been a busy week. Tomorrow should be a stay-at-home photo day.
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; up
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; up

File.Name: ba29b_ba29-011.jpg
Notes: 68150218 Here is a cluster of new native glandular flowers of Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family catching the first light of day - at about 10:30 am! We're losing the sun at our house on the north slopes of the East-West trending Santa Ynez Mountains. These are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear. Hummingbirds like them. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2011)

File.Name: ba29c_ba29-017.jpg
Notes:
68150143 These are beautiful tubular flowers of Tree Tobacco (Nicotiana glauca) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's a common weedy shrub introduced into California from South America back in Spanish days. This particular shrub near the entrance of Ron and Dianne's Manzanita Nursery is especially beautiful. (Manzanita Nursery, Santa Ynez Valley, 29 October 2011)

Tree Tobacco is real tobacco, but there are rumors on the web that it doesn't contain nicotine, but anabasine which is something similar. There is a market for it on the "Legal Highs" web sites, and it's being promoted as a smoking herb to help quit smoking. Good, pick them all! But please leave this one plant at Manzanita Nursery that is so beautiful!
ScientificName: Nicotiana glauca; Solanaceae; Manzanita Nursery; anabasine
associatedTaxa: Nicotiana glauca; Solanaceae; Manzanita Nursery; anabasine

File.Name: ba30a_ba30-023.jpg
Notes:
68154081 Spider love - or spider prey?
I suspect it's spider love.

Here are a pair of Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) in the woods today. They are deep in a clump of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) along the road in the canyon. I tried to brush the *darn* grass stems out of my photo, but this is the best I could get before they disappeared. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

I'm sure they are genus Phidippus - possibly the Cardinal Jumper (Phidippus cardinalis). The lower spider is quite a bit larger than the upper one.

I've photographed something similar before:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/50126997

It was great to get out in the woods with my camera today - it's been almost a week!
CommonNames: Jumping Spiders; Smilo Grass; Cardinal Jumper
ScientificName: Salticidae; Piptatherum miliaceum; Phidippus; Phidippus cardinalis
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Piptatherum miliaceum; Phidippus; Phidippus cardinalis

File.Name: ba30b_ba30-036.jpg
Notes:
68153973 Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms!

I found this early "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom in the woods today. It has impressive bulk - the cap is about 8 inches (20 cm) across! These bolete mushroom have a spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills. It doesn't look very appetizing - in fact it's quite poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

These mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle then, but they are not that other notorious blue-staining mushroom. It's fun to leave messages for other mushroom collectors to find!

It's interesting that all the early mushrooms in the woods after our early rain on October 5 are LARGE mushrooms. See my recent photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68117417
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68117483
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68095348

File.Name: ba30c_ba30-044.jpg
Notes: 68153826 I found this small brown "Rough Stink bug" - probably in the genus Brochymena of the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera in the woods today. There are many different kinds. This one is perched on a branch of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). It seems an odd time of year to find plant-feeders in the woods, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)
ScientificName: Rough Stink bug; Brochymena; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus
associatedTaxa: Rough Stink bug; Brochymena; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Rubus ursinus

File.Name: ba30d_ba30-030.jpg
Notes:
68153699 This is a ripe seed pod of Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family in the canyon. It's a little bucket full of precious seeds! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

I have lots of photos of the amazing summer flowers:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67646334
ScientificName: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae
associatedTaxa: Calochortus weedii; vestus; C. fimbriata; Liliaceae

File.Name: ba30e_ba30-029.jpg
Notes: 68153628 These are beautiful late-season flowers of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. Sometimes they keep flowering until spring. All parts of the flower are here: buds, new flowers, open flowers, spent flowers, and a seed head. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

File.Name: ba30f_ba30-005.jpg
Notes:
68153522 Like little birds leaving the nest...

I've posted so many photos of insects on native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family lately that I should finish the cycle. These are the ripe seeds of Coyote Bush, blowing in the wind. The shrubs are all done in our canyon, but they may still be going elsewhere in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

File.Name: ba31a_ba31-049.jpg
Notes:
68158626 This is the beautiful web of a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides or kin), with the spider in the center. You can see some engineering detail in this photo. The web really is a spiral, and you can see where it starts - like the golden brick road in the Wizard of Oz! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2011)

Today's photos are all *spiders* in honor of Halloween. I don't find spiders creepy at all, though I hate when I walk through a large spider web at night - WHERE'S THE SPIDER?!

File.Name: ba31b_ba14-002.jpg
Notes:
68158598 I think this is a truly frightening photo for Halloween.

This is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's tangled up in (I think) the disorganized web of a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) web on a cactus plant in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2011)

I was getting photos of the wasp when the spider ran out out to see what it had! I was startled looking through my macro lens, and the focus slipped. But it's still a gripping photo, looking spider-death in the eye.

This story has a happy ending - for the wasp, but not the spider. The spider took a quick look and went away. After a few minutes, the wasp escaped.

I have another photo of the same wasp at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68091157

File.Name: ba31c_ba30-032.jpg
Notes: 68158521 This is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a finished flower of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon. I wonder what insect it hopes will visit these spent flowers? (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

File.Name: ba31d_ba04-015.jpg
Notes:
68158481 Spider fear? Here are lots of tiny spiderlings for Halloween!

These are lots of young Green Lynx Spiders (Peucetia longipalpis, but cp. P. viridens) in the family Oxyopidae. I got this photo a few weeks ago, just after our first rain of the new rain year, when the little spiders were active. (San Marcos Pass, 4 October 2011)

File.Name: ba31e_ba18-034.jpg
Notes:
68158402 This is a Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking for prey on a leaf in the garden. It's mostly looking off to the left, but it's also looking right at me with its "anterior lateral eye" (ALE). You can't sneak up on these little spiders! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2011)

These spiders have simple eyes, not compound eyes like insects, so they must have highly developed retinas behind those lenses.

I have another photo of the same spider at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68104548

File.Name: ba31f_ba30-020.jpg
Notes:
68158285 This is another photo of the Jumping Spiders (Salticidae) in the woods yesterday. The photo isn't sharp, but look for the eyes at the bottom. You can tell that it's two spiders, entangled. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

I believe it's spider love, and that the spiders are genus Phidippus - possibly the Cardinal Jumper (Phidippus cardinalis). The top spider is quite a bit larger than the one at the bottom. See my [Previous] photo.

When I first saw them, I thought "Oh my god, a jumping spider has another as prey!" But I think it's love.

They are deep in a clump of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) along the road in the canyon. I tried to brush the *darn* grass stems out of my photo, but this is the best I could get before they disappeared.
Locality: Piptatherum miliaceum) along the road in the canyon. I tried to brush the *darn* grass stems out of my photo
CommonNames: Jumping Spiders; Cardinal Jumper; Smilo Grass
ScientificName: Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus cardinalis; Piptatherum miliaceum
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Phidippus; Phidippus cardinalis; Piptatherum miliaceum

File.Name: bb01a_bb01-014.jpg
Notes:
68162920 Finally, I got a clear photo of this enormous Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)

Well, it SEEMS enormous because after all it's just a fly. It's about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. I found this one working the late flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river today. These impressive flies have been scarce this year.

It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae. See my [Next] photo tomorrow.

Today's photos are all interesting flies.
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Arctiidae
associatedTaxa: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Arctiidae

File.Name: bb01b_bb01-016.jpg
Notes:
68162874 This is an bristly Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) working an almost finished flower cluster of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub by the river today. Beautiful flies on beautiful flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)

This might be the same kind of fly as my [Previous] photo, but I'm not sure. There are differences in the flies' face.

Adult Tachinid Flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: bb01c_ba31-028.jpg
Notes:
68162830 Here I am looking up at the amazing face of a Tachinid Fly - family Tachinidae in the order Diptera - with a good view of its impressive proboscis. This fly is working flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - a few of which are still going in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2011)

Adult Tachinid Flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: bb01d_bb01-042.jpg
Notes:
68162589 This another remarkable wasp-mimic Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - maybe Spilomyia interrupta or kin. This fly seemed a bit smaller than the flies at home. They have such amazing patterned eyes! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)

This one is working late flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. The flowers are finally winding down for the year, but there still are a few. I'm sorry to see them go.
Locality: Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub. The flowers are finally winding down for the year
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Spilomyia interrupta; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: bb01e_ba30-014.jpg
Notes:
68162530 This is another photo of the same fly (order Diptera) in my [Previous] photo. You can see the six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes. Of course, it's a tight crop. If you've ever tried to swat a fly, you know how well the compound eyes work! (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

Nano - I can't usually resolve a fly's simple eyes, but sometimes I get them. I'd rather resolve the fly's remarkable face!

File.Name: bb01f_ba30-012.jpg
Notes:
68162400 This is just a fly (order Diptera) - I'm not sure what kind it is, though it's bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly (Tachinidae). It's perched on a twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

"Just a fly..." - but look how perfect it is in form and function! Today's photos are all interesting flies.
CommonNames: fly; Tachinid Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: bb02a_bb02-024.jpg
Notes:
68167282 Suction cups on its toes and a black stripe through its eyes - this is a cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla). It posed for me and my kids today on a stem of Bulrush or Tule - genus Scirpus in the Cyperaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 November 2011)

I'm doing a "Nature Photography" class for a small group of Dunn Middle School kids on Wednesday afternoons. We had a bit of excitement today when a Cooper Hawk swooped down for prey right in front of us, but no one got the photo.

And then there was an event for "NatureTrack" - a new local non-profit foundation designed to provide outdoor education for local school kids. I'm doing a workshop for docents on local plants and critters in a couple of weeks, and I'll probably become more involved. For more info, see:
   http://www.naturetrack.org/

I'm tired. Three photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: bb02b_bb01-066.jpg
Notes:
68167242 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say)

This is a handsome Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly "puddling" on damp soil in the garden. It's a "Blue" butterfly, and the wings are quite blue on their upper surface. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2011)

It odd because they usually appear after the rains. I have photo-records for every month from January to July, and one record from December last year. This is my first photo from November - and the first day of November at that. Well, we did get some good rain last month.

File.Name: bb02c_bb02-007.jpg
Notes:
68167196 Grasshoppers in love!

Here are a mating pair of Short-horned Grasshoppers (family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera). Their nuptial bed is a branch of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family that is done flowering. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 2 November 2011)

File.Name: bb03a_bb01-005.jpg
Notes:
68171499 Here is a perfect little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf on the wild edge of our garden. You can make out flecks of iridescent color on its abdomen. It's tiny - only a few millimeters long. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2011)

There have been showers tonight, and a bit of winter rain is coming in. It's not a big storm, but every bit helps after our long summer drought.

I did my after-school Hike Club today, and I'm tired. Three photos will do - again.

File.Name: bb03b_bb01-033.jpg
Notes:
68171451 This is a beautiful pale-green adult Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's working flowers of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub on the dry river wash.

It may be an Angle-wing Katydid in the genus Microcentrum - possibly the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). These are gentle leaf-like creatures which do no harm. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)
CommonNames: Katydid; Scale Broom; Angle-wing Katydid; California Angle-winged Katydid
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Microcentrum; Microcentrum californicum
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Microcentrum; Microcentrum californicum

File.Name: bb03c_ba31-038.jpg
Notes:
68171420 I found this strange critter in the woods a couple of days ago. It seems to be a pupa inside the split shell of the larva - but I'm really not sure. I'm just the photographer! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2011)

The were several of these critters in one area on the upper stems of a particular shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I didn't see them anywhere else. Is it maybe an internal parasite?

File.Name: bb04a_bb04-007.jpg
Notes:
68175736 This is an unusual native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods - out of it's shell and moving along! I think this is the first time I've actually seen one, though I often find their pretty shells in winter. It's not that they are rare so much as rarely seen. I guess they are nocturnal. I found this one under a board by the mailbox this morning, after we got a bit of rain from a winter storm last night. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2011)

This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails (Helix aspersa) that were apparently brought to California over 100 years ago as a food source. Introduced snails have a vertical shell. Fortunately we don't have those snails here in the mountains.

I have no idea what species this snail is. A web site I found lists 28 taxa of gastropods known just for Ventura County to the south of us, and there is a list of 279 species in California! Maybe this is genus Helminthoglypta? The web site is:
   http://www.magney.org/photofiles/VenturaCountySnails1.htm/VenturaCountySnails1.htm

We had some rain last last night and today, and it's turned cold - in the low 40s, and it will probably dip into the 30s tonight. We only got a tenth of an inch or so, but at least it will keep the seedlings alive.
Locality:
Helix aspersa) that were apparently brought to California over 100 years ago as a food source. Introduced snails have a vertical shell. Fortunately we don't have those snails here in the mountains.

I have no idea what species this snail is. A web site I found lists 28 taxa of gastropods known just for Ventura County to the south of us

File.Name: bb04b_bb01-049.jpg
Notes: 68175507 Here is a little Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) of the order Hemiptera. It's waiting for prey on a new flower cluster of Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family. These little bugs are fierce predators, grabbing their prey in their crab claw-like front legs. They are now past their peak and starting to get scarce as winter approaches. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)

File.Name: bb04c_bb02-001.jpg
Notes:
68175433 This is a pale green "stink bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera. This critter is probably in the subfamily Pentatominae, but there are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2011)

I think the common name is a misnomer. If you happen to squish one or just handle it, they emit a smell like cilantro. I like it.

File.Name: bb04d_bb01-026.jpg
Notes: 68175319 This is a beautiful Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterfly - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti). It's working the still-flowering native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrubs by the river. These butterflies pose so nicely! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum
associatedTaxa: Apodemia mormo; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Apodemia virgulti; Lepidospartum squamatum

File.Name: bb04e_bb01-025.jpg
Notes: 68175305 This is a handsome Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly perched on a leaf of Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium) on the dry wash of the river. These common skippers have been getting scarce as cold weather approaches. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2011)

File.Name: bb04f_ba30-041.jpg
Notes:
68175184 This is what the large bristly Tachinid Flies - family Tachinidae in the order Diptera - do to the equally large and bristly "woolly bear" caterpillars of Tiger Moths (family Arctiidae in the order Lepitoptera). See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68162920

I found this husk of a caterpillar down by the creek and placed it on this leaf for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2011)

File.Name: bb05a_bb05-024.jpg
Notes:
68179605 This is a new mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae protecting her egg case against a potential predator - me! Her nest is on a no-longer-flowering stem of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. What a face! (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)

I cheated on this photo in two ways. I used my on-camera flash because she was hiding in the shade, and I rotated the photo about 120∞ clockwise so she would look more "natural".

We had our first frost last night. It was just a bit of "radiation frost" - the kind of frost that happens on cold, clear nights on surfaces that are open to the dark sky. It was still 36∞F early this morning, but flat surfaces under a clear sky - like our porch railing and my truck roof - can radiate more heat energy than they absorb, and freeze even when the air temperature is above freezing.

There's a 60% chance of more rain tonight and tomorrow, followed by more wind. I'll take the rain, but hold the wind please!
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; radiation frost; radiate
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae; radiation frost; radiate

File.Name: bb05b_bb05-021.jpg
Notes:
68179424 We had a bit of rain yesterday, and autumn rain is the trigger for these Termites (order Isoptera) to form winged "alates" and fly off into the unknown. These are emerging along the chip seal road in our canyon. No house is nearby - but a dragonfly was, picking them off as they took off. Their antennae look like strings of beads - very different from ants. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)

As the interesting website TermiteWeb (http://www.termiteweb.com/) says: "These flying termites are winged reproductives that comprise either males or females whose sole purpose is to start new colonies and become the future king and queen of their new colony.... Very few survive to start a new colony. Termites are poor fliers; the average dispersal range for most species is less than 100 meters (300 ft) from the original colony.

File.Name: bb05c_ba30-040.jpg
Notes:
68179268 Like little birds leaving the nest...

These are ripe seeds of a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon. Though often considered a weed, it is a native species that competes very well with naturalized weeds in disturbed areas, often along roads. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)

File.Name: bb05d_bb05-001.jpg
Notes:
68179205 Just a fly... but it has remarkable form and structure.

I believe this is a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family of the order Diptera. It's perched on a leaf (of Coast live Oak?) on the wild edge of our garden, trying to warm up after a cold night. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)

File.Name: bb05e_bb05-028.jpg
Notes: 68179129 This little Snowy Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) found the perfect shelter from our rain yesterday. It's huddled under a cupped leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree - like an umbrella. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)
CommonNames: Snowy Tree Cricket; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: bb05f_bb04-009.jpg
Notes:
68179076 I can't resist posting another photo of the unusual native terrestrial Land Snail (class Gastropoda) that I found in the woods yesterday. It's possibly genus Helminthoglypta. Here you can see the beautiful cryptic markings on its shell. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2011)

And HOW does it attach to its shell???

File.Name: bb06a_bb06-001.jpg
Notes:
68183746 This is a pretty "bracket fungus" - maybe "Turkey Tail" (Trametes versicolor) or kin, but I didn't examine it close enough to tell. It's growing on a fallen branch in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

I like what David Arora (Mushrooms Demystified, 1986) says about edibility: "Boil for 62 hours, squeeze thoroughly, and serve forth."

We had more rain last night, bringing our total to about 1/2 inch for the weekend, and it's still cold. I don't think I saw an insect all day - not even a fly. Today's photos are all new life that the rain brings...

File.Name: bb06b_bb06-018.jpg
Notes:
68183610 I found a few more of these large "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushrooms in the woods today. They have impressive bulk - the cap of this one is about 8 inches (20 cm) across! It's growing with Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) and a native sedge (Carex globosa). (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

This one has been nibbled... hah, it looks like someone went at it with an ice cream scoop! Maybe a deer? These mushrooms are quite poisonous to you and me, though I often find them nibbled by critters. I wonder if the critters get a tummy ache? (The human symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, and severe cramps.)

For more info, see my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68153973
(That mushroom is gone now - no doubt eaten!)
ScientificName: Boletus satanas; Salvia spathacea; Carex globosa
associatedTaxa: Boletus satanas; Salvia spathacea; Carex globosa

File.Name: bb06c_bb06-005.jpg
Notes:
68183515 I actually found this odd Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing on the wild edge of our garden last year. This photo today is my evidence that the old fruiting bodies last forever! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

If you look very close, you can see a tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera) inside the sack at the top. I didn't notice it until I got my photo on the computer screen. It makes a good umbrella from the rain last night.

I found this "mushroom" last year and set it above the retaining wall along our parking place to use as my humidity sensor. Really! The mushrooms respond to humidity by opening when its wet and closing when its dry. I believe they work like a "bimetallic strip thermostat" - there are two layers in the fungus that respond differently to humidity (instead of temperature), and the result is movement.

I agree it's about as useful as hillbilly weather predictor - a stick: "Hold it out the window. If itís wet, Rain. If it moves from side to side, Wind." - but it is fun!

File.Name: bb06d_bb06-014.jpg
Notes:
68183227 The forests of Lilliput...

These are little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia?) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the woods. I assume the little "pyxie cups" and everything else in this photo are the same lichen. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

File.Name: bb06e_bb06-011.jpg
Notes: 68183059 This is a different Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) than that shown in my [Previous] photo. It's another "resurrection plant" already turned green after a bit of rain on Friday night. I'm not sure if this is one plant or a colony of separate plants, but they appear to be growing from a common center. It's growing on slightly decomposed sandstone. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

File.Name: bb06f_bb05-010.jpg
Notes:
68183004 Many plants are called "resurrection plants", but I think this tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) truly deserves the name. We had a bit of rain on Friday night and here it is greening up on midday Saturday! It starts as the black crust you can see in the top part of my photo. It's turning green from the bottom up, maybe because the rain water accumulated there? I went back today, and it was totally green. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2011)

This liverwort has tiny leaves, only 3 or 4 mm. I'm not sure if this is one plant or a colony of separate plants. It's growing on slightly decomposed sandstone.

File.Name: bb07a_bb07-016.jpg
Notes:
68188430 We have banana slug weather in November! But last year I found them in October. See my photo:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65230173

This is a giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) navigating an oak leaf in the woods today. These slugs are large, 5-8 inches long, though this one was on the small side. I found it in a place where I always find them first - I don't know why. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

It's another freezing night... The cold has knocked down the insects, but it's brought a regime change in the woods - interesting new stuff to photograph!

File.Name: bb07b_bb07-027.jpg
Notes:
68188343 Here is another photo of a beautiful and unusual native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods, maybe genus Helminthoglypta. You can see its translucent body. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

It was hiding under a board by the mailbox, just like the one I found a few days ago. I turned the board over and got a few photos - and of course I turned the board back over when I was done. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: bb07c_bb07-024.jpg
Notes: 68188301 This is a beautiful and unusual native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods, maybe genus Helminthoglypta. It was hiding under a board by the mailbox. I turned the board over and got a few photos - and of course I turned the board back over when I was done. See my [Next] photo. This is a good shot of its beautiful cryptic shell and translucent body. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

File.Name: bb07d_bb07-011.jpg
Notes:
68188251 This is the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom in our woods. The caps are smooth and polished, with yellow-green colors. It's a soft-gilled mushroom with a "death cup" volva at the base. It didn't have much of a ring on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

Learn the poisonous mushrooms first, then try everything else! But try them cautiously - just a nibble at first, then a bit more if it tastes OK. You know the old saying, "There are old mushroom collectors and bold mushroom collectors - but no old, bold mushroom collectors!

File.Name: bb07e_bb07-007.jpg
Notes:
68188157 I found this early Bolete mushoom in the genus Boletus on my walk today. I'm not sure what species it is. (I'm not in mushroom mode yet!) Maybe B. subtomentosus or B. dryophilus? It's probably edible, but not choice. I'll wait for the good boletes! (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

It has a smooth tan cap and pores like a sponge instead of gills. The pores stain blue when bruised, but they are not that other notorious mushroom.

File.Name: bb07f_bb07-004.jpg
Notes:
68187995 This is a perfectly named "Dead Man's Foot" (Pisolithus tinctorius) "mushroom" coming up in it's favorite habitat - along a road. I've also heard it called the "Dog Turd Fungus", "Dyemaker's Puffball", and "Bohemian Truffle". You can see some bluish color if you look close. The species name "tinctorus" refers to its use as a fabric dye for colors ranging from gold to dark blue depending on the mordant. (San Marcos Pass, 7 November 2011)

This one has obviously been out for a while, but I didn't notice it before. The genus name "Pisolithus" literally means "pea stone" - it gets its name from the pea-shaped peridioles which are visible in my photo. They break down to release the spores.

File.Name: bb08a_b924-007.jpg
Notes:
68192909 Here is a little resident Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) fluffed up on a cold day. That yellow spot on its bill is probably pollen from the Salvia greggii still flowering in the garden. I sometimes wonder why they stay here all winter when they could fly down the mountain to Santa Barbara, but they do. Maybe because I keep the feeder full of sugar water. I'm glad they stick around! (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2011)

It's been very cold with freezing nights and frosty mornings. There's been a regime change in the birds as well as the insects and mushrooms and snails. I'm not a great bird photographer like JQuental. I usually look down rather than up! Today's photos are some of the birds I've found lately.

Light is getting scarce at our house on the north-facing slopes of the east-west Santa Ynez Mountains. The sun doesn't shine on the ground until 11:00 am, but it does light up the trees in the morning. I like to be on the upstairs porch in the morning with a cup of coffee to see what I can capture.

File.Name: bb08b_bb08-007.jpg
Notes: 68192842 This is a beautiful female Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird posing for me today in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the upstairs porch. The dark eye stripe is paler on the female than the male in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 November 2011)

File.Name: bb08c_bb03-011.jpg
Notes:
68192819 This is an acrobatic Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi) bird looking for a treat in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the upstairs porch. They are beautiful birds that stick around most of the winter and then disappear north in the spring and summer. I guess this is their south. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2011)

I believe this is a male with those black eye stripes. The females are not so dark. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: bb08d_ba02-003.jpg
Notes:
68192767 I've been sitting on this photo for a while hoping for a better shot, but the birds seem to have passed through. The bird on the upper-left is a hopeless blur, but look in the lower-right!

I believe this is a Black-Throated Gray Warbler (Dendroica nigrescens). It's a migrant that we see in the fall and spring as it flies from here to there. It's a striking bird with those contrasting colors and yellow eyebrows, but very camera shy. They can sense when I'm looking at them! This one is perched on a pot of succulents by our "bathtub water garden". (San Marcos Pass, 2 October 2011)

File.Name: bb08e_bb01-001.jpg
Notes: 68192704 I believe this is a little Bushtit (Psaltriparus minimus) bird in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near the upstairs porch. It's a classic "LBB" - Little Brown Bird. We don't see them very often. Every once in a while they come through the oaks in a flock and move about furiously looking for whatever they are looking for... and then disappear. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2011)

File.Name: bb08f_ba03-002.jpg
Notes:
68192596 I believe this is a little Ruby-crowned Kinglet (Regulus calendula) bird in the native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family that's growing on the side of our house. Our old "bathtub water pond" is right below it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2011)

The bird has white wing wing bars and an incomplete eye ring. The male birds have a rarely seen red crown that they can expose or contract, but I reckon this is a female.
ScientificName: Regulus calendula; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Regulus calendula; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: bb09a_bb09-005.jpg
Notes:
68197376 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a twig in the blackberries this morning. Sure, it's overexposed - but the spider looks so punk! That gleam in its many eyes is the morning sun. I'll post a more correctly exposed photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2011)

There's a ring around the moon tonight, and another storm approaching with rain starting Friday evening.

I had my nature photography class this afternoon, followed by a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting. I'm tired. Three photos is fine tonight.

File.Name: bb09b_bb09-008.jpg
Notes: 68197359 This is a Banded Argiope (Argiope trifasciata) garden spider in a rosemary shrub at school today. It's web looks pretty tattered - maybe an errant volleyball. A middle school playground is a tough place to be a spider! (Dunn School, Santa Ynez Valley, 9 November 2011)

File.Name: bb09c_bb06-009a.jpg
Notes: 68197338 This is another large Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides or kin) hanging out in a willow tree by the creek. Heh - those little water drops on the black spots of it's abdomen make perfect eyes. Does anyone else remember the comic book/strip character Henry? (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2011)

File.Name: bb10a_bb10-045.jpg
Notes:
68201719 Kids in trees - where they should be!

I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek to the base of Grass Mountain today. This is looking up into a grand old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's my favorite climbing tree. The kids are inching along the branch to get to the next tree without touching the ground. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 November 2011)

(*But hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

I'm officially retired now, but I still do my after-school Hike Club every Thursday for Dunn Middle School kids. This is the 29th year of Hike Club!
Locality:
*But hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

I'm officially retired now

File.Name: bb10b_bb10-033.jpg
Notes:
68201709 Kids in trees - where they should be!

I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek to the base of Grass Mountain today. Here they are on the horizontal branch of a grand Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) in the Plantanaceae plant family growing by the perennial creek. (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 November 2011)

(*Hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

File.Name: bb10c_bb09-002.jpg
Notes: 68201695 This is the same little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) as my [Previous] photo. It's resting on a blackberry leaf, and I watched it jump to the twig. It's a cute little spider. The black spots on its carapace line up with its eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2011)

File.Name: bb11a_bb10-001.jpg
Notes:
68205810 Insects are scarce now, but I found this little yellow-faced Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in the woods today on the hairy stem of a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. She is ovipositing (laying her eggs) on the plant stem. There are aphids nearby, and the larvae are predators. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

This was a lucky photograph - well, the kind of luck you have when you take 20 photos to get one keeper! There is a storm moving in, and it was cloudy and windy. The storm has been teasing us all day with bits of rain and even hail. It seems to be finally settling into steady rain now.

File.Name: bb11b_bb11-032.jpg
Notes:
68205767 Yum - here are delicious new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms for dinner! I found them in the woods today growing along the road. They sell in local markets for $25 a pound. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

Before last year, my record for earliest chanterelles was November 25, 2004 - but last year I found them on October 30. These mushrooms are still pretty early.

They are small. I'll see if they grow a bit before harvesting them for the table.

File.Name: bb11c_bb10-019.jpg
Notes:
68205712 I found this dinner plate-sized "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushroom in the woods today. Some creature has eaten large slices of it, I guess a deer. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous to you and me, though I often find them nibbled by critters. I wonder if the critters get a tummy ache? (The human symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, and severe cramps.)

File.Name: bb11d_bb11-016.jpg
Notes: 68205660 These are delicate new flowers of Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on a shady bank in the woods. The mystery is why are they flowering now? They usually open in July, but I never saw them this year - until now. This has been a summer for late flowers, but this is downright peculiar. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

File.Name: bb11e_bb11-027.jpg
Notes: 68205590 This was interesting. I found this large Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) with its head stuck in an acorn of Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus). (See my [Previous] photo.) I separated slug and acorn, and the slug crawled right back inside. I don't know if it was feeding or trying to hide "Ostrich-style". (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

File.Name: bb11f_bb11-023.jpg
Notes:
68205523 The acorn cups of this Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family resemble the spiny husks of a chestnut rather than the usual scaly cups of most of our oaks. The nuts were prized by native Americans. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

These trees are not common in our mountains, though we have a colony of them right by our house. They are part of the "relict association" of plants that really belong with the redwoods further north.

File.Name: bb12a_bb12-020.jpg
Notes:
68209696 Here is a pretty new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing on the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They are quite poisonous, and have a bitter taste to deter accidents. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2011)

For some reason, these mushrooms don't look as yellow as I see them. Maybe it's a white balance issue? Yellow is always the most difficult color with digital cameras.

We ended up with 0.85 inches of rain last night. Not as much as originally expected, but enough to keep the mushrooms going for a while. I think this rain will really bring the mushrooms out to complete the regime change in the woods.
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: bb12b_bb12-012.jpg
Notes:
68209634 These are new Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2011)

I can't imagine why anyone would confuse them with delicious chanterelle mushrooms, but I suppose anything is possible. DON'T confuse them with chanterelles!

Both mushrooms are orange and sort of trumpet-shaped, but these are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge, and they always grow from wood, sometimes buried. But I've been fooled before - just for a moment!

Go on a mushroom walk with someone who knows. You will never confuse them after you've seen them both. Some varieties are said to glow in the dark, but I've never seen that here.

File.Name: bb12c_bb12-014.jpg
Notes:
68209589 Here are the fruiting bodies of the well-named "Split-Gill" (Schizophyllum commune) fungus in our woods on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree encrusted with lichens. They look like they have snow on their caps, but it's just white hairs. Each gill is actually composed of two adjacent plates which can open and close. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2011)

These are probably the same fruiting caps I found last year - see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66075256

I've read that specimens sealed in a tube in 1911, then moistened 50 years later, unrolled their gills and began shedding spores!

File.Name: bb12d_bb12-005.jpg
Notes:
68209518 This is a large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods. They only have the breathing hole - called a pneumostome - on their right side. The slugs have two pairs of retractile 'feelers' on their heads. The upper pair has eye spots, while the lower pair provides a tactile sense and smell. The challenge as photographer is to capture them with feelers extended because they pull them in when alarmed. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2011)

Go UCSC! (Banana slugs are the University of California, Santa Cruz mascots!)
Locality: Banana slugs are the University of California

File.Name: bb12e_bb12-008.jpg
Notes: 68209490 I found this odd little spider snuggled into the curled leaf margin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It has a remarkable color pattern on its cephalothorax - almost like a diagram of the optic nerves going from eyes to brain! It also has enlarged pedipalps, so it's a male maybe looking for a female. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2011)

File.Name: bb12f_bb10-005.jpg
Notes:
68209431 Insects are scarce in the woods now, but I also found this different little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in the woods yesterday on a flower of a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. (See my [Previous] photo, and look at the eyes.) These might be the only pollinator-type insects left in the woods now as winter kicks in. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2011)

You can clearly see this fly's "spurious vein" if you know what to look for.

File.Name: bb13a_bb13-001.jpg
Notes:
68213634 This gnarly Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides or kin) has captured an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

The spider is having better luck than I am catching local insects, at least photographing them! It was a few degrees warmer today, and these bees are the proof.

I went looking for mushrooms in my usual haunts today, but I didn't find much. It takes a few days after a rain.

File.Name: bb13b_bb13-017.jpg
Notes: 68213596 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) trying to hide under a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) as the light was fading today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

File.Name: bb13c_bb13-019.jpg
Notes:
68213569 Here I am touching a tiny white-spored Mycena mushroom in the woods today with my pen tip. This pen has a 0.7 mm ball, so you can figure the size of the mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

This is the first little Mycena mushroom I've found this year. I expect there will be troops of them in a day or two.

File.Name: bb13d_bb13-011.jpg
Notes:
68213479 These are pretty little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on the soil with mosses in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

I've been confused about these lichens, but Wayne Armstrong cleared it up for me at Wayne's Word (a web site I keep going back to):

"Although the flattened thallus resembles a foliose lichen, it is technically a fruticose lichen because of the upright structures called podetia that resemble miniature golf tees. Inside the cuplike apex of each podetium are numerous soredia, appearing like granules in the right photo. Each soredium is a minute clump of fungal filaments intertwined with algal cells. It is an asexual reproductive structure that may disperse this species to new areas by the wind. Although many species of lichens produce powderlike soredia, Cladonia produces them on a podetium."
(http://waynesword.palomar.edu/pljan98c.htm)
Locality:
a web site I keep going back to):

"Although the flattened thallus resembles a foliose lichen
ScientificName: Cladonia; thallus; foliose; fruticose; podetia; soredia
associatedTaxa: Cladonia; thallus; foliose; fruticose; podetia; soredia

File.Name: bb13e_bb13-014.jpg
Notes: 68213452 This is a native Goldback Fern (Pentagramma triangularis) growing with new mosses in the woods today. A little bit of rain changes everything! I'll try to get another photo that shows the gold backs of these ferns. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

File.Name: bb13f_bb13-016.jpg
Notes:
68213287 I found this unusual pure white California Fuschia (Epilobium canum) in the Onagraceae plant family on my hike today. The flowers are usually deep red. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67919308

I can't recall seeing this before. The flowers lack any pigment. I find these freak "albino" flowers in many species, but these are especially beautiful. There were several plants in the same place that lacked pigment, so maybe it breeds true? I'll try to remember to collect seeds. I want it in our garden! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2011)

By the way, this plant used to be known as Zauschneria cana - with fasciculate, subfiliform leaves, and gray tomentose-canescent foliage - to cite the old Munz flora (1970). But the newer Jepson flora (1993) collapsed the species into one and even changed the genus. Those pesky botanists!

Notes:
68217700 Here is the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom in our woods today. The caps are smooth and polished, with yellow-green colors - though this one was mostly white. It has the remains of a ring on the stem and a "death cup" volva at the base. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2011)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods today, and mushroom spores can travel around the planet. There is a discussion here:
   http://www.msi.harvard.edu/downloads/teacherworkshop/Readings/Ben_Papers%20_TWS/Pringle%20and%20Vellinga%202006.pdf

Rhus trilobata is also called Basket Bush, and the Chumash/Spanish name is Chiquihuite. It was an important plant for the native Americans, who used the stems for the "warp" of their baskets.

It looks a lot like its relative Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum), but the leaves are different. (See my [Next] photo.) The middle leaf does not have a separate stalk; it has berries rather than dry seeds; it has a distinctive "skunk-like" smell; and it does not make you itch. The berries are tart and tasty if you "suck and spit" - but don't suck too long or you notice the skunk!

File.Name: bc01c_bb26-042.jpg
Notes:
68286038 These are tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). They are NOT mushrooms or fungi, thought they look fungus-like. I found them on the dark side of a conifer log in the Oregon woods. (My exposure was 6 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200!) They have just finished their "streaming stage" and are starting to form their spore-bearing fruiting bodies or sorocarps. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2011)

I think these are my favorite organisms, though I don't find them very often - they are tiny, and they don't last long in a visible form. You have to be in the right place and the right time to get a photo.

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

The lumber trucks have been driving past our cabin, so we drove up the gravel road about 10 miles on Sunday to inspect the damage. *Sigh* Clearcut timber operations are something you have to accept in Oregon - but you don't have to like it.

(Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

I've also found these odd critters at home. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58635266

File.Name: bc02a_bb25-039.jpg
Notes:
68289960 This is looking close under the cap of a fantastic Violet Cortinarius (Cortinarius violaceus) mushroom in the Oregon woods last week. It's so violet that it's almost black. It's said to be edible but not choice. It's principal appeal is its beauty. I'll post another photo of the whole mushrooms tomorrow. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

File.Name: bc02b_bb20-032.jpg
Notes: 68289906 Here are an unknown mushroom, mosses, and a plant poking through the snow when we first got to our cabin in the Oregon woods last week. We were lucky this year. It snowed the day before we got to our cabin, and made for a great train ride over the Cascades from Klamath Falls. The snow soon melted, though it never did get warm. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

File.Name: bc02c_bb24-025.jpg
Notes:
68289845 I believe this is the real Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis aka G. junonius and G. venticosus) growing in the Oregon woods last week. Our west coast mushrooms seem to lack the inebriating qualities of the Japanese and east coast G. spectabilis, so David Arora's name Jumbo Gym is probably more appropriate. Que l·stima. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)

Inebriating qualities aside, it really is a remarkable mushroom. They are large. This one is growing from the side of a conifer tree in the woods, and there were more coming up from the base of the tree. It was starting to rain when I got this photo of this oddly-scrunched specimen.
ScientificName: Gymnopilus spectabilis; G. junonius; G. venticosus; G. spectabilis
associatedTaxa: Gymnopilus spectabilis; G. junonius; G. venticosus; G. spectabilis

File.Name: bc02d_bb24-018.jpg
Notes: 68289699 This is a beautiful Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria) mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods last week. Lepiota mushroom caps usually have a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands, making them easy to recognize. This one is said to be poisonous. It's best to avoid all slender woodland Lepiotas. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)

File.Name: bc02e_bb20-022.jpg
Notes: 68289623 I believe these are the Western Grisette (Amanita pachycolea) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. They have free white gills and a volva, but no ring on its stem. The dark brown striate (lined) cap is distinctive. It's said to be edible - if you dare eat any Amanita. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

File.Name: bc02f_bb20-018.jpg
Notes: 68289500 These are pretty little dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushrooms coming up in the Oregon woods last week. They are actually growing from a cluster of deer dung - you can a few scattered "deer berries" at the bottom of the photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

File.Name: bc03a_bb26-040.jpg
Notes:
68293581 This is another photo of the very beautiful yellow mushroom that I found in the Oregon woods last week. I believe it is the "Questionable Stropharia" (Stropharia ambigua) mushroom with purplish-black spores, though the gills look quite white. (See my [Previous] photo.) It has distinctive cottony white veil fragments on the margin of the cap. It's said to taste like "old leaves". David Arora (author of "Mushrooms Demystified") says it is "one of the most exquisitely beautiful of all mushrooms" - and I think I agree. I've never seen them at home. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2011)

File.Name: bc03b_bb26-039.jpg
Notes:
68293506 This is a very beautiful yellow mushroom that I found in the Oregon woods last week. It looks a bit like the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) in my [Previous] photo. It has the flaring ring of an Amanita, but no volva, the defining trait of the genus, and there are other differences. I was confused by the apparently free gills that are very white.

After some research, I believe this is a "Questionable Stropharia" (Stropharia ambigua) mushroom with purplish-black spores. This one has "seceding gills" that have pulled free from the stem. See my [Next] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2011)

Take this as a lesson NOT to assume the spore color is the same as the color of the gills!

File.Name: bc03c_bb25-030.jpg
Notes:
68293373 This is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) - yellow cap with white "warts" which are the remains of the universal veil, skirt-like ring on the stem, and a free-rimmed volva at the base. It looks a bit like a yellow Amanita muscaria, but the volva (not visible here) is different. It's an attractive mushroom, but it's not edible because it intergrades with the poisonous Amanita pantherina. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

I find a more robust form of this mushroom at home growing under Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae plant family. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65893423
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Amanita muscaria; Amanita pantherina; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Amanita muscaria; Amanita pantherina; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: bc03d_bb22-007.jpg
Notes:
68293259 I believe these attractive mushrooms are Cystoderma fallax or kin. What a remarkable flaring "ring" they have on the stalk - it looks like they are wearing warm stockings for the cold. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2011)

Like mushroom guru David Arora says about his own photo of this species, "The illustration does not do them justice, but since when is justice usually done?

File.Name: bc03e_bb24-009.jpg
Notes: 68293204 I believe these are beautiful Orange Milk Cap (Lactarius subflammeus or L. luculentus, aka L. aurantiacus). I sliced the gills of the middle mushroom with my pocket knife, and you can see the white "milk" or latex that doesn't change color. The caps are viscid and colorful. Edibility is unknown. Fortunately you don't have to eat a mushroom to appreciate it! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)
ScientificName: Lactarius subflammeus; L. luculentus; L. aurantiacus
associatedTaxa: Lactarius subflammeus; L. luculentus; L. aurantiacus

File.Name: bc03f_bb25-041.jpg
Notes: 68293098 Here is another photo of the fantastic Violet Cortinarius (Cortinarius violaceus) mushrooms I found in the Oregon woods last week. They're so violet that they are almost black. This photo looks a little duller than my [Previous] photo. I picked that one mushroom and moved it to a beam of sunlight, but this shot is in dark shade - like most of these Oregon mushroom photos. The mushrooms are said to be edible but not choice. Their principal appeal is their beauty, and that's enough! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

File.Name: bc04a_bb21-007.jpg
Notes:
68297407 This is the White Chanterelle (Cantherellus subalbidus) mushroom growing in the Oregon woods last week. It's edible and delicious. I think I like it even better than the orange chantrelles in my [Previous] photo. There were lots of them in the woods just in time for our Thanksgiving dinner. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2011)

Chanterelles have smooth folds under the cap instead of sharp-edged gills like most mushrooms. They are easy to recognize once you actually see one. The mushrooms are remarkably resistant to just about all pests - insects, worms, and decay seem to have no effect on them.

File.Name: bc04c_bb20-036.jpg
Notes: 68297239 I believe these are Corn Silk Inocybe (Inocybe sororia or kin) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. They have a distinctive "witches hat" shape with umbonate caps and they smell like freshly husked corn. I found small groups of these scattered under conifers. Note the bit of snow in the upper right. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

File.Name: bc04d_bb21-033.jpg
Notes: 68297180 There were many Russula mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. They are easy to recognize to genus, but harder to species. These stems are white, which suggests Russula emetica, but cf. Russula rosacea which favors conifers. Fortunately, I don't have to know Oregon mushrooms! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2011)

File.Name: bc04e_bb21-032.jpg
Notes:
68297054 These are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods last week. These little mushrooms have wiry stems and an interesting connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain. You can wear them like earrings if your ears are pierced! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2011)

We also have these mushrooms at home, though I haven't found them yet this year. See my photo at:

File.Name: bc04f_bb25-048.jpg
Notes: 68296969 This photo isn't quite sharp, but these are very cool "mushrooms" so here it is anyway. These are strange Bird's Nest Fungi (family Nidulariales, maybe Crucibulum laeve?) growing on the dead branch of a conifer in the Oregon woods. The tiny mushrooms look like little birds' nests complete with eggs. The eggs are peridioles that hold spores. The force of a single rain drop in the "splash cup" is enough to distribute the eggs. As you can see, the young cups have a cap. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

File.Name: bc06a_bb24-013.jpg
Notes:
68305777 This is loking close at the podetium (mushroom-like stalk) of a beautiful British Soldier Lichen (Cladonia cristatella or kin - maybe C. deformis?) in a bed of moss in the Oregon woods. These podetia are true lichen structures that is not present in unlichenized fungi. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - but sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)

Today's photos are all interesting lichens from our recent visit to the Oregon woods. Enough mushrooms!

File.Name: bc06b_bb24-011.jpg
Notes:
68305724 The forests of Lilliput...

These are mostly beautiful British Soldier Lichens (Cladonia cristatella or kin - maybe C. deformis?) in the Oregon woods growing with moss. I believe the "red hats" at the top of the lichens are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. See my [Next] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)

File.Name: bc06c_bb21-010.jpg
Notes: 68305685 Here are remarkable apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. These are growing on the "leaves" of the Oregon Lung Lobaria (Lobaria oregana) lichen. This lichen has fallen to the ground from its perch in the trees. (See my [Previous] photo.) Maybe the fungi know when it's time to abandon ship? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2011)

File.Name: bc06d_bb20-026.jpg
Notes: 68305656 Here is a clump of Oregon Lung Lobaria (Lobaria oregana) lichen growing as an epiphyte on branch of Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana). It's a common foliose-type lichen in the Oregon woods. It looks like monster skin! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)
CommonNames: Oregon Lung Lobaria; Oregon White Oak

File.Name: bc06e_bb22-002.jpg
Notes:
68305598 These are cute little Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) lichens in the Oregon woods. They are considered "fruticose" lichens because they stand up on their own. The "pyxie cup" structures look like little mushrooms, but they are part of the lichen. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2011)

These upright structures are called podetia. Inside the cups are numerous soredia - minute clumps of fungal mycelium intertwined with algal cells. This is an asexual reproductive structure that may disperse this species to new areas by the wind.

Reproduction is difficult for lichens because they are actually dual organisms - a fungus and an algae - so both critters have to reproduce to make a new lichen.

(Hah - how many different organisms are you and I composed of? I suspect many! Rest in peace, Lynn Margulis.)
Locality: Hah - how many different organisms are you and I composed of? I suspect many! Rest in peace

File.Name: bc06f_bb24-022.jpg

Lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - but sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2011)

File.Name: bc07a_bb21-023.jpg
Notes:
68309821 I found a few of these large Slugs (Gastropoda) in the Oregon woods during our Thanksgiving visit. I don't know what kind of slug it is, and I'm too tired to keep trying. It's quite large, maybe 3 inches. Maybe it's a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus), though I doubt it - at least our Southern California species look very different. I can't believe it's an introduced species deep in the Oregon woods like this. I'll update this if I find it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2011)

Today's photos are a few more from our Thanksgiving trip to Oregon. I have many more. The Oregon woods grow trees and mushrooms like nobody's business. I photographed 70+ different kinds of mushrooms during our trip last week! But I've been taking photos every day at home, and we also have new mushrooms in the mountains. Time to move on!

File.Name: bc08b_bb20-046.jpg
Notes: 68314004 These are handsome fern-like Moss (Bryophyta) leaves on a long cone of Sugar Pine (Pinus lambertiana) on the forest floor in the Oregon woods. These pines are uncommon in our Oregon woods. They are large and beautiful. The flooring of our cabin is native Sugar Pine. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2011)

File.Name: bc08c_bb27-010.jpg
Notes:
68313987 This is a cluster of ripe (maybe over-ripe) winter berries of Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess the birds missed these berries. A book I have says they taste better after some frost. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2011)

We also have these plants at home, but they are a remnant population that almost never form berries - and the birds get them when they do.

File.Name: bc08d_bb26-008.jpg
Notes: 68313968 This is a willow in the North Umpqua River flowing through the canyon in Oregon. It's a beautiful river, renowned for fly-fishing. The water was high and turbid during our visit. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2011)

File.Name: bc08e_bb27-019.jpg
Notes:
68313953 You don't see the clearcuts driving along the river, but they ar striking when you get up into the mountains. This is up the mountain road from our cabin down in the river valley. You can see past clearcuts across the canyon. All of my photos for the past week are taken down in the valley. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2011)

Clearcut timber operations are something you have to accept in Douglas County, Oregon - but you don't have to like it. I believe most of our property was logged during World War ll, and it's come back well. But I don't think it was clearcut. I think the idea of clearcutting is to allow natural regeneration in a natural mosaic, but some of the clearcuts don't come back so well. I need to do some real research about this.

I believe that's Kelvin on the left, with two White Canterelles in the basket that he's holding up so proudly. I like that nothing in the photo is quite sharp except for Kelvin and his basket! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2011)

File.Name: bc09a_bc08-046.jpg

Fotolog was down earlier tonight, then it came back close to "fotolog midnight". I decided to post my photos late. I'm tired - I hope my comments make sense.

File.Name: bc09b_bc03-002.jpg

I'll post another photo of this unusual plant tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2011)

This plant only gets about 20 minutes of direct sunlight right now, so this was a well-timed shot. The sun has slipped below the mountains, and our home and garden are mostly in the shade as we approach the winter solstice in two weeks. Our house is on the north-facing slope - hot in summer and cold in winter.

Notes:
68320432 We don't need to go to Oregon to find giant slugs in the woods! See my photo at:


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68309821

I found this large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods today. They only have the breathing hole - called a pneumostome - on their right side. The slugs have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair has eye spots, while the lower pair provides a tactile sense and smell. The challenge as photographer is to capture them with feelers extended because they pull them in when alarmed. They don't move fast, but they do keep moving. They are actually a challenge to photograph - I had to bump up my ISO to 400 for this photo, and 800 would have been better. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2011)

I haven't seen them all week. It's been very dry, and windy. But there's a forecast for "likely rain" starting tomorrow night, and here was a ring around the moon last night. Do they know rain is coming?

File.Name: bc10b_bc10-022.jpg
Notes:
68320415 This is a yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom catching a rare beam of afternoon sunlight in the woods today. It's small, but I've been spotting them to collect and cook up for my Christmas Hike Club to our house next week. I've found about a dozen so far! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2011)
