This is a small but yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom catching a rare beam of afternoon sunlight in the woods today. It's small, but I've been looking for them to collect and cook up for my Christmas Hike Club to our house next week. I've found about a dozen so far! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2011)

Chanterelles sell in local markets for $25 a pound. This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings!

File.Name: bc10c_bc10-009.jpg
Notes:
68320260 These are the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms in our woods today. The caps are smooth and polished, with hints of yellow-green colors. The partial-veil ring on the stem is just pulling free, and there is a "death cup" volva at the base. It is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2011)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

There is some evidence that this mushroom has been introduced from Europe. It doesn't seem likely as I found this deep in the woods today, and mushroom spores can travel around the planet. There is a discussion here:
   http://www.msi.harvard.edu/downloads/teacherworkshop/Readings/Ben_Papers%20_TWS/Pringle%20and%20Vellinga%202006.pdf

File.Name: bc10d_bc01-012.jpg
Notes: 68320125 This mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae is protecting her newly hatched spiderlings. The babies are not visible here, but you can see the shed skins of their first molt under the spider. She has such an angular face! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2011)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; angular
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; angular

File.Name: bc10e_bc01-002.jpg
Notes:
68320090 This looks like a studio shot, but it's just a garden photo - the plant is catching a beam of sunlight in our winter-dark woods.

This plant is an interesting non-native Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has large, divided leaves and long, branching, bamboo-like stems and white daisy-like flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2011)

We got this plant as a cutting from my Dad's garden on the coast. It's quite large. It's said to be frost-tender, but it's still holding on after a cold week.

File.Name: bc10f_bb30-005.jpg
Notes:
68319989 I'm teaching an afternoon class on nature photography for Dunn Middle School, and I took my photo kids to Nojoqui Falls last week for a photo shoot. It's named after the Chumash village "Naxuwi" that used to be nearby - pronounced "Naw-ho-wee" not "no-joke-ee". It falls 164 feet over a massive travertine front that is slowly building up, like a stalactite. Beautiful. (Nojoqui Falls County Park, 30 November 2011)

The green plants spreading like a cape on the travertine face of the falls are mostly Maidenhair Fern (Adiantum capillus-veneria) and Coastal Brookfoam (Boykinia occidentalis) in the Saxifragaceae plant family.

Nojoqui Falls is a county park about 20 miles west of home, also on the north slopes of the Santa Ynez Mountains. It's a hard place to photograph. The kids went close without thinking. I stood back to capture them. The kids are visible at the bottom of my photo.
Locality:
Boykinia occidentalis) in the Saxifragaceae plant family.

Nojoqui Falls is a county park about 20 miles west of home
CommonNames: Nojoqui Falls; Maidenhair Fern; Coastal Brookfoam

File.Name: bc11a_bc11-034.jpg
Notes:
68323689 Somehow the mushrooms know that rain is coming!

This is a very dark cluster Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) trees in the woods. You can see a leaf and acorn cups of the tanoak in the foreground. The mushrooms will develop their honey colors as they mature. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)

I've been checking for these mushrooms almost every day since before our Oregon trip, but they waited until now - with rain expected tonight or tomorrow. Can the mushrooms really sense that rain is coming?

It's been looking like rain all day, and it feels like rain - and rain is forecast by the NWS tonight or tomorrow! We need it. The woods feel very dry, but there is a surprising diversity of mushrooms hiding under their mushrumps in the woods. I think even a bit of rain will bring them up!
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Tanbark Oak
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Lithocarpus densiflorus
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Lithocarpus densiflorus

File.Name: bc11b_bc02-007.jpg
Notes:
68323688 These may be the tiniest "mushrooms" in our woods. They are fruiting on a fallen leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf above our retaining wall at home. I put my pen in the photo for scale. It has a 0.7 mm ball, so these little mushrooms are less than 0.7 mm across! (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)

I use the word "mushroom" advisedly. Mushrooms are properly the fleshy fruiting bodies of fungi, and I doubt that these fruiting bodies are at all fleshy.

File.Name: bc11c_bc11-006.jpg
Notes:
68323684 Here is a lesson in mushroom anatomy.

This is the same unknown white Cortinarius mushroom as my [Previous] photo, in the subgenus Bulbopodium. You can see the remnant of the "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills, now turned rusty-orange from falling spores. There are also some similar hairs on the mushroom cap where they used to attach. These cobwebby hairs are the hallmark of the genus Cortinarious - maybe the largest genus of all mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)

File.Name: bc11d_bc11-003.jpg
Notes:
68323481 I don't know what this white Cortinarius mushroom is, but it's definitely in the subgenus Bulbopodium of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. I don't think I've ever seen it before. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)

You can see a remnant of the "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills, now turned rusty-orange from falling spores. It may be edible - but you can't reliably tell cortinarius mushrooms apart. As they age, they all look the same.

I searched the web for "white bulbopodium cortinarius" and found this report from San Diego, which looks similar:
   http://mushroomobserver.org/obs/34526

File.Name: bc11e_bc10-020.jpg
Notes: 68323481 This is another Cortinarius mushroom with yellow colors on the stem and cap. I guess it is part of the Cortinarius fulmineus group, at least that's a handy name to remember these mushrooms with yellow colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. You can see a remnant of the "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills, now turned rusty-orange from falling spores. It may be edible - but you can't reliably tell cortinarius mushrooms apart. As they age, they all look the same. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2011)

File.Name: bc11f_bc07-016.jpg
Notes: 68323398 This is another Cortinarius mushroom with violet colors on the stem and cap - and probably on the younger gills as well. I guess it is part of the Cortinarius sodagnitus group, at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with violet colors. It's definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, and non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. The gills have turned rusty-brown from falling spores, and you can see a remnant of the now orange "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills. It may be edible - but you can't reliably tell cortinarius mushrooms apart. As they age, they all look the same. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 December 2011)

File.Name: bc12a_bc12-022.jpg
Notes:
68327713 "Grounded by bad weather..."

I was surprised to find this Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) hanging on a plant of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. I guess it's grounded by the rainy weather we've been having. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

It wasn't windy today, but there was a gentle breeze rocking this plant back and forth. That and the low light on a gloomy afternoon made this a difficult photo to get.

Maybe I shouldn't be surprised. I've found them in almost the same place near the bridge over our creek for the past three years. See my photos:
   2010 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66052982
   2009 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56940048
   2008 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/44454306
ScientificName: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Sympetrum corruptum; Libellulidae; Odonata; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: bc12b_bc12-003.jpg
Notes:
68327666 It's always fun to try to spot the smallest mushroom in the woods, and I think this qualifies - at least it's the smallest mushroom that I have a name for.

This is a tiny Horsehair Fungus (Marasmius androsaceus or kin) mushroom sprouting from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree leaf in the soggy woods today. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm tip. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

The smallest mushrooms? Now look closer - there are even tinier mushroom on the leaves to the right and below the mushroom!

This Marasmius mushroom was no doubt here before the rain today. They have a way of appearing right after a rain when they were here all along, shrivelled up and inconspicuous. Maybe they have a connection with lichens?

File.Name: bc12c_bc12-008.jpg
Notes:
68327529 Here are the beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushrooms in our woods today. You can clearly see the yellow-green colors on the smooth and polished caps, but they're not always this obvious. The mushroom on the left has a perfect partial-veil ring on the stem, but the "death cup" volva at the base is harder to see. This mushroom is responsible for the majority of fatal mushroom poisonings worldwide. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

This is one of the wild mushrooms you MUST learn to recognize in all it's forms if you collect any wild mushrooms for the table - it's deadly poisonous!

For more info, see my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68320260

File.Name: bc12d_bc12-010.jpg
Notes:
68327491 We have Banana Slug weather today!

This is another large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods today. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair has eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. Here it is facing a decision - up or under? It went slug-left instead. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

We had not-quite-an-inch of rain last night, and some drizzle today. Not as much as I was hoping for, but it was a good soak for the woods.

File.Name: bc12e_bc11-021.jpg
Notes: 68327402 This is a group of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing in the hole they've made in a stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They are said to cause a heart rot in trees, and you can see it here. They are also said to glow in the dark, but the mushrooms in our canyon don't seem to do that. It's quite poisonous - don't eat it! There's no reason to confuse them with chanterelles. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)

File.Name: bc12f_bc11-017.jpg
Notes:
68327296 I believe these are large white Sordid Waxy Caps (Hygrophorus sordidus) mushrooms in the woods yesterday growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The species name apparently comes from Charles Peck, who named the species in 1898, because the white caps are "usually defiled by adhering dirt." These certainly are.

They are quite large - salad plate size at least - but otherwise rather boring mushrooms, with no interesting veils or smells or interesting colors. They are said to be edible but not choice. Mushroom author David Arora describes it as "slugulose". The gills are soft, and they don't feel particularly waxy to me. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2011)
CommonNames: Sordid Waxy Caps; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: bc13a_bc13-054.jpg
Notes:
68331748 This is looking close at the cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in my [Previous] photo. You can see the honey-colors that are the namesake of these mushrooms. You can also see the minute brown fibrillose scales on the caps which are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

Honey-mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "oak root fungus", but they seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps. (Knock on wood!)

File.Name: bc13b_bc13-048.jpg
Notes:
68331724 This is a beautiful cluster of honey-colored Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up from a stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). They are white-spored, with a ring and no volva, and adnate gills. They are edible and tasty. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

This is only about 10 feet away from the very dark cluster of these mushrooms I featured two days ago, but I never noticed it before.
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68323689

File.Name: bc13c_bc13-056.jpg
Notes: 68331656 This is a beautiful "LBM" - little brown mushroom - above our retaining wall with a background of fallen apple leaves. It has dark spores. I won't try to guess what the mushroom is, but the grass is native California Brome (Bromus carinatus), which we've been encouraging here. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

File.Name: bc13d_bc13-001.jpg
Notes: 68331636 This handsome Agaricus mushroom has a thick felt-like partial veil to protect the immature gills, which forms a ring on the stem. It has colored gills and no cup or volva at the base of the stem, like the Aminatas. It looks delicious, but I suspect it is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom which is poisonous to many people. It is stains yellow with handling, and has a "phenolic" taste. It is not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

File.Name: bc13e_bc13-038.jpg
Notes:
68331554 These are tiny yellow Cup Fungi (Aleuria or kin) growing on a rotting branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

These are Ascomycete (phyllum Ascomycota) fungi, not the usual Basidiomycete (phyllum Basidiomycota) fungi which include most mushrooms. They comprise the largest group of fungi, with over 64,000 species. Pardon me for not knowing which one these are!
Locality: phyllum Basidiomycota) fungi which include most mushrooms. They comprise the largest group of fungi
CommonNames: Cup Fungi; Coast Live Oak; Ascomycete; Basidiomycete

File.Name: bc13f_bc12-026.jpg
Notes: 68331411 Here is a close photo of the Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly (family Libellulidae, order Odonata) that I found yesterday. See my [Previous] photo. At first I thought it was out of focus, but look very close at the water drop on it's eye. Here are the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes magnified by the drop of water. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

File.Name: bc14a_bc14-012.jpg
Notes:
68339531 With so many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) in the trees, the real challenge is to get just one or a few. Here they are, hanging from non-native Eucalyptus leaves with nice backlight. (Ellwood Mesa, 14 December 2011)

See my [Previous] photos for more info about these amazing butterflies. I got these photos with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens. It's not as sharp as my macro lens, and it shows - especially on tight crops like this.

File.Name: bc14b_bc14-025.jpg
Notes:
68339486 I took my Wednesday afternoon "nature photography" kids to the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. Here is another photo of many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from the non-native Eucalyptus trees. (Ellwood Mesa, 14 December 2011)

The monarch butterflies from all the western states on our side of the Rocky Mountains congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. (East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

I guess the stumper is what are these native butterflies doing in non-native trees? I guess part of the answer is that I've also found them in native Black Cottonwood (Populus balsamifera ssp. trichocarpa) trees. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57875013
ScientificName: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Eucalyptus; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa
associatedTaxa: Danaus plexippus; Lepidoptera; Eucalyptus; Populus balsamifera; trichocarpa

File.Name: bc14c_bc14-013.jpg
Notes:
68335737 "Strange fruit hanging from the eucalyptus trees..."

I took my Wednesday afternoon "nature photography" kids to the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove today, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. These strange fruit are many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from the non-native Eucalyptus trees. (Ellwood Mesa, 14 December 2011)

These aren't the horrible "strange fruit" that Billie Holiday sang of - here is something wonderful! The monarch butterflies from all the western states on our side of the Rocky Mountains congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. (East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

They are a challenge to photograph. They look quite brilliant in the sun, but they look like dried leaves in the shade. It's like trying to photograph a redhead with strong backlight.

I did my afternoon class, then a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting. I'm tired. One photo will do today. More monarchs tomorrow!
Locality:
East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

They are a challenge to photograph. They look quite brilliant in the sun

File.Name: bc15a_bc15-008.jpg
Notes:
68339636 I brought my after-school Hike Club kids home today to gather and cook wild mushrooms. Here is a colander full of freshly washed Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms that we collected. They were as delicious as they look! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2011)

I brought my after-school Hike Club kids home today to gather and cook wild mushrooms. Here is a colander full of freshly washed Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms that we collected. They were as delicious as they look! (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2011)

Chanterelles are easy to recognize once you've seen them - but you have to learn for yourself. They are yellow-orange, and they have blunt folds underneath rather than sharp gills. Books and photos help, but you really have to learn the mushrooms from another person who knows, and then you will know. It's like learning music - this is real person-to-person culture.

I also added a couple of butterfly shots to yesterday's photos. Don't miss them.

File.Name: bc15b_bc15-019.jpg
Notes:
68339606 I brought my after-school Hike Club kids home today to collect and cook wild chanterelle mushrooms. Not much of a hike, but a fun time!

Here's a bunch of the yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms we found "stewing in their own juices". It's really remarkable how much juice these mushrooms make in a hot pan, especially after a rain. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2011)

Slice your chantrelles into a pan on high heat with no oil, and they make their own sauce as the water cooks out of them. Then let the juice cook down until it's almost gone, and add a bit of olive oil, salt, and garlic to simmer... delicious!

File.Name: bc15c_bc12-012.jpg
Notes: 68339566 This is a yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom coming up along our road a few days ago. This is all you see - if you're lucky! Most early chanterelles is the woods now are just "mushrumps" - little rises in the leaf litter. If you dig, you sometimes discover gold! (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2011)

File.Name: bc16a_bc16-006.jpg
Notes:
68343339 This is a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the woods today. Or maybe I should call it a Hover Fly because there are so few flowers in the winter woods, and these flies do hover in the sunbeams? They are one the few winter insects still on the wing. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

I believe these syrphid flies lay their eggs to become parasitoids on aphids and caterpillars. I'm not sure what they are doing active so close to the dead of winter - though I watch the birds every morning looking for *something* in the oak trees from our upstairs porch. I guess there are new insects forming, and maybe *now* is the time to get them, before the new insects emerge?
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak; Hover Fly

File.Name: bc16b_bc16-019.jpg
Notes:
68343293 Here is a colorful - and edible - new Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods, the first I've found this year. They are edible, but not incredible. They are kind of a "weed mushroom" in our woods, usually growing in masses of oak leaves along the road. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

Be careful though - the mushrooms start blue, but they soon fade to gray or tan. And we also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. Caveat emptor!

File.Name: bc16c_bc16-031.jpg
Notes: 68343249 I believe this is a handsome cluster of "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms in the woods today. They have brown spores, which you can see as the natural spore prints in my photo - like a cascade of wine glasses spilling into one another. It doesn't usually grow in clusters like this. As the common name indicates, this mushroom is not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

File.Name: bc16d_bc13-020.jpg
Notes: 68343222 These are new (since the last rain) fruiting bodies of Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. They are said to be edible, but some people are apparently sensitive to it. Like my [Next] mushroom, they are common in our woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2011)

File.Name: bc16e_bc16-020.jpg
Notes: 68343165 These are handsome Russula mushrooms in the woods today. They all have white to yellow-orange spores and dry, brittle stems that you can snap like a piece of chalk. There are so many different kinds that mushroom hunters sometimes call it a JAR - "Just Another Russula". I didn't taste these. Just another Russula, but they are attractive mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

File.Name: bc16f_bc13-017.jpg
Notes:
68343086 This is a strange Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica) mushroom growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, it's mostly water. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

The other fungus in the photo is a resupinate Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two." If you look at the left side of the jelly mushroom you can see them getting intimate. I'll leave it to the mycologists to figure that out!
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Crust Fungus

File.Name: bc17a_bc17-039.jpg
Notes:
68346715 Where do you go when you reach the end of the grass blade? (This critter went back down.)

This is a little Beetle (order Coleoptera) - maybe a Weevil (Curculionidae). This beetle is really tiny, just 2 or 3 mm long. It's reached the end of its grass blade, probably native California Brome (Bromus carinatus), which we've been encouraging here. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

I did a "100 inch hike" along the top of our retaining wall today. It's a convenient height for my tripod. When you look very close, you find critters! It was overcast today, but there wasn't a breath of wind. I got all of my photos today using my time-delay shutter, though these are all tight crops of macro shots near 1:1 magnification. This is as close as I can get.

I'm distracted tonight because Turner Classic Movies (TCM) is having a night of "screwball comedies" with Cary Grant and Katharine Hepburn. Playing as I post is "The Philadelphia Story" - so smart and funny, way up near the top of my favorite movies!
Locality: TCM) is having a night of "screwball comedies" with Cary Grant and Katharine Hepburn. Playing as I post is "The Philadelphia Story" - so smart and funny
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Curculionidae; Bromus carinatus; The Philadelphia Story
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Curculionidae; Bromus carinatus; The Philadelphia Story

File.Name: bc17b_bc17-030.jpg
Notes: 68346625 Here is a tiny but remarkable Planthopper insect in the family Membracidae of the order Homoptera. It seems to be all head, but that's actually the enlarged pronotum of the thorax. I think I see a owl-face with eyes on its pronotum! It's perched on an old stem of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass, which we've been encouraging here. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc17c_bc17-026.jpg
Notes: 68346518 This is a tiny Wasp in the order Hymenoptera - I won't try to guess which kind, though it does have interesting wing venation. (I will guess - maybe Braconidae?) It appears to be standing in the air, but I think it's standing on a spider web. I have no idea what it's up to, though there are lots of tiny insects in the woods for it to parasitize. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc17d_bc17-033.jpg
Notes: 68346458 This is a remarkable tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) resting on a blade of grass above the retaining wall on the wild side of our garden. I'm pretty sure the grass in this place is native California Brome (Bromus carinatus), which we've been encouraging here. It was overcast today, but not too gloomy - and not a breath of wind. Perfect for close photos. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc17e_bc17-040.jpg
Notes:
68346396 I guess this is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae perched on the inner circle of its web. It's munching an even tinier fly, though it's hard to see much. This spider has remarkable translucent "wings" on the sides of its cephalothorax. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

I did a "100 inch hike" along the top of our retaining wall today. It's a convenient height for my tripod. When you look very close, you find critters! It was overcast today, but there wasn't a breath of wind. I got all of my photos today using my time-delay shutter, though these are all tight crops of macro shots near 1:1 magnification.

File.Name: bc17f_bc16-012.jpg
Notes:
68346276 This is a different Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) from my [Previous] photo. It's a different fly, but probably the same species. (It's hard to tell with these flies!) This one is hanging on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

See my [Previous] photo for more info.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Monkey Flower

File.Name: bc18a_bc18-007.jpg
Notes:
68350122 Look close to see the tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on these blushing flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2011)

They are tiny, but fun to watch - like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend their time chasing each other away.

File.Name: bc18b_bc18-026.jpg
Notes:
68350093 This is a native Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2011)

I love these wild bees and want to find out more about them. I might have a chance tomorrow. I'm meeting with Gordon Frankie to discuss setting up a new wild bee-monitoring site in the Santa Ynez Valley. He knows his stuff. See his web page at:
   http://nature.berkeley.edu/urbanbeegardens/

File.Name: bc18c_bc18-021.jpg
Notes:
68350012 Here is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2011)

Honey bees are efficient pollinators, but they were actually introduced to California in 1850 or so. The native bees are more interesting. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: bc18d_bc01-003.jpg
Notes:
68349884 Here is a pretty little fly (order Diptera) resting on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods. It looks like a predator to me, but I have no idea what kind of fly it is. (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2011)

I've been sitting on this photo for a couple of weeks waiting for other new insects to share it with. Now is the time.

File.Name: bc18e_bc17-067.jpg
Notes: 68349803 This looks like one of the old Gateway Computer boxes - a Holstein cow! It must be the pupal case of a moth or other insect. It's on a stem of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass. I found it yesterday on my "100 inch hike" along the top of our retaining wall. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc18f_bc17-067.jpg
Notes:
68349717 The scavenger - scavenged.

I recognize the face - this is part of the remains of a Turkey Vulture (Cathartes aura) bird. Now it's roadkill, tossed unceremoniously along Stagecoach Road in our mountains. Usually these are the birds that scavenge the dead, but this one has met his kind. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc19a_bc17-057.jpg
Notes:
68354046 This is a beautiful mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. I know our local mushrooms pretty well, and I don't have a clue what this is! It has great texture on the cap and stem.

My first thought was Lactarius, but I sliced the gills and there is no latex. I flipped through a few of my mushroom books tonight - maybe it's the Plums and Custard (Tricholomopsis rutilans) mushroom? (Great name!) But that one has yellowish gills, and the gills of this one are quite white. I'm glad there are still surprises in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

We had a bit of rain today. Not much, but it was wet this morning. In a dry year (so far), every bit helps!

File.Name: bc19b_bc18-028.jpg
Notes:
68353914 I believe this is a beautiful and delicious White King Bolete (Boletus barrowsii) in the family Boletaceae in the woods! This one has brown colors on the cap and the gills are yellow, but that can happen with age. The pores do not stain blue. This is one of the best of our edible mushrooms - maybe THE best. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2011)

I found this mushroom growing under a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) tree near an old homestead in the mountains. There were several of them. I'm going back with my basket! This is a handsome Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom in the woods. It has a potato-like minutely velvety cap, and yellow to red colors on the stem. It has sponge-like pores instead of gills, which stain blue when you bruise them. This mushroom also had copious amounts of yellow mycelium at the base. It's said to be edible, though I've never tried it. There are better mushrooms in the woods! (See my [Next] photo!) (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)
CommonNames: White King Bolete; Coulter Pine; Oak-Loving Bolete
ScientificName: Boletus barrowsii; Boletaceae; Pinus coulteri; Boletus dryophilus
associatedTaxa: Boletus barrowsii; Boletaceae; Pinus coulteri; Boletus dryophilus

File.Name: bc19c_bc16-036.jpg
Notes: 68353836 This is a handsome Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom in the woods. It has a potato-like minutely velvety cap, and yellow to red colors on the stem. It has sponge-like pores instead of gills, which stain blue when you bruise them. This mushroom also had copious amounts of yellow mycelium at the base. It's said to be edible, though I've never tried it. There are better mushrooms in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

File.Name: bc19d_bc18-001.jpg
Notes: 68353759 I believe these are interesting leaf galls on Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. They are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). If you peel one of the galls open, you can see the tiny aphids inside. This plant is covered with the galls. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2011)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: bc19e_bc17-074.jpg
Notes:
68353686 In with the new...

These are new flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Along with Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) and Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum), they are the first flowers of the new rain year to appear. They are quite pretty with gray foliage. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

Another new year is unfolding in our mountains...
CommonNames: Woolly Indian Paintbrush; Bigberry Manzanita; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Castilleja foliolosa; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ribes malvaceum
associatedTaxa: Castilleja foliolosa; Scrophulariaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ribes malvaceum

File.Name: bc19f_bc17-076.jpg
Notes:
68353558 Out with the old...

These are one of the last of the native summer flowers - Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. They seem to get rosier as the weather gets colder. They are hard to photograph with small clusters of flowers scattered on long stems. It has to be dead calm to get a photo like this, and it was. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc20a_bc20-021.jpg
Notes:
68358033 These are brilliant Boletus flaviporus mushrooms in the woods by Lake Cachuma today. They have viscid reddish-brown caps and intensely yellow gills that do not stain blue. They are striking mushrooms that are endemic to the west coast. They are said to be edible, but mediocre. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 20 December 2011)

The stems sure seem reticulate (like a net) at the top - see my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: bc20b_bc20-019.jpg
Notes:
68358021 This photo is looking close at the stem of a beautiful Boletus flaviporus mushroom in the woods by Lake Cachuma today. It has brilliant yellow pores (not gills) that do not stain blue when handled. See my [Next] photo. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 20 December 2011)

The stem is said to be NOT truly reticulate "like that of B. edulis and B. fibrillosus, but the tubes may form a slight raised network at the stem apex." (David Aurora, Mushrooms Demystified, 1986 - my mushroom bible!) Well, they sure look reticulate - like a net!
ScientificName: Boletus flaviporus; B. edulis; B. fibrillosus; Mushrooms Demystified
associatedTaxa: Boletus flaviporus; B. edulis; B. fibrillosus; Mushrooms Demystified

File.Name: bc20c_bc17-013.jpg
Notes:
68357996 These are very dark Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) trees in the woods where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. You can see a leaf and acorn cups of the tanoak in the foreground. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

I showed this same cluster a week ago. They still don't have honey colors:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68323689

Compare my photo of a different cluster of the same (?) mushrooms at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68331748
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; Coast Live Oak; Tanbark Oak
ScientificName: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Lithocarpus densiflorus
associatedTaxa: Armillariella mellea; Quercus agrifolia; Lithocarpus densiflorus

File.Name: bc20d_bc16-032.jpg
Notes: 68357974 These are little genus Lepiota mushrooms growing with new annual seedlings along the road in our canyon. There are many species and I won't try to guess, but they all have a cap with a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands, making them easy to recognize. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

File.Name: bc20e_bc16-004.jpg
Notes:
68357947 This is a remarkable blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods. (It's not that my white balance is off!) You can see the exquisite cobwebby veil - the "cortina" - that covers the gills tearing free, stained rusty-orange from the falling spores. (San Marcos Pass, 16 December 2011)

I don't know what this mushroom is, but Blue Cortinarius seems a fine name. It's in the sub-genus Bulbopodium with a viscid cap and abrupt swollen base. The problem is that all cortinarius mushrooms turn dingy brown with age and lose the brilliant colors. There are lots of species, which you can't tell apart. It's probably not edible.

This is probably the cortinarius that sometimes looks so much like our edible Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms. DON'T confuse them!

File.Name: bc20f_bc17-023.jpg
Notes:
68357857 Mushrooms open up mysterious places when they come up in the woods!

This is a White Russula (Russula albidula or kin) mushroom. It's pushing up a "mushrump" in the woods. (That's David Arora's felicitous phrase.) You can imagine tiny elves living under there. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2011)

File.Name: bc21a_bc21-049.jpg
Notes:
68361748 Oh boy - I found this delicious King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom in the woods today! It's also known as It's also known as porcini, cËpe, steinpilz, and others. I like the Italian name porcini the best - "little pig" because that's what it feels like, so solid and dense! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

Like all Boletes, this one has pores instead of gills. The fresh pores are white and "stuffed" - not yet open to the air. This mushroom is prime, except for that yellow streak that may be the start of an infestation of the Bolete Eater (Hypomyces chrysospermum) fungus that turns them into yellow mush. We'll trim that part before we eat it.

They are said to usually occur under conifers, but here it is under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia).

This was a good find for the Winter Solstice at 9:30 PST tonight. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting longer tomorrow in our mountains north of the Equator, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow.
Locality:
Quercus agrifolia).

This was a good find for the Winter Solstice at 9:30 PST tonight. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting longer tomorrow in our mountains north of the Equator
CommonNames: King Bolete; Bolete Eater; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Boletus edulis; porcini; cËpe; steinpilz; porcini; Boletes; Hypomyces chrysospermum; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Boletus edulis; porcini; cËpe; steinpilz; porcini; Boletes; Hypomyces chrysospermum; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: bc21b_bc21-033.jpg
Notes:
68361690 Here is another in situ photo of the dangerous Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom that I found in the woods today. Here you can see the brown cap with striate margin and white veil-fragments on top. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

This mushroom is quite poisonous. It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. It should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: in situ; Amanita pantherina; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: in situ; Amanita pantherina; A. muscaria

File.Name: bc21c_bc21-035.jpg
Notes:
68361646 I believe this is a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom in the woods today, on a bed of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. The white warts on the cap are fragments of the universal veil that once covered the whole mushroom and forms a volva at the base of the stalk. Here you can see the free white gills, ring on the stem, and cup-like volva with a free rim at the base. See my [Next] photo for more. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

This mushroom is quite poisonous. It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. It should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria

File.Name: bc21d_bc21-051.jpg
Notes:
68361596 These are beautiful Cortinarius mushrooms with violet colors. I guess they are part of the Cortinarius sodagnitus group, at least that's a handy name to remember for these mushrooms with violet colors. They are definitely in the Bulbopodium subgenus of the genus Cortinarius with a viscid cap, a non-viscid stem, and rimmed basal bulb. You can see a remnant of the "cortina" veil of hairs that once covered and protected the immature gills, now turned orange from falling spores. It may be edible - but you can't reliably tell cortinarius mushrooms apart. As they age, they all look the same. Beautiful mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

This is NOT the deep blue cortinarius mushroom that looks like a blewitt. See my photo yesterday:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68357947

File.Name: bc21e_bc21-013.jpg
Notes:
68361474 This is a hefty mushroom - my knife is about 3 1/2 inches long, so this mushroom is dinner plate-size or larger!

I believe it is a Sordid Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus sordidus) mushroom. They are rather boring, with no interesting veils or smells or colors. They are said to be edible but not choice. Mushroom author David Arora describes it as "slugulose". The gills are soft, and they don't feel particularly waxy to me. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

File.Name: bc21f_bc21-003.jpg
Notes: 68361406 This is a little white-spored Mycena mushroom growing above our retaining wall. It's hard to get white-balance when it's so dim out, but tonight at 9:30 PST is the Winter Solstice. The days will start getting longer tonight in our mountains north of the Equator, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

File.Name: bc22a_bc22-013.jpg
Notes:
68365355 This is a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf catching spores from a cascade of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods today - like snow. You can see the spore accumulation grow from top to bottom. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2011)

I've been showing lots of mushrooms here lately, but that's what there is in the woods right now. Enjoy!

File.Name: bc22b_bc22-020.jpg
Notes:
68365312 Here is a pretty new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing on dead wood in the woods. They are quite poisonous, and have a bitter taste to deter accidents. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2011)

For some reason, these mushrooms are especially hard to photograph in our dim winter-solstice light. They look brighter yellow than my photo shows, though they do have the greenish tint. Yellow is always the most difficult color with digital cameras.

File.Name: bc22c_bc21-029.jpg
Notes: 68365278 You have to look close at this photo to get it. Look at that black space near the bottom, then right above it. This is a "Little Brown Mushroom" (LBM - maybe genus Inocybe?) pushing up a whole living world as it emerges through the native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the woods. There are mosses and lichens and seedling plants uprooted in this upheaval! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

File.Name: bc22d_bc22-003.jpg
Notes:
68365203 We have many kinds of Russula mushrooms in the woods at home. They come in many colors from white to deep red, but these violet-capped ones are uncommon. I believe this is the Variegated Russula (Russula cyanoxantha) which is said to be edible and sweet - but maybe it's some other Russula that is spicy hot and poisonous if you eat more than a nibble! It's growing just up the hillside at home. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2011)

I found the same kind of mushrooms growing in the same place last year:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65893044

I'm very conservative with the mushrooms I collect for the table. Chanterelles of course, honey mushrooms, the occasional agaricus, and a few special boletes are about it. But I sure do enjoy finding and photographing and studying them!

File.Name: bc22e_bc21-063.jpg
Notes:
68365056 Yum! Here are slices of a delicious King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom sautÈing with kale and garlic in a bit of olive oil and lemon juice for dinner last night - served with lamb chops. It doesn't get much better than this! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

This is one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! See my [Previous] photos for more info.

File.Name: bc22f_bc21-058.jpg
Notes: 68364976 This is the same delicious King Bolete (Boletus edulis) mushroom as my [Previous] photo, sliced for dinner along with kale and garlic. It has some yellow spots that we cut out, but there were no worms at all. It is remarkably solid and white, like an apple or potato. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2011)

File.Name: bc23a_bc23-019.jpg
Notes: 68369034 Here are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. You can see the thin white "frost" on the caps, and a white "spore print" on the cap of the mushroom on the right. The velvety caps and wide-spaced gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2011) Our son Ryan came home from Humboldt tonight for the holidays! I'll do what I can for photos tomorrow.

File.Name: bc23b_bc23-006.jpg
Notes:
68369010 It's always fun to try to spot the smallest mushroom in the woods. This isn't the record, but it is small.

I guess these are tiny white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing above our retaining wall. They seem to be growing from old acorn cups. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm tip. The mycelium hairs on the stem and cap are remarkable. I reckon they are the remains of a partial veil. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2011)

File.Name: bc23c_bc23-010.jpg
Notes: 68368940 I believe these are the Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms in our garden. I sliced the gills of the young cap on the bottom, and you can see that it has watery white latex. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of L. fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2011)

File.Name: bc23d_bc23-013.jpg
Notes: 68368865 I believe these are the "Nitrous Entoloma" (Entoloma nidorosum) or kin. They have a surprising chlorine smell like bleach or a swimming pool, and they are definately NOT EDIBLE. You wouldn't want to try one. They have unusual "pink" flesh-colored spores that are not visible in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2011)

File.Name: bc23e_bc22-004.jpg
Notes:
68368767 I guess this is the Red-Cracked Bolete (Boletus chrysenteron or kin) mushroom in the woods under the oaks that I found in the wood today - but cp. B. subtomentosus and B. dryophilus. The cap on the right is cracked, but not yet red-stained. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2011)

This bolete has blue-staining yellow pores instead of gills, and a stem that's mostly yellow above and reddish below. It's edible, but not choice - said to be "mushy and insipid". I haven't tried it - there are better boletes in the woods!
ScientificName: Boletus chrysenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. dryophilus
associatedTaxa: Boletus chrysenteron; B. subtomentosus; B. dryophilus

File.Name: bc23f_bc22-009.jpg
Notes:
68368624 The Grateful Dead had the "wall of sound", and we have a wall of mushrooms!

This is a mass of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods yesterday. They are growing from a stump in the woods that might be a native Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2011)

The mushrooms are edible and tasty if you get them before the worms do. They get their common name from the color, not the taste.

File.Name: bc24a_bc24-024.jpg
Notes:
68372019 Santa will surely come visit these beautifully-decorated Christmas trees tonight!

Here are thousands of Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus hanging on the non-native Eucalyptus trees on the afternoon before Christmas eve. The monarch butterflies from all the western states congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. This is one of the world's great migrations. (Ellwood Mesa, 24 December 2011)

We went to Ellwood Mesa this afternoon to visit the butterflies and hike to the beach for a super-low tide of -1.6 feet. Time got away from me. Three photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: bc24b_bc24-034.jpg
Notes: 68372013 We went to the beach below Ellwood Mesa this afternoon for the super-low tide of -1.6 feet. There were lots of beachcombers prowling around the pools at low tide, including this Great Egret (Ardea alba)! (Ellwood Mesa, 24 December 2011)

File.Name: bc24c_bc24-029.jpg
Notes:
68372000 These are interesting Amanita mushroom look-alikes.

They are Volvariella speciosa mushrooms in the Pluteacea family growing in the iceplant by Elwood Mesa today. They look like Amanita with a "death cup" on the stem - but they don't have a ring and they have pink spores, and they don't have "amanita vibes" (really!). They are said to be edible - but be very careful you don't confuse them with a deadly Amanita mushrooms! People have died making this mistake. (Ellwood Mesa, 24 December 2011)

File.Name: bc25a_bc25-016.jpg
Notes:
68375030 I got cockroaches, wasps, flies, and a spider for Christmas - just what I wanted!

I'm sure this is a native Cockroach (order Blattodea) in the woods today. I found it in the oak leaves across the canyon, and it "played dead" on a bit of wood, allowing me a chance for a photo. Browsing at BugGuide, I think it might be a "Hairy Desert Cockroach" (Eremoblatta subdiaphana), though I don't know anything else about it or what it's doing here in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

I'm sure this is NOT one of those pesky roaches in Santa Barbara apartments! This critter is definitely a native who belongs in our woods.

You can tell at a glance where I got my photos today. Those with a bright background (like this one) are across the canyon on the south-facing sunny side; while those with a bluish background are close to home, where the winter sun never shines at this time of year. But the sun is slowly getting higher after the solstice, and the days are getting longer!
Locality: like this one) are across the canyon on the south-facing sunny side; while those with a bluish background are close to home

File.Name: bc25b_bc25-008.jpg
Notes: 68374862 This is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. They were out today in the dead of winter rummaging around for *something* in the oak leaves. It warmed up a few degrees last night, and the insects noticed. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

File.Name: bc25c_bc25-010.jpg
Notes:
68374840 This is a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a grass stem by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

I got this photo on the sunny side our canyon today, across the creek. Our home is on the north-facing slope of our east-west mountains with no sun at all this close to the solstice. Here on the south-facing side of the canyon, it's very different!

File.Name: bc25d_bc23-007.jpg
Notes:
68374789 Here is a small (but not tiny) fly (order Diptera) resting on a dead grass stem in the native part of our garden this morning. Judging from the colors and mouthparts, I guess it's a Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae. It has wonderful eyes. Maybe it's been visiting the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) just up the hill? (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

These flies love the mushrooms. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66075196

File.Name: bc25e_bc25-004.jpg
Notes:
68374707 This is a tiny spider with prey (an even tinier beetle) on the inner circle of its little web in our shaded garden this morning. It has an orb-web, so I guess it's an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. This spider has remarkable translucent "wings" on the sides of its cephalothorax. I guess it's a Christmas present for the spider - but not the beetle! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

I cheated and used my on-camera flash for this photo. I have another photo of the same spider here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68346396

File.Name: bc25f_bc25-001.jpg
Notes:
68374587 This tiny fly (order Diptera) was perched on a leaf in the native part of our garden this morning. If I had to guess what kind of fly it is, I'd say it's a Fungus Gnat (Mycetophilidae) - but that's partly because I'm a mycophile myself! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. Its a tight crop. The fly is about 240 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.6x240/4288 = 1.3 mm. This fly is really tiny!

I got this photo without flash in our shady garden this morning. We get no direct sun at all this close to the solstice on the north-facing side of our east-west mountains. Our house is winter-cold and summer-hot, but good for mushrooms!

File.Name: bc26a_bc26-032.jpg
Notes:
68378754 This is a beautiful golden Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working rosy new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. This is an interesting bee without the usual black bands on its abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2011)

It was warm today, and very dry - though there was still ice on the ground on my hike this morning near Knapp's Castle. We're stuck in a "La Nina" weather pattern with a clockwise swirl of high pressure stretching from Washington into Mexico like a window in the clouds. It's keeping the storms away, and new mushrooms are getting scarce. We need rain.

File.Name: bc26b_bc24-004.jpg
Notes: 68378666 This is an unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) caught in a spider web. I found this above our retaining wall yesterday, but the bee could have been trapped months ago. It's so interesting that the bee's antennae have been removed and moved back behind its wings! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

File.Name: bc26c_bc26-003.jpg
Notes:
68378606 Here are new "flowers" of a native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family just starting to open. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2011)

This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The flowers of this particular shrub will only produce pollen. The female flowers come later. They appear to be wind-pollinated, at least they release pollen when I shake the plant.

File.Name: bc26d_bc25-023.jpg
Notes: 68378522 Here are interesting small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. They might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. They are starting to produce umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction. Those tongue-like structures with little bumps on the lower and right leaves are maybe the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. Liverworts are primitive but complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

File.Name: bc26e_bc25-020.jpg
Notes:
68378292 When I can't find anything new to photograph in the woods, I like to find a sunny spot and sit and wait to see what comes along.

This is a little Wolf Spider (Lycosidae) on a sandstone outcrop near our still-dry creek yesterday. They chase their prey on the ground. They spin no web and use their silk only to make an egg case. There are many different kinds. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

I'm sure the little "black dots" on the rock are a lichen.

File.Name: bc26f_bc25-012.jpg
Notes: 68378172 This is another photo of the native "Hairy Desert Cockroach" (Eremoblatta subdiaphana) in the insect order Blattodea that I found in the woods yesterday. Here you can see it's beetle-like head and spiny legs. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

File.Name: bc27a_bc27-010.jpg
Notes:
68382718 This is a tiny (but adorable!) Jumping Spider (Salticidae) peering at my camera from a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek today. It's remarkable to make eye contact with anything so small! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2011)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. Its a tight crop. The spider is about 245 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.6x245/4288 = 1.3 mm across. This cute spider is really tiny!

File.Name: bc27b_bc27-018.jpg
Notes:
68382673 I found these very colorful Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) insects in the order Homoptera resting on leaves of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon today. I think they may be the "Blue-green Sharpshooter" (Graphocephala atropunctata) insect. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2011)

Compare the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/15889/bgimage

This little critter is considered a serious pest of grapes. They vector the bacterium Xylella fastidiosa which causes Pierce's disease in grapes, which are an important crop in the Santa Ynez Valley.

At least they aren't the notorious Glassy-winged Sharpshooter (Homalodisca vitripennis). Fear of that insect created an embargo on carrying plants across the Santa Ynez Mountains. It created a problem for our Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society annual plant sale.
CommonNames: Leafhopper; Wild Sweetpea; Blue-green Sharpshooter; Pierce's disease; Glassy-winged Sharpshooter
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Graphocephala atropunctata; Xylella fastidiosa; Homalodisca vitripennis
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Graphocephala atropunctata; Xylella fastidiosa; Homalodisca vitripennis

File.Name: bc27c_bc27-014.jpg
Notes: 68382550 This is a little spider sitting in an "open boat" seed capsule of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. It has its legs hanging lazily over the side, but look closer. It seems to be holding an old insect prey with its lowest leg. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2011)

File.Name: bc27d_bc27-002.jpg
Notes:
68382507 There's a new cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in our woods. These are growing from an old stump of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2011)

For gardeners, these honey-mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "Oak Root Fungus". They seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps. (Knock on wood!)
CommonNames: Honey Mushrooms; California Bay; Oak Root Fungus

File.Name: bc27e_bc24-021.jpg
Notes:
68382380 This is looking inside a broken mushroom of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushroom. It was growing in the woods on a dead stump. You can see the purple-gray spores from its greenish gills being trapped by the partial veil. I really like this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2011)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous, and have a bitter taste to deter accidents.

File.Name: bc27f_bc26-034.jpg
Notes: 68382311 This must be Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) heaven! This bee is suckling from an urn-like flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. It's furry eyes seem half closed with pleasure - but I don't think they have eyelids! You can see by it's empty pollen baskets that it's going for nectar rather than pollen. The hummingbirds like these flowers too, though they are anything but "hummingbird flowers". (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2011)

File.Name: bc28a_bc28-015.jpg
Notes:
68386596 This is a slender Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a late native flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has a curved abdomen like a wasp. If you look close, you can see the "spurious vein" on its wings. Maybe this critter is genus Ocyptamus? (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

It was sunny and beautiful today, and warm tonight - almost 60. The crickets are singing and the Northern Saw-whet Owl (Aegolius acadicus) is singing in the canyon. Beautiful, but it's not right. It should be raining tonight!
Locality: <i>Aegolius acadicus<i>) is singing in the canyon.  Beautiful
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; California Aster; Northern Saw-whet Owl
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Ocyptamus
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae; Ocyptamus

File.Name: bc28b_bc28-009.jpg
Notes:
68386533 These are beautiful new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I think my favorite part is is the pinhead-dotted calyces and bracts in the whorls of the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

These flowers have two long-exserted stamens and a single long-exserted pistil, as you can see on the flower on the right. So what is that dingus hanging down in the flower on the left like a serpent-tongued epiglottis in the throat of the flower??

File.Name: bc28c_bc28-020.jpg
Notes:
68386401 I found this odd little diurnal (daytime) Moth (Ledpidoptera) on new flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family today. Judging from the elongated mouth parts, it might be a Crambid Snout Moth in the family Crambidae - but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

The individual flowers of Ceanothus are tiny, but in a good year you can see them from the beach, like snow on the mountains! It remains to be seen if this will be a good year.
CommonNames: Moth; BigPod Ceanothus; Crambid Snout Moth
ScientificName: Ledpidoptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Crambidae; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Ledpidoptera; Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Crambidae; Ceanothus

File.Name: bc28d_bc28-008.jpg
Notes: 68386285 I believe this is a Russula densifolia or R. albonigra mushroom in the woods today. It's as hard as a rock! The first flush of mushrooms is just about done, and the dry weather has put the kibosh on new mushrooms. I'm hoping for rain! (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

File.Name: bc28e_bc28-004.jpg
Notes:
68386178 I guess this is a tiny Midge in the family Chironomidae with those plumose antennae. I got this photo above our retaining wall this morning. It's a convenient shoulder-height to look for tiny critters. Too bad the sun never shines here in winter. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. Its a tight crop. The fly is about 380 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.6x380/4288 = 2.1 mm. This fly is really tiny!

This might be one of the little flies that "dance in the sunbeams". See my photo (and click [Previous] to that) at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57754891

File.Name: bc28f_bc27-009.jpg
Notes:
68386004 I can't resist posting one more photo of tiny (but adorable!) Jumping Spider (Salticidae) that I found on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek yesterday. I'm sure it's still making eye-contact with *some* of those many eyes! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2011)

This spider's eyes seem to change color from photo to photo, from red to green. I guess it's like a camera "red-eye" thing, but I'm not sure. Maybe these spiders have a tapetum lucidum - an iridescent layer of tissue behind the retina that reflects light back onto the source to improve night vision, like a cat? (I don't trust the web sources I've found.) Either way, they have amazing eyes!

I did a stumper about this years ago:
   http://www.rain.org/~mkummel/stumpers/20oct00a.html
Locality: I don't trust the web sources I've found.)  Either way

File.Name: bc29a_bc29-005.jpg
Notes:
68390288 Here is a beautiful Honey Mushroom (Armillariella mellea) dropping her veil in the woods this morning. The partial veil of these mushrooms covers and protects the immature gills, and falls away to leaves a ring on the stem. I found impressive new clusters of these mushrooms in the woods this morning. I'll post a photo tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)

It's another warm night with crickets and owls. I'm not complaining, but it's not right. The National Weather Service mentioned a chance of rain in a week. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: bc29b_bc29-039.jpg
Notes: 68390218 These are beautiful native Goldback Ferns (Pentagramma triangularis) growing with the mosses and lichens in the woods today. I turned a leaf over for the photo. You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)

File.Name: bc29c_bc29-015.jpg
Notes:
68390153 These are beautiful little Cladonia-type lichens growing with mosses in the woods today. I don't know what kind it is. Lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae. These have a "foliose" base, but "fruticose" stems. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)

Lichens have always been a problem for taxonomy because they are actually two organisms -"Alice Algae and Freddy Fungus" as the story goes. The temporary solution is to classify lichens by the fungal component.

But the taxonomy problem may go deeper - how many different critters are YOU and I made from, all cooperating together? I suspect it's a lot! (Think about our intestinal flora for a start.) See the writings of the late Lynn Margulis for inspiration!

File.Name: bc29d_bc29-030.jpg
Notes: 68390016 Maybe these little puffballs are Dark Puffballs (Lycoperdon foetidum). They are almost black, but they don't have much of a sterile base, so my ID is tentative at best. There are lots of different kinds of puffball mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)

File.Name: bc29e_bc29-029.jpg
Notes: 68389936 This is probably the "Boring Brown Bolete" (Boletus subtomentosus) mushroom growing in the woods, showing the tooth-marks of the rodent who ate it. It has rather large yellow pores instead of gills. It doesn't stain red or blue much. Look how the stem is coarsely reticulate with pretty yellow outlines. It's said to be edible, but definitely not choice. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)

File.Name: bc29f_bc28-013.jpg
Notes:
68389828 Here's another photo of the beautiful little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) I featured yesterday. It's probably genus Ocyptamus, maybe Ocyptamus lemur. It hovered in front of the flower hesitantly for some time before landing, and I got the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2011)

The fly is visiting a late-season native flower of California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look how neatly the fly folds its hind legs in flight!
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Ocyptamus; Ocyptamus lemur; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Ocyptamus; Ocyptamus lemur; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: bc30a_bc30-031.jpg
Notes:
68393953 This is a little (I guess) Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera that I found in the bathroom last night attracted to the light. It looks like a big ant. I trapped it under a glass until this afternoon, and then tipped it out onto the porch railing in the sun for a photo. It was surprisingly active when I released it, but it calmed down in my hand. Here it is resting on my ring finger, with my well-worn gold wedding ring in the lower-left. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

(Nah, my fingers aren't that pink. It must be a white balance thing!)

Browsing at BugGuide, I think it might be Ipochus fasciatus in the tribe Parmenini. Look at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/145039/bgpage

File.Name: bc30b_bc30-010.jpg
Notes: 68393866 This is a furry bee-like Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon today. It's a syrphid fly with fur! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

File.Name: bc30c_bc30-006.jpg
Notes:
68393837 Here are new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. They are remarkably intricate! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

These trees have amazing nuts inside their avocado-like fruit. We roasted the nuts for the first time this year, and they are truly delicious - something between bitter dark chocolate and roasted coffee beans! I have photos of the process which I've been saving for a rainy day... if it ever comes!

File.Name: bc30d_bc30-014.jpg
Notes:
68393747 This a handsome Golden Milky Cap (Lactarius alnicola) mushroom in the woods growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I didn't pick this mushroom, but I sliced the gills with my knife to show the white latex "milk". It has zoned cap and a spicy taste that not quite pleasant. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

Mushrooms are getting scarce in our woods because it's been so dry this month, but there are still a few surprises!
CommonNames: Golden Milky Cap; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: bc30e_bc30-021.jpg
Notes:
68393699 I think I made a boo-boo putting these mushrooms together for this photo.

The mushroom on the left is definitely the Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina). The mushroom on the right had yellow colors on it's cap, so I assumed it was a cross between A. pantherina and the Jonquil Amanita (A. gemmata), which happens in our woods.

But now I think that mushroom on the right is another deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom. It could have been a fatal mistake, but this was no problem - they are BOTH potentially deadly mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

Mistakes happen - let this be a lesson!
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Jonquil Amanita; Death Cap
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; A. pantherina; A. gemmata; Amanita phalloides
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; A. pantherina; A. gemmata; Amanita phalloides

File.Name: bc30f_bc30-005.jpg
Notes: 68393555 Here are more beautiful Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods, growing from an old stump of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. They are so stately! (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2011)

File.Name: bc31a_bc31-003.jpg
Notes:
68397085 This interesting little diurnal (daytime) moth is a Pyrausta dapalis in the Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. In this photo, you can see it sticking its proboscis out past its "snout mouth" onto the stem of an oak leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2011)

They have brilliant red hind wings, like little underwing moths. I suspect they are pretty common at this time of year, just rarely noticed. When they fold their wings up, they almost disappear!

I may have another recent photo of a different Crambid Snout Moth here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68386401

File.Name: bc31b_bc31-006.jpg
Notes:
68397062 This is the first new butterfly of the year! (Never mind the calendar.) I believe it's a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. Its a kind of "Blue" butterfly, and you can glimpse a bit of blue on its upper wings in my photo. I kept hoping it would turn to profile, but instead it stuck its tongue out at me and took off! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2011)

Our dry weather is continuing. Last year we had 24.5 inches of rain by now; this year we've had just 5.7 inches. We're running at 56% normal. It's bad for mushrooms and plants, but maybe a dry year after a wet year will be good for the butterflies?

File.Name: bc31c_bc31-014.jpg
Notes:
68397030 Ready for the flood!

This is a gorgeous little spider sitting in an "open boat" seed capsule of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. I suspect from its web that it's a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. I watched it catch a tiny fly and immediately retreat to the boat. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2011)

Notice that bundle of "insect parts" hanging over the side of the boat. Maybe it's a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) hanging its waste in a different place?
CommonNames: Bush Monkey Flower; Orb-Weaver Spider; Garbage Line Spider

File.Name: bc31d_bc31-018.jpg
Notes:
68397017 Here is another handsome little Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) insect in the order Homoptera resting on the furry stem of a native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family in the canyon today. I don't know anything about these tiny insects, but they sure are attractive. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2011)

They have amazing eyes - a trait they share with Jumping Spider (Salticidae). I guess these tiny critters which jump need to see where they're going!

I don't have any idea why it has such spiny hind legs.
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Salticidae
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Salticidae

File.Name: bc31e_bc29-019.jpg
Notes: 68397005 This is a small black fly (order Diptera) working new flowers of Big-berry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. It looks pretty bristly, so it may be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae, but I really don't know. I'm interested in these native pollinators other than the ubiquitous introduced Honey Bees. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2011)
CommonNames: fly; Big-berry Manzanita; Tachinid Fly; Honey Bees

File.Name: bc31f_bc20-014.jpg
Notes:
68396994 This amazing mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae is guarding another egg sack - maybe her third this year! She is perched on a no-longer-flowering stem of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. She deserves some kind of lifetime achievement award! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2011)

These photos probably each show the same spider in the same plant!
   Nov. 30 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68179605
   Sep. 30 - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68026682
ScientificName: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae; Hazardia squarrosa; Asteraceae

File.Name: a101a_9c31-121.jpg
Notes:
57958731 Raindrops on native Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) in the canyon - reflecting the woods. I have no idea how the plant gets it's name. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

We went out of the old year with a bit of rain. It was drizzly all day, perfect for raindrops on the leaves. I'm hoping for a rainy El Nino spring!

File.Name: a101b_9c31-124.jpg
Notes:
57958110 Like the jeweled tiara for a princess!

This is a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family growing in the canyon. Nothing catches raindrops like a lupine leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

File.Name: a101c_9c31-134.jpg
Notes:
57957640 Here's a little white-spored Mycena mushroom growing in the woods - with raindrops. We had just a bit of rain two days ago, but it was drizzly all day and enough to dampen the top layer of the woods that these critters call home. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2009)

Darn that pesky blade of grass!

File.Name: a102a_a102-013.jpg
Notes:
57988734 Beautiful new flowers of well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2010)

Like many natives, they are early this year. According to my records, I usually find these flowers in February or March - and on into April - but they are at the beginning of January. But I have one even earlier record from December!

Well, we had an early rain - 10 inches on October. Some plants sprouted early and others didn't. I guess we'll have an interesting wildflower year with early plants and the later sprouters.

My concern is that the insect pollinators aren't ready to visit flowers yet. There are very few insects on the wing, but these early flowers are ready for attention!

File.Name: a102b_a102-019.jpg
Notes:
57988216 Here's another flower that is appearing early this year - Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2010)

According to my records, I usually find these flowers in February or March - and on into May - but here it is at the beginning of January. Well, we had an early rain - 10 inches on October. Some plants sprouted early and others didn't. I guess we'll have an interesting wildflower year!

My concern is that the insect pollinators aren't ready to visit flowers yet. There are very few insects on the wing, but these early flowers are ready for attention!

File.Name: a102c_a101-178.jpg
Notes:
57987530 Here is a wonderful mushroom-like plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This early-flowering native has mostly red leaves with just a bit of green. Other plants already have flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2010)

It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It looks mushroom-like coming up fully-formed from the earth, and it probably has a fungal connection with its host plant.

I usually find the tubular red flowers in February, but it looks to be early this year - like many other natives.
Locality: Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. It looks mushroom-like coming up fully-formed from the earth

File.Name: a103a_a102-031.jpg
Notes:
58016635 I believe these are "Fan Pax" (Paxillus panuoides - aka Tapinella panuoides) mushrooms growing from a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2010)

It's interesting that these are pretty common on the Kinevin Ranch property on the other side of the highway where I hiked yesterday, but I've never seen them in our canyon.

These are three different wood-decomposing "mushrooms" that I've posted photos of today. It brings to mind my stumper for the holidays:
