"The rains are bringing out wild mushrooms. I'll be stalking them over vacation! A mushroom is the fruiting body of the hidden fungus mycelium that lives in the ground or other substrate, like the apple on an apple tree. They release tiny spores that can cover the Earth riding on air currents. They have fantastic shapes, colors, smells, textures, and tastes that range from gourmet to deadly poison. I have two questions. If the spores are so tiny, why don't we find the same mushrooms everywhere? If they really just release spores, why are they so beautiful and diverse?"

I don't really have an answer to this stumper - I reckon a good question is better than an answer! But I'll share my answer tomorrow.
ScientificName: Paxillus panuoides; Tapinella panuoides; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Paxillus panuoides; Tapinella panuoides; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: a103b_a103-083.jpg
Notes:
58015193 Here is another strange fungus growing from rotting wood that is not really a mushroom because it is not at all fleshy.

This is the Elegant Polypore aka Black-Foot (Polyporus elegans) growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch. It really is a polypore fungus, but it grows on a stalk rather than as a shelf on decomposing wood. It's very tough, but it dries nicely for decorative purposes! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)
CommonNames: Elegant Polypore; Black-Foot; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: a103c_a103-075.jpg
Notes:
58014472 I don't think these qualify as "mushrooms" - they are the fruiting bodies of fungi, but they are not by any stretch of the imagination fleshy!

These are the fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin). They are very tough - like the wood they grow from, only more so. The ripe fruiting bodies are about an inch tall. They look like tiny moose antlers when mature! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

File.Name: a104a_a104-089.jpg
Notes:
58047369 Here are beautiful new flowers of the Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing on the pass. I did no color correction on this photo. The hanging deep-red flowers are classic hummingbird flowers. Maybe this is why my hummers have been avoiding the feeder lately? (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2010)

This is another native plant that is flowering early this year on the pass. I've found the first flowers before in February, but never in January.

File.Name: a104b_a104-092.jpg
Notes:
58046527 Strange new flower of a California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing on the pass. It's early - like many wildflowers in this odd rain year - but I've found them in January before. They "should" flower in February and March. I guess they got an early start from our unusual 10 inches of rain in October. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2010)

The plants usually hide under the chaparral, but these plants are growing in a place that was cleared (and "masticated") last summer as part of a fire break. The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb.

File.Name: a104c_a102-033.jpg
Notes:
58045791 This is the same mushroom as my [Previous] photo, though the young version doesn't look anything like the old caps. I'm pretty sure these are "Fan Pax" (Paxillus panuoides - aka Tapinella panuoides) mushrooms growing from a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2010)

You can see an old cap in the upper-left. I pulled away the bark to expose this mushroom, and you can see that it is turning the oak wood into mush.
ScientificName: Paxillus panuoides; Tapinella panuoides; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Paxillus panuoides; Tapinella panuoides; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: a105a_a103-079.jpg
Notes:
58082030 Here is a beautiful but deadly Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) mushroom in the woods. The caps are smooth and polished, mostly yellow-colored in ours. You can see the "skirt" of the partial veil that once covered the immature gills. You can't see the "death cup" at the base of the stem in this photo, but I'm sure it's there. They have "Amanita vibes"! Enjoy the beauty, but don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

The Death Caps are having a good year in our woods. This is interesting because the delicious Chanterelles are having a bad year - I haven't found a single one, or seen any in the local markets.

*Sigh* I went back to teaching school today after a fine winter vacation. Still gotta pay the bills!

File.Name: a105b_a103-061.jpg
Notes:
58081237 I'm pretty sure these mushrooms I found in the woods on Sunday are the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis). Like all Agaricus mushrooms, they have chocolate-brown spores, though the gills look quite pallid in this photo. They lack the "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. There is a thick felty ring on the stem on the lower mushroom, but look how the ring has torn on the upper one. When this cap fully opens, the ring will hardly be noticeable. This is what makes mushroom collecting interesting! You have to read the clues...

These mushrooms look delicious, but they are poisonous to many people. They have an unpleasant phenol-like taste. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

File.Name: a105c_a103-064.jpg
Notes:
58080021 This is how I actually find most mushrooms in the woods - you notice them from the side but not straight down, because they are hidden by the mounds of leaves - "mushrumps" - they push up. That's a hint - look for the mushrumps!

I'm pretty sure this is Russula emetica or kin. It looks beautiful, and it has an interesting hot spicy taste if you just nibble. But as the name suggests, it is quite poisonous. Not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. There is a rumor that parboiling destroys the toxins. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

File.Name: a106a_a106-104.jpg
Notes:
58115836 Here is another beautiful new flower of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2010)

The flowers usually face the Earth - I propped this on up into the light with a stick. The perennial roots used to be collected locally for export to China as a medicinal herb.

I have lots more fantastic mushroom photos from winter vacation, but it's a pleasure to get some new photo in the sun! All our flowers are still early - before the insects that should pollinate them.

File.Name: a106b_a106-106.jpg
Notes:
58115272 Blue flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum Xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2010)

These plants are listed as a different variety because they are quite glabrous - without hairs. Compare my recent photo of the species plants near home:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57065584

Little structural differences like this mean a lot to the botanists who make the floras we depend on!

File.Name: a106c_a101-176.jpg
Notes: 58114365 Here is a beautiful plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family coming up like a mushroom in the woods. This one already has red tubular flowers. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2010)

File.Name: a107a_a101-165.jpg
Notes: 58153154 Here is a beautiful little brown-spored mushroom hiding in a mound of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. I guess from the white-trimmed gills and the little black specks on the cap that it is a Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin mushroom. I often find these mushrooms speckled with tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects stuck in the viscid caps when they dry. It smells a bit like a radish, but it's probably poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2010)

File.Name: a107b_a102-017.jpg
Notes: 58152397 Here are a troop of perfect little white-spored Mycena mushrooms growing from the fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves on the forest floor. These little mushrooms appear quickly after a rain, and disappear just as fast. They are as ephemeral as the rain this year. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2010)

File.Name: a107c_a106-100.jpg
Notes:
58152109 Here are unknown mushrooms coming up with the new grasses and wildflowers in the woods near Lake Cachuma. The spores are brown, but not rusty-brown like the more common Cortinarius clan. I guess this is the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox aka Pholiota praecox) or kin in the Bolbitaceae family, but I wouldn't bet the farm on it - or collect them for dinner! There's no sign of a ring on the stem. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2010)

The mushrooms are early in the woods just like the wildflowers. Last year I found these mushrooms growing in the same place in February.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Agrocybe praecox; Pholiota praecox; Bolbitaceae

File.Name: a108a_a108-173.jpg
Notes:
58198386 "And now for something completely different..."

We had a special concert after school today by Gilles Apap and the Transylvanian Mountain Boys! Gilles is a world-class violinist who also plays gypsy and folk tunes on the side. (A rare combination!) He was the Concertmaster of the Santa Barbara Symphony Orchestra for over a decade.

This is Gilles Apap's violin case.
"Oh! The Places Youíll Go!"
(Santa Ynez Valley, 8 January 2010)

Gilles Apap is a remarkable musician with many hats. You should watch all three of these YouTube videos to get an idea - but at least watch the second one:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i3STQBTk_D8
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HbiLBJMrymE
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OpVeNtem5Yk

File.Name: a108b_9c20-102.jpg
Notes: 58197203 Here is a beautiful Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods - the first I've seen this year. You can see the remains of the "cortina" hairs that once covered and protected all the immature gills, but now hang from the stalk. The hairs turn rusty-brown from the falling spores. You can also see some residue of the "slime veil" that once covered the whole mushroom and leaves bands of slime behind on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2009)

File.Name: a108c_9c20-113.jpg
Notes:
58196686 I'm not sure what this family of mushrooms are, but I've found them before in the same place. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/29825194

They have a distinctive funnel-shape. They are growing from fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves, which gives a sense of scale. I guess these are genus Clitocybe with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2009)

File.Name: a109a_a109-011.jpg
Notes: 58232500 Pretty spider hanging from her web in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. Maybe she's a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

File.Name: a109b_a109-001.jpg
Notes:
58232045 This is an early caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly on a Penstemon plant in the garden. The caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

Nature is crazy this year!

File.Name: a109c_a109-007.jpg
Notes:
58231577 We don't have a great convergence of these Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - so far this year, but there are a few in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

The last ladybug rave happened on October 17 after our great rain:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/55640438
I went back the next day and they were all gone.

File.Name: a110a_a109-021.jpg
Notes:
58258606 I believe these beautiful mushrooms are Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It's winter by the calendar, but they have autumn colors. You can see that the edge of the cap and the ring turn yellow with age or handling. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

Like all Agaricus mushrooms, they have chocolate-brown spores that color the gills. They have a skirtlike ring on the stem, with no "death cup" volva at the base.

These mushrooms have a pleasant sweet odor and taste, like anise or almond extract. Said to be edible, but be careful! Some people are allergic, and there are other yellow-staining agarics with a nasty phenol-disinfectant taste. I'm wary of all woodland Agaricus species. "Once bitten, twice shy.

File.Name: a110b_a109-026.jpg
Notes: 58257778 This handsome mushroom is part of the Cortinarius cylindripes group of mushrooms. It's a "Slimy Purple Cortinarius" according to my book, and it certainly is slimy - both the cap and the stem. That places it in the subgenus Myxacium. David Arora - the author of my Mushrooms Demystified book - says succinctly: "Better eyed than fried". (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

File.Name: a110c_a110-047.jpg
Notes:
58257249 It's not beautiful, but this is a mighty mushroom!

I believe this is the Manzanita Bolete (Leccinum manzanitae) mushroom growing under a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree - a manzanita relative in the Ericaceae plant family. Maybe you can see the dark scales ("scabers") on the stem. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2010)

When I went to pick this mushroom, I had to fight it - it was attached to a cabbage-sized mass in the ground! It's edible, but not the best.

File.Name: a111a_a109-034.jpg
Notes:
58299468 The light bulb mushroom!

This is a Gemmed Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the woods. I actually found this mushroom two weeks ago and pulled it from the ground for a photo, but it still looks fresh. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

Always "Replace Divots" - put your mushroom back in the same hole you got it from, and pat it down. It might re-attach to the mycelium. And it's a courtesy to other mushroom collectors who might follow you!

Blessed rain is in the forecast, and the frogs are singing tonight! How do they know?
ScientificName: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: a111b_a109-015.jpg
Notes:
58298833 This is a different Agaricus mushroom still in "button" stage with its intimate veil intact. I guess it is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom - partly because I recognize the structure of the veil, and mostly because I found it in the same place last year. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

Mushroom "buttons" are remarkable. They contain every cell of the final mushroom in reduced form. Just add water to bring them out!

File.Name: a111c_a110-046.jpg
Notes:
58298339 Here is an older Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or kin) mushroom growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees yesterday. You can see that the cap is stained quite yellow. Compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2010)

Like all Agaricus mushrooms, they have chocolate-brown spores that color the gills. They have a skirtlike ring on the stem, with no "death cup" volva at the base.

These mushrooms have a pleasant sweet odor and taste, like anise or almond extract. Said to be edible, but be careful! Some people are allergic, and there are other yellow-staining agarics with a nasty phenol-disinfectant taste. I'm wary of all woodland Agaricus species. "Once bitten, twice shy.

File.Name: a112a_a110-058.jpg
Notes:
58332924 These are the interesting new flowers of a native male Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Silk-Tassel (Garryaceae) plant family. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2010)

This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The flowers of this particular shrub will only produce pollen.

My photo-friend Lynn Watson has a nice page about this native plant at:
   http://natureshutterbug.com/wordpress/2010/01/12/a-plant-with-wintery-woollen-attire/

File.Name: a112b_a112-075.jpg
Notes:
58331662 Flowers of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family simply fall apart as they mature. First the petals go, then the stamens, and finally the heart-shaped yellow nectary-disk parts at the base of the flower. That will leave the large thick-walled seed-bearing follicles that are the real heart of this flower. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2010)

I got this photo today in the woods near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. The flowers usually face the Earth - I propped this one up into the light with a stick.

File.Name: a112c_a109-035.jpg
Notes: 58331042 Here is another "light bulb mushroom" - though it doesn't look much like a light bulb in this photo. This is a Gemmed Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the woods. It turns brown with age. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)
ScientificName: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Lycoperdon perlatum) mushroom growing under a California Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: a113a_a102-034.jpg
Notes:
58366671 This is one of our most beautiful mushrooms - the Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushroom in the woods among the fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Or is it a Chocolate Marasmius (Marasmius fuscopurpureus or kin) mushroom? I don't really care - it's beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

These little mushrooms have wiry stems and an interesting connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain.
CommonNames: Frosted Marasmius; Coast Live Oak; Chocolate Marasmius
ScientificName: Marasmius plicatulus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius fuscopurpureus
associatedTaxa: Marasmius plicatulus; Quercus agrifolia; Marasmius fuscopurpureus

File.Name: a113b_a109-031.jpg
Notes:
58366276 "Leaves of three, let it be.
Leaves of four, eat some more!" - Homer Simpson

The first shiny new leaves of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Sumac (Anacardiaceae) plant family are out! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits!

I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't usually get the rash. It's a good thing - considering how much time I spend in the woods!

File.Name: a113c_a109-032.jpg
Notes:
58366015 Just another fly (order Diptera) in the woods - but it's interesting that it's active on a warm day in the middle of winter when very few insects are on the wing! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

At first I was excited because I thought I recognized the fly's speckled abdomen, and it reminded me of a Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae) that I captured last fall:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/56425536

But no - I see that the eyes and antennae and bristles are completely different.

File.Name: a114a_a109-016.jpg
Notes: 58394266 Beautiful little sporophytes of a Moss (Bryophyta) plant growing in the canyon. The tiny sporophytes have a remarkable structure. They look perforated like little salt shakers to sprinkle out the invisible spores! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

File.Name: a114b_a109-024.jpg
Notes:
58393934 This is "looking under the skirts" of the Leptonia mushroom in the Entolomataceae that I show in my [Previous] photo. You can see some "pink" (actually flesh-colored) spore deposit on the stem. The beautiful well-spaced white gills show no hint of the spore color. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

This beautiful mushroom was growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods.

File.Name: a114c_a109-023.jpg
Notes:
58393421 Oh what a beautiful mushroom! It is charcoal black with a shiny black stem. I first thought it was a different kind of Marasmius because of the shiny, almost metallic stem, but it seems to have "pink" spores - see my [Next] photo. I guess this is a pink-spored Leptonia in the Entolomataceae. Maybe it is Leptonia parva or kin? It's growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

David Largent lists over 100 species of Leptonia in his monograph on the genus. I'm happy just to know the genus!
ScientificName: Marasmius; Leptonia; Entolomataceae; Leptonia parva; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Marasmius; Leptonia; Entolomataceae; Leptonia parva; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: a115a_a103-050.jpg
Notes:
58426401 Here is another large mushroom in the woods that has stumped me. I guess it is the genus Clitocybe with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk. You can see a natural spore print under the mushroom on the right. The tan caps are handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

I'm glad I can still be stumped by a mushroom after 40 years in these woods!

The National Weather Service says we are in for a week of rain starting tomorrow night - maybe 12-16 inches in the mountains. What a treat!

File.Name: a115b_a109-012.jpg
Notes:
58426154 This impressive Lactarius mushroom has a pink cap, a "scrobiculate" stem with darker or lighter spots, and white latex in the gills. The cap is "viscid" or slimy. It really doesn't look like a milky-cap with that large stem, but it definitely has "milk" in it's gills. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2010)

I don't know which Lactarius it is. I'm glad I can still be stumped by a mushroom after 40 years in these woods!

File.Name: a115c_9c31-114.jpg
Notes: 58425637 Strange fruiting bodies of "Fairy Fingers" (Clavaria vermicularis or kin) mushrooms growing in a pile of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves. My book says "edible but insubstantial" - sounds right. These little mushrooms wither away after the rain. They are all gone now, but we'll probably get a new crop next week - rain is coming! (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2010)

File.Name: a116a_a116-016.jpg
Notes:
58456334 This is looking under the cap of a beautiful Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today.

That's not really a "cortina" of hairs - it seems more like a membrane as the veil covering the gills of this mushroom. I just show what I find in the woods. Maybe it's the dry weather?

The forecast is still on track for as much as 20 inches of rain on the Pass starting tomorrow night. That's more rain than we had all last year. Yipes!
Locality:
58456334 This is looking under the cap of a beautiful Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today.

That's not really a "cortina" of hairs - it seems more like a membrane as the veil covering the gills of this mushroom. I just show what I find in the woods. Maybe it's the dry weather?

The forecast is still on track for as much as 20 inches of rain on the Pass starting tomorrow night. That's more rain than we had all last year. Yipes!

File.Name: a116b_a116-009.jpg
Notes: 58455945 This is the Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The unpleasant phenol-disinfectant smell (and worse taste) and the yellow-staining cap are signs that it's poisonous. The flesh at the very base of the stem turns bright yellow when nicked, as you can see in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

File.Name: a116c_a116-024.jpg
Notes: 58455735 I'm pretty sure this is "Poison Pie" - a Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin mushroom in the woods today. The caps are "viscid" - sticky - when it rains, but they dry out and catch the tiny Springtail (order Collembia) insects on the cap. Here it is with native grass and sedge in the woods. Don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

File.Name: a117a_a116-042.jpg
Notes:
58477524 A trio of beautiful black Leptonia mushrooms in the Entolomataceae. Maybe they are Leptonia parva or kin? They're growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. You can see some of the "pink" spores (really flesh-colored) on the stem and gills of the mushroom on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

It's a good thing I got photos yesterday, because the rain started early today - the first impulse of storms that may go on all week! I love the rain - and all that the rain brings to the woods. We live on top of the Pass, where the rain comes. I feel for those people who live down below - where the rain goes - facing possible mud slides from the burn areas.
ScientificName: Leptonia; Entolomataceae; Leptonia parva; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Leptonia; Entolomataceae; Leptonia parva; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: a117b_a116-044.jpg
Notes:
58476695 I guess this little brown mushroom is the Fragrant Inocybe (Inocybe pyriodora or kin). It has brown spores and an "umbonate" cap with a bump at the center. It also has an interesting sweet smell like overripe pears! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

Many Inocybes have interesting smells - like fruit or corn or chocolate or "spermatic". Otherwise they are a pretty boring group of LBMs - "little brown mushrooms". I've noticed that they often push up big "mushrumps" (mounds of leaves) for their small size - but sometimes the oder makes them worth digging up.

File.Name: a117c_a116-058.jpg
Notes:
58476053 I'm pretty sure these are the Deer Mushroom (Pluteus cervinus). They are growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump in the woods. These mushrooms are not young, but you can still see that they are "pink" spored - really flesh-colored. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

Heh - the exposure for this photo was 13 seconds @ f/22 @ ISO 200. It really was in a dark crack of the old tree roots.

This mushroom is edible if you can find younger specimens.
ScientificName: Pluteus cervinus). They are growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Pluteus cervinus). They are growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica

File.Name: a118a_a118-069.jpg
Notes:
58512413 The first new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae) plant family growing in our canyon picked a wet day to open! I've seen a few flowers on the drive down to Santa Barbara, but these are the first at home. (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2010)

We had a major winter storm yesterday and today. We got about 3.5 inches - on the low side of the 3-6 inches we were promised, but I'm not complaining! The rain was pretty intense, up to 1 inch/hour according to a neighbor's automatic rain gauge on the Web. Julie and I took a walk this afternoon when the rain was mostly cleared out and the sun was failing in our winter-shady canyon. The creeks are running and everything looks fine!

File.Name: a118b_a118-067.jpg
Notes: 58512062 Nothing catches raindrops like lupine leaves! These are new leaves of perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family in the woods this afternoon. It rained too hard for a really good rain drop photo - the leaves shed the hard drops - but this will do! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2010)

File.Name: a118c_a118-071.jpg
Notes:
58511566 Here are new flowers on the native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family - with raindrops! - even though they already flowered early in November this year. It's not a massive flowering like we usually get in January, but there are some new flowers for the hummingbirds. I don't know what that gall-like mass on the left is. This is what I saw - I did no color correction on this photo. The urn-like flowers are really beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2010)

We had a major winter storm yesterday and today. We got about 3.5 inches - on the low side of the 3-6 inches we were promised, but I'm not complaining! The rain was pretty intense, up to 1 inch/hour according to a neighbor's automatic rain gauge on the Web. Julie and I took a walk this afternoon when the rain mostly cleared out and the sun was failing in our winter-shady canyon. The creeks are running and everything looks fine!

File.Name: a119a_a119-113.jpg
Notes:
58544877 The local creeks are running after good rains so far this week! Note that the water is the color of weak tea - I reckon it's all the tannic acid being leached from several drought years' accumulation of fallen oak leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2010)

Maybe you can see the strange thick white foam that accumulates in the eddies of the flowing water. I'm sure this creek is not polluted in any way. Natural saponifiers?

We got another inch of rain today, and the biggest storm of the week (and maybe the year) is expected tomorrow. It was raining hard enough this morning that I called in a "rain day" to school and stayed home. But then the rain backed off to drizzle, so Julie and I went exploring!

It's strange - the phone has no dial tone, but the DSL is working. At least the line has its priorities right!
Locality: and maybe the year) is expected tomorrow.  It was raining hard enough this morning that I called in a "rain day" to school and stayed home.  But then the rain backed off to drizzle

File.Name: a119b_a119-085.jpg
Notes:
58544213 We have a river again! This is the Santa Ynez River at the first crossing of Paradise - that's the first place the road crosses the river in the section of the Santa Ynez River called Paradise above Lake Cachuma. It's flowing all the way to the lake. Nothing huge yet, but the ground is saturated and more rain is coming tomorrow! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 January 2010)

I remember an old 1950s comic book - maybe Bugs Bunny? - it involved a tornado. The lesson was "Go where it's been, not where it's going." We're fine at home on top of the mountains, but the river is where all the runoff water is going!

File.Name: a119c_a118-077.jpg
Notes:
58543538 The mushrooms know that it's been raining! This is an Earthstar (Astreus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The "rays" of the fruiting body fold back to lift up the spore sack inside when it's damp. When it's dry, they fold back up to enclose the spore sack. It's been very wet! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2010)

I have a photo that shows the dry fungus at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/29191472
(I know - it's kind of like a "rain rock" in the garden - if this rock is wet, it's raining. They're that good!)

The mushrooms know that it's raining in another way as well. Mushrooms are creatures that the rain brings, but I've noticed they don't usually appear while it's raining. I figure they can't spread their spores in the rain. Somehow they know!

We got another inch of rain today, and the biggest storm of the week (and maybe the year) is expected tomorrow.
Locality: I know - it's kind of like a "rain rock" in the garden - if this rock is wet

File.Name: a120a_a119-081.jpg
Notes:
58576294 We've been stuck in the house all day because of the rain, and so has this interesting bug! I guess it's am Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae or kin) in the order Hymenoptera. I dig those long antennae! Here he is looking out the sliding glass porch door at the rain on the deck beyond. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2010)

What's an interesting wasp like this doing inside our house on a rainy day like this??

We've had pretty good rain today, about 2 1/2 inches by my coffee can rain gauge on the porch - lower than expected, but always welcome. More rain will happen tomorrow. School was canceled today of the rain, so Julie and I went through the dusty old books on the bookshelves. Spring cleaning!

File.Name: a120b_a120-119.jpg
Notes:
58575737 We've been stuck in the house all day because of the rain, and so has this interesting bug! I guess it's a Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera - though it's not the common Giant Crane Fly (Holousia grandis). Here it is looking out the kitchen window at the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2010)

What's this interesting fly doing inside our house??

File.Name: a120c_a120-123.jpg
Notes:
58575284 Here is the little waterfall flowing next to our driveway in the woods! It falls about 20 feet total, though this photo only shows part of it. It's a small drainage and it takes a lot of rain to get it working. We've had pretty good rain today, about 2 1/2 inches by my coffee can rain gauge on the porch - lower than expected, but always welcome. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2010)

School was canceled today of the rain, so Julie and I went through the dusty books on the bookshelves. Spring cleaning!

File.Name: a121a_a121-133.jpg
Notes:
58606819 We call this the "Carpe Diem" tree at school - "Seize the day!" This grand Valley Oak (Quercus lobata in the Fagaceae family) tree has seized many days and weathered many storms. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2010)

I guess this tree is 150-200 years old. I can almost see the (now extinct) California Grizzly Bears ((Ursus californicus)) sharpening their claws on the trunk, and Native American Chumash hunters, and Californio vaqueros, and pioneer farmers, and hopeful new settlers and commuters all resting in the shade... and even middle school kids dancing!

This mighty tree of life is a good testament that even on a rainy day in winter, life goes on. I went back to school today despite the continuing rainfall. All is well.

We just got pounded by a thunderstorm of hail with thunder and close lightening about 1/2 hour ago! I quickly shut my computer down, but I started it up again after it was quiet for a while. The temperature is 34 degrees Fahrenheit. We'll probably get some snow tonight.
Locality: Ursus californicus)) sharpening their claws on the trunk

File.Name: a121b_a121-145.jpg
Notes:
58606394 The Santa Ynez River is flowing into Lake Cachuma. This is the view at the upper end of the lake this afternoon. It's really a dammed (damned!) reservoir. It was about 20 feet down, but I'll bet it's filling fast. (Santa Ynez Valley, 21 January 2010)

Heh - three of us foolish friends rafted the river from Gibraltor Dam down to the first crossing after the "March Miracle" rains of 1991 when we got almost 20 inches of rain in one week! (We only got about 8 inches this week.) The river was cranking at least 15,000 cfs. We got to the end after dark to be greeted by the Search and Rescue people that Julie had called, who were wondering what to do about us. All I can say is YAHOO! (And NEVER AGAIN!)
Locality: We only got about 8 inches this week.)  The river was cranking at least 15

File.Name: a121c_a119-095.jpg
Notes: 58605600 The Santa Ynez River is running again, but it's nowhere near flood stage. Here is Julie looking up a still-dry overflow channel of the river bed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 January 2010)

File.Name: a122a_a116-052.jpg
Notes:
58635653 This is the shell of a native Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods. This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in town and at school. We don't have those snails here in the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

I rarely notice the live native snails in the woods, though I find their shells - maybe they are nocturnal? See my one photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9497119/

We've had rain, rain, rain this week. We even had some snow today, but I was away at school so I missed it. A mostly sunny weekend is expected. I'm ready!

File.Name: a122b_a116-040.jpg
Notes:
58635266 These are the fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) in the woods. They are very tough - like the rotting wood they grow from, only more so. The ripe fruiting bodies are about an inch tall. They look like tiny deer or moose antlers! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2010)

I don't think these qualify as "mushrooms" - they are the fruiting bodies of fungi, but they are not by any stretch of the imagination fleshy!

File.Name: a122c_a118-075.jpg
Notes:
58635112 The soggy Lepiota mushrooms are the only mushrooms I know that fruit in the rain. This one is coming up through the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves in the woods after two days of rain this week. I have no idea what species this is - a good reason not to consider eating it. It's too waterlogged anyway! (San Marcos Pass, 18 January 2010)

Mushrooms are creatures that the rain brings, but most of our mushrooms seem to wait until the rain is done so that they can spread their spores.

We've had rain, rain, rain this week, but a sunny weekend is expected. I'm ready!

File.Name: a123a_a123-006.jpg
Notes:
58663614 Here is a beautiful - and tasty! - Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the soggy woods today! It's the first one I've found this year, so I guess the season is on. Of course, we collected it for dinner tonight. This is o+ne time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

Mushrooms are creatures that the rain brings, but I've noticed they don't usually appear while it's raining. I figure they can't spread their spores in the rain. I love the exceptions!

We had about 9 inches of rain last week - respectable, but not the 20 inches I was hoping for. But I'm not complaining! The storms came through fast, and they came from the west rather than the south, so we didn't get our usual "orographic" enhancement up here on the Pass.

File.Name: a123b_a123-012.jpg
Notes: 58663159 I found this group of interesting Common Club Coral (Clavariadelphus pistillaris or kin) mushrooms in the woods today after our rains this week. They are related to the beautiful coral mushrooms, but they don't branch like coral mushrooms do. Are they edible? My book says they are "Harmless. The taste and texture are reminiscent of stale rope." (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

File.Name: a123c_a123-010.jpg
Notes: 58662627 This is a handsome Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom - but it's not edible. For one thing, it's too soggy after all the rain this week. But apart from the rain, it has an unpleasant phenol-like taste and is poisonous to many people. Pity - because it looks so tasty! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

File.Name: a124a_a124-075.jpg
Notes: 58688605 Here is a tiny <i>Mycena acicula<i> mushroom coming up in our old woodpile - with my pen for scale. My pen has a ball of 0.7 mm, so the cap is only about 4 mm across. The small cap is only about 1.5 mm across! This is the tiniest mushroom in the woods for which I know a proper name. It's that beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)

File.Name: a124b_a124-040.jpg
Notes:
58687457 Someone's been collecting mushrooms in one of MY SECRET PLACES on the pass, and they're doing it all wrong! I'll call this mushroom collector a "he", but he could just as well be a "she".

There are 4 things wrong with this photo:

1. He pulled the mushroom from the ground instead of gently lifting it out. He must have missed the large saclike volva at the base of the stem. This could be a fatal mistake with a different mushroom!

2. He left the mushroom on the ground just like this! Please, always replace mushrooms that you examine but don't collect. Put them back in the earth in the same hole that you got them from, and tamp then down. It's a courtesy to other mushroom hunters who will follow, and the mushroom might reconnect with its mycelium.

3. He handled this mushroom way too much. You can hardly recognize the cottony whitish patch of volval tissue on the cap!

4. He didn't collect it! This is a beautiful and tasty Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - in the woods growing under a non-native pine and native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae) family.

Please collect mushrooms responsibly!
(San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: a124c_a123-002.jpg
Notes:
58686097 I posted a photo of a tasty Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom yesterday, and here is a tasty Blewitt (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods today! I found this mushroom growing under the Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) close to the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)

Be careful - the mushroom caps start blue, but they soon fade to gray or tan. Yet they always have a "blewitt aura" that is recognizable. We have a blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. Caveat emptor!
ScientificName: Cantharellus cibarius; Clitocybe nuda; Rubus ursinus; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor
associatedTaxa: Cantharellus cibarius; Clitocybe nuda; Rubus ursinus; Cortinarius; Caveat emptor

File.Name: a125a_a124-083.jpg
Notes:
58723712 Yum - a Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom from the woods for dinner! It's one time us knowledgeable naturalist peasants can eat like kings! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)

This was the fate of the mushroom from last Saturday:
  http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58663614

We always "dry saute" these fine mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Add a bit of fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil. Delicious!

File.Name: a125b_a125-088.jpg
Notes:
58723428 This beautiful Bolbitius vitellinus mushroom picked an interesting place to live - on a pile of native Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes) droppings! The wood rats usually build big beaver-like mounds of sticks in the woods, and this rat's house was overflowing. I got this photo in the woods by Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 January 2010)

Is it edible? Well, consider the source! The flesh of these mushrooms if very thin and fragile. Even on a different substrate, there's not much to it.

File.Name: a125c_a125-103.jpg
Notes: 58723263 I found this beautiful Boletus flaviporus mushroom in the woods by Lake Cachuma today on my way to school. It has brilliant yellow pores (not gills) that do not stain blue when handled. The caps are very slimy when damp - it was hard to pick up! My mushroom book says it is "edible, but mediocre." (Santa Ynez Valley, 25 January 2010)

File.Name: a126a_a123-022.jpg
Notes:
58749582 Interesting fly (order Diptera) in the woods. Actually the fly looks pretty ordinary, but it's behavior is interesting. It was walking round and round on the outside of these flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

This fly wasn't interested in the throat of the flower where the nectar is hidden - just the outside of the flowers. (Maybe it could squeeze into a flower, but it would have a tough time turning around to get out!) I don't know what it was getting.

I might be able to ID this fly from the wing venation in the photo...
Locality: Maybe it could squeeze into a flower

File.Name: a126b_a123-030.jpg
Notes: 58748998 The Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera are coming out from under the oak leaves on a sunny day after a week of rain. We don't have a great convergence of these pretty beetles so far this year, but there are "a few" in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

File.Name: a126c_a123-033.jpg
Notes:
58748779 From the angular shape of the cap and the yellow stains on the cap and stem, I'm pretty sure this is a young Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees along the road in the woods. This mushroom's secret veils are still intact. They will become a skirtlike "ring" on the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

The unpleasant phenol-disinfectant smell (and worse taste) and the yellow-staining cap are signs that this mushroom is poisonous.

I went back the next day to get another photo, and the mushroom was kicked to pieces. I suspect the neighbor's teenage son. No problem - kicking mushrooms is a fine way to propagate spores!
Locality:
and worse taste) and the yellow-staining cap are signs that this mushroom is poisonous.

I went back the next day to get another photo

File.Name: a127a_a127-179.jpg
Notes:
58781017 Here is an enormous cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms growing from the base of a nearly dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the valley near school. I guess there's almost a full bushel of mushroom-mass in this cluster! (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2010)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous, causing intense sweating and stomach issues. The stems are tough and have a greenish tinge, and they always grow from wood, sometimes buried. Tomorrow I'll show what's left of the "tree" they are growing from!

Now that work is done, and I can finally hear the rest of President Obama's speech tonight...

File.Name: a127b_a127-169.jpg
Notes:
58780594 I found this interesting Amanita mushroom in the woods today. It has a cap covered with cottony universal-veil fragments and no sign of a volva at the base of the stem. It was growing in sandy soil. It definately has "amanita vibes"! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2010)

I first thought it might be the Pine Cone Amanita (Amanita magniverrucata, but the warts on the cap aren't distinct enough. Now I guess this is the Woodland Amanita (Amanita silvicola). It has a pretty distinct ring on the stem, but see the photos at:
   http://mushroomobserver.org/image/image_search?pattern=amanita+silvicola

Edibility is unknown. Do not experiment!
Locality: Amanita silvicola). It has a pretty distinct ring on the stem

File.Name: a127c_a123-024.jpg
Notes:
58779987 Here is another photo of the little fly (order Diptera) working the outside of the pretty flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2010)

I can't ID it - the wings are too indistinct in my photos, and I'm not good enough with the keys any longer, and life's too short! But it's interesting behavior for a fly.

File.Name: a128a_a128-187.jpg
Notes:
58811478 Under the sea?

I believe this is the strange "Crested White Coral" (Clavulina cristata) mushroom, growing from the leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near home. It's only about an inch (2.5 cm) tall. I haven't found these in the woods for several drought years, but they're easy to overlook. Maybe they need good rains? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2010)
CommonNames: Crested White Coral; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: a128b_a128-183.jpg
Notes: 58810905 Here is a slimy mass of "Witch's Butter" (Tremella mesenterica or kin) fungus growing in the woods on a fallen oak branch. I guess it's edible. It's said to be "harmless but flavorless" - so why bother? These gelatinous "mushrooms" are mostly water. They shrivel up to almost nothing when dry and can revive quickly after a rain. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2010)

File.Name: a128c_a127-178.jpg
Notes:
58810361 The last branch of a once-mighty Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by school came down during the storms last week. Look close at the base of the trunk under the fallen branch, and you can see the enormous bright orange cluster of Jack-O-Lantern (Omphalotus olivascens) mushrooms that I showed in my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2010)

I've read that this fungus causes heart rot in trees, and I know that several dying oak trees in the woods where I once found the mushroom have since fallen over. Is this mushroom the reason why this grand oak finally lost the battle to survive?

File.Name: a129a_a128-185.jpg
Notes:
58839813 This is one of those WTF photos! It shows some kind of spiral-shaped animal scat (aka turds) in the woods. The individual turds are about one inch (2.5 cm) long - though they would be longer if straightened out. I moved them into the light, but I found them in one pile like this. They look bird-like with that white splotch, but what bird has turds like this and visits the same place for a bathroom? WTF? (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2010)

I would appreciate any help with this one. I know where they are, so I'll collect them tomorrow to examine. "Eschatology" or "Scatology" - I'm up for it!

File.Name: a129b_a124-055.jpg
Notes:
58839229 Here is a beautiful plant of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This one has red tubular flowers hidden among the leafy red bracts. There are two stamens bundled inside each flower - you can see the outlines in silhouette - along with the single exerted pistil. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)

They come up like mushrooms in the woods. This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place. They are having a good year!

File.Name: a129c_a124-047.jpg
Notes: 58838934 I believe these little mushrooms in the woods are the "Lackluster Laccaria" (Laccaria Laccata). They live up to their name! They are common "trail mushrooms" - living in the sandy soil along trails. They are attractive when fresh, with faint violet colors to the gills. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2010)

File.Name: a130a_a130-039.jpg
Notes:
58866706 To paraphrase William Burroughs, "Nobody owns life, but anyone who can pick this mushroom owns death."

Here is the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found I found deep in the woods today where the sun never shines. It's remarkable to hold this mushroom knowing that it is deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold or photograph it!

You can see the "death cup" - really the saclike volva - at the base of the stem. A bit of the volva is stuck to the cap. It will also has a "ring" on the stem, though this one hasn't developed yet. It's one of our most elegant mushrooms - pristine white, like finding a styrofoam cup in the woods. Enjoy it, but don't eat it! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

File.Name: a130b_a130-012.jpg
Notes:
58866359 This may not be our tastiest Bolete mushroom - though it is edible - but it is certainly our most beautiful! Here is a Zeller's Bolete (Boletus zelleri) mushroom in the woods today.

It has a brilliant color combo - dark "fuscous" cap with a frosted "glaucous" look, yellow pores (no gills), and a deep red stem. This pretty 'shroom is growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

File.Name: a130c_a130-014.jpg
Notes:
58865848 Beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees today. The wide-spaced gills are especially handsome. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

These little mushrooms have wiry stems and an interesting connection with lichens - they have a remarkable ability to dry out and then revive after new rain. You can wear them like earrings if you're ears are pierced!

File.Name: a131a_a130-001.jpg
Notes:
58893706 This is the smallest "mushroom" I've ever noticed! I have no idea what it is, but I don't think it has gills. It's growing on a fallen leaf of the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. My pen has a ball of 0.7 mm, so the cap is only about 1 mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

I'm not sure how I noticed this tiny 'shroom. Today's photos are all tiny critters that I somehow managed to notice!

File.Name: a131b_a130-033.jpg
Notes: 58893305 Look close! This tiny fly (order Diptera) in the woods is ovipositing - laying her eggs - on the ovule of a native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) flower in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

File.Name: a131c_a130-052.jpg
Notes:
58892922 Tiny fly (order Diptera) resting on a branch in the woods. I captured this critter at 1:1 magnification and cropped. This is as close as I can go with natural light! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2010)

Maybe this is one of those gnats that dance in the sunbeams?

File.Name: a201a_a130-034.jpg
Notes:
58923430 Here is the strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods, the first I've seen this year. It's said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. Sometimes when you touch the caps, they shoot off little clouds of spores! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

I think of this as a spring mushroom - it comes out later in our rain year rather than sooner. Soon there will be wildflowers and insects to photograph!

File.Name: a201b_a130-050.jpg
Notes:
58923078 Here is another strange "Crested White Coral" (Clavulina cristata) mushroom, growing from the leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near home. It's small - only an inch or so (2.5 cm) across. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

I love these wonderful creatures that "strange nature" produces!
CommonNames: Crested White Coral; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: a201c_a130-046.jpg
Notes:
58922866 Here's another photo using my pen as a ruler. These look like little puffball mushrooms, but I'm pretty sure they are Slime Molds (Myxomycetes) growing from an old piece of oak bark in the woods. I suspect this critter (or critters) is Lycogala epidendrum. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm which makes the slime molds about 5 mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part protist. They live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as these tiny little mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

That dried-mucous-like stuff in the photo may be the remnant of the plasmodium. It looks like a new "cap" is forming just below the "puffball" my pen is pointing at.

File.Name: a202a_a130-007.jpg
Notes:
58952024 I found this pretty little "true bug" (order Hemiptera) - maybe in the family Rhopalidae or Lygaeidae? - perched on a sunny branch of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I guess they overwinter as adults. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

It looks like a Western Box Elder Bug (family Rhopalidae), but if so it's lost! The closest Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) trees are about 30 miles west of us, though there are related native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) trees in the Aceraceae plant family growing nearby.

This is about the only time of year that we have insects, wildflowers, and mushrooms all at the same time. It takes some subtle shifting of camera settings that I often forget!
Locality:
Acer macrophyllum) trees in the Aceraceae plant family growing nearby.

This is about the only time of year that we have insects
CommonNames: true bug; Bush Monkey Flower; Western Box Elder Bug; Box Elder; Bigleaf Maple
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Rhopalidae; Lygaeidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Rhopalidae; Acer negundo; californicum; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Rhopalidae; Lygaeidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Rhopalidae; Acer negundo; californicum; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

File.Name: a202b_a131-064.jpg
Notes: 58951514 Here is a beautiful new flower of the well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) in the Primulaceae plant family. Spring is coming! (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2010)

File.Name: a202c_a130-019.jpg
Notes:
58951323 I'm pretty sure this is a large cluster of Hygrophorus sordidus mushrooms in a nest of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens). The mushrooms are hefty - 10 to 20 cm across. They are said to be "edible but not choice". It's a pity, as they are a large and surprising find. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

We are fortunate to have a healthy community of native grasses (Poaceae) in our canyon. They are getting scarce in the mountains because of the introduced weedy grasses.

File.Name: a203a_a203-073.jpg
Notes:
58986050 These are sporophytes of tiny Liverworts (Marchantia or Asterella?) - related to mosses - on the ground still damp from recent rains. My first thought is surfing palm trees! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2010)

These are primitive plants related to mosses, but they're still going strong. The woods are full of surprises when you look close!

File.Name: a203b_a203-069.jpg
Notes:
58985210 I can't get enough of these new Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) flowers in the Scrophulariaceae plant family! This native wildflower is having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2010)

In this photo with backlight, you can make out the "wiring diagram" of the tubular flowers - especially in the lower left. The top "wire" goes to the pistil - which is exposed on the right, but still included on the left. The lower two "wires" go to the dark stamens that keep inside the flowers waiting for pollinators. The flowers have two short "lower lips" that are green.

This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in this place.

Now it gets interesting! It turns out that the buds of this Indian Warrior plant are sold in the internet "legal high" market. The effects are described as "sweet in aroma and tranquilizing" (Wikipedia), and they sell for quite a bit. There were probably $100s of dollars worth of the buds in the place where I found these today.

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bugs me!

File.Name: a203c_a130-030.jpg
Notes: 58983484 After several days with my mushroom book, I finally think these are new Crepidotus mollis mushrooms growing from a wound on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. What had me stumped was the dark fibrillose caps. These mushrooms are usually quite bare and light-colored, but I mostly find old caps. In age, they become quite flabby and boring, but the young caps are interesting. Sound familiar? (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

File.Name: a204a_a131-062.jpg
Notes:
59015531 Finally, here are beautiful flowers of the native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The hanging deep-red flowers are classic hummingbird flowers. You won't confuse them with the other native gooseberry's, but it's interesting that they all flower together, early in the season. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2010)

Look at the flowers from left to right. On the left, the male anthers are barely out of the "gate" and the female style with two pistils is well ahead. In the middle, the anthers are catching up. And on the right, the anthers have caught up with and surpassed the pistils. Many flowers are like this - allow for pollination, but self-pollinate to make sure!

Today's photos are a trio of native Gooseberry shrubs in the genus Ribes. They have something in common, but they are different too. This is what keeps natural history interesting!

I've had a cold for days. *Blah*

File.Name: a204b_a131-055.jpg
Notes:
59014781 Here is the native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family flowering in a different canyon. It's built differently than the Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in my [Previous] photo. This plant has thorns, but the leaves and pedicels don't have hairs or glands, and the anthers are a pale color. Despite what my flora says, the flowers seem to have three pistils extending beyond the stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2010)

We don't seem to have these in our canyon at home, but I find them a mile or two up the road. These little differences in the plants and where the grow keep the botanists busy!

File.Name: a204c_a130-004.jpg
Notes:
59014123 This is the native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon. The flowers have glandular hairy leaves and pedicels, and they have dark maroon-colored anthers. They also seem to have two pistils extending beyond the stamens. Pretty - I always think of ballet dancers when I see them! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

Despite the name, the berries taste sweet - if you know how to eat them. You have to bite down with your front teeth to get past the thorns, then the innards are tasty.

File.Name: a205a_a124-068.jpg
Notes:
59030350 These shy Deer Mushrooms (Pluteus cervinus) are growing from the wood of an old California Bay (Umbellularia californica) stump that has split the sandstone rock in the woods. They are hidden back in the crevice of the rock. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

I'm posting early today. My school talent show is tonight, so it will be a late night.

File.Name: a205b_a116-035.jpg
Notes: 59030056 I believe this is a Lentinus lepidus mushroom growing from dead oak wood. The rough serrated gills are distinctive. The mushrooms are tough, even in old specimens like this one. Said to be edible when young. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2010)

File.Name: a205c_a125-095.jpg
Notes: 59029791 Beautiful Boletus flaviporus mushroom in the woods by Lake Cachuma. It has brilliant yellow pores (not gills) that do not stain blue when handled. The caps are very slimy. (Santa Ynez Valley, 1 February 2010)

File.Name: a206a_a205-134.jpg
Notes:
59070224 Where is the end of the rainbow? No problem - it's right over there, at the base the oak tree to the right!

I got this photo driving home on Highway 154 after school yesterday. It was a beautiful full rainbow, but I was most interested in the end of the rainbow - it ended before the trees! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2010)

You can see a couple of interesting optical effects in my photo. The tree branches seem brighter outside the bow, and there is a foggy area inside the base of the bow that I don't think is raindrops on my lens.

We've had about 2.1 inches of rain yesterday and today. It's welcome, but less than expected - like all our recent storms. It rained most of today. I spent the day working through my school talent show photos from last night. I only had a brief window of time to get photos between when it cleared and when we lost the light, so I stuck with what I know.

Tomorrow should be an interesting new day in the woods!

File.Name: a206b_a206-003.jpg
Notes: 59069636 This is a tiny Mycena acicula mushroom coming up in our old woodpile, and a fallen brother. I included my pen as a ruler. My pen has a ball of 0.7 mm, so this cap is about 8 mm across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom I know. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2010)

File.Name: a206c_a206-001.jpg
Notes:
59069355 These are the light-trails of the tiny gnats (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after a rain - and a few raindrops. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so! (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2010)

I set my camera to it's lowest ISO setting and took the photo at 0.5 sec @ f/32. A ND filter would help. You have to slow things down to get these action shots!

File.Name: a207a_a207-020.jpg
Notes:
59099144 I guess this is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) mushroom that I found in the woods today growing under a Madrone tree (Arbutus menziesii) in the Ericaceae plant family. I've also found them under related Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). It's saucer-sized, and it has very soft flesh. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2010)

My first thought at finding this mushroom is that it is the yellow-form of Amanita muscaria, but the structure of the volva is wrong - it's collar-like, not scaly. There's not much sign of a "ring" on the stem.

Pretty as it is, this mushroom is quite poisonous. It probably contains the same "interesting" toxins as A. muscaria and A. pantherina.

We had rain for the last two days, so I wasn't expecting to find new mushrooms today - but here they are!
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Amanita muscaria; A. muscaria; A. pantherina

File.Name: a207b_a207-010.jpg
Notes: 59098010 Maybe this mushroom in the woods today is Phaeocollybia olivacea? It was growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I first thought Cortinarius, but it has no sign of a cortina, and it has a very long stem - almost a foot long! The cap is very slimy. It's a striking mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2010)

File.Name: a207c_a207-030.jpg
Notes: 59097516 Here is a brilliant Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It looks beautiful, and it has an interesting hot spicy taste if you just nibble. But as the name suggests, it is quite poisonous. Not "drop down dead" poisonous, but "wish you were dead" stomach upset. There is a rumor that parboiling destroys the toxins. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2010)

File.Name: a208a_a208-041.jpg
Notes:
59139309 I found this pollen-dusted fly (order Diptera) with a mighty proboscis in the woods today working new flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Buckthorn (Rhamnaceae) plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2010)

It looks gnat-like, but I suspect it is an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. See BugGuide at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/9861

File.Name: a208b_a208-039.jpg
Notes: 59139002 Here are native wildflowers of Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae) plant family growing in the mountains. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2010)

File.Name: a208c_a208-037.jpg
Notes:
59138827 This is a hefty Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - growing in the woods today under a non-native pine and native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the Ericaceae) plant family. This beautiful mushroom is soft to the touch. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2010)

It's edible, but I generally avoid all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. My mushroom book describes it as having a "fishy" taste.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita

File.Name: a209a_a207-008.jpg
Notes:
59156258 The Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - are stirring in the canyon, despite the continuing rain. Look at the water drop hanging from the bottom lady beetle! (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2010)

These beetles converge for the winter in our canyon, among others. Some years we have thousands, but this year we only have dozens. I don't know what makes the difference.

File.Name: a209b_a207-011.jpg
Notes:
59155651 This is another photo of the Cortinarius-like mushroom in the woods that I posted a few days ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/59098010

I believe this is Phaeocollybia olivacea (or kin). It has no apparent cortina, and it has a very long stem - almost as long as my foot, and I probably broke it when I picked it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2010)

Edibility is unknown. I wouldn't mess with it.

File.Name: a209c_a209-049.jpg
Notes:
59155093 There was a rainbow in the canyon when I got home from school today. Julie said it also snowed for about 15 minutes, but I missed it again. (San Marcos Pass, 9 February 2010)

We had another 6/10 inch of rain today. This rainbow happened at the end of the storm when the rains were becoming showery. The rains have been moderate but well-spaced this year, and above normal. I'm looking forward to a beautiful spring!

File.Name: a210a_a210-053.jpg
Notes:
59192768 I found this impressive Tachinid Fly (family Tachinidae in the order Diptera) in the woods today. It's working new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2010)

I recognize the fly because of it's scooped out face and bristly abdomen. It interesting that these flies are among the first and last insects I find in the woods as the seasons turn. The adults visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. Different insects make their larvae in the spring and in the fall to keep the flies busy.

The manzanitas are having an interesting year. They flowered very early this season after our great October rain. But now when they should be flowering, there are only occasional flowers. This is not good for the hummingbirds and insects, but I hope they'll make do.
ScientificName: Tachinidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Tachinidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: a210c_a210-052.jpg
Notes: 59191994 Here are tiny little mushrooms growing "under the hood" of a "Bracket fungus" - maybe "Turkey Tail" (Trametes versicolor) or kin. I guess the tiny mushrooms are genus Mycena. They are growing at about head high on a dead Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2010)

File.Name: a211a_a210-063.jpg
Notes:
59220426 This is a beautiful Constricted Grisette (Amanita constricta) mushroom the woods by Lake Cachuma on the way to school. It looks like a "normal amanita" but it has no partial veil ring on the stem. My mushroom book describes it perfectly: "the gray, conspicuously striate cap, absence of a partial veil, and peculiar constricted volva are the hallmarks of this Amanita... and frequent presence of universal veil tissue on the cap." I'll show a photo of the constricted volva tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2010)

It's edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is another distinctive mushroom that I should try. I'm sure I won't mistake it for a deadly amanita.

File.Name: a211b_a210-060.jpg
Notes: 59219978 I guess this is the Woodland Amanita (Amanita silvicola) - but it sure looked like the deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) when I found it growing in the woods! It has a smooth white cap without the powdery warts I've seen before. But you can see that it doesn't have a sac-like volva at the base of the stem like it's deadly sister. Edibility is unknown. Don't experiment! (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2010)

File.Name: a211c_a208-034.jpg
Notes:
59219674 Here is the beautiful Coccora mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - Amanita calyptrata, aka A. calyptroderma - growing in the woods today under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees. This beautiful mushroom is soft to the touch and the eyes. Despite the Italian name, it is only known from the west coast of the US. This is the pale form that fruits in the spring. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2010)

The mushroom is covered with a thick layer of the universal veil material that once covered the whole mushroom in an "amanita egg".

It's edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't too encouraging.
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; A. calyptroderma; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita

File.Name: a212a_a211-102.jpg
Notes:
59247143 Kids in trees - where they are supposed to be!

I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek to the base of Grass Mountain yesterday. This is looking up into a grand old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Trees to climb, mushrooms to find, the creek is running, the sun is *almost* shining - what fun! (Santa Ynez Valley, 11 February 2010)

(*Hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

File.Name: a212b_a211-070.jpg
Notes:
59246875 Here is the real thing! I found this beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom just up the hill from home yesterday. It looks soiled by the dirt it's pushed up. It's remarkable to hold this mushroom knowing that it is deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold or photograph it. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2010)

You can't see the "death cup" - really the saclike volva - at the base of the stem in this photo, but you can see the pure white cap and "ring" on the stem. It has an "amanita aura". It's one of our most elegant mushrooms. Enjoy it, but don't eat it!

File.Name: a212c_a210-065.jpg
Notes:
59246624 This is another photo of the Constricted Grisette (Amanita constricta) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. Here you can see the "constricted" volva at the base of the stem. The lower portion of the volva is confined around the stem, but the upper margin flares outward. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2010)

It's edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is another distinctive mushroom that I should try sometime. I'm sure I won't mistake it for the deadly amanita.

I got these photos deep in the woods late in the day when everything was in shade. The "white balance" is a little funky.

File.Name: a213a_a213-014.jpg
Notes:
59271244 Something happened in the woods today - it feels like spring!

Here is a perfect California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly getting moisture from a bank of moss and lichen on a rock in the woods. There were half a dozen butterflies fluttering about, and this one relaxed enough to let me get close with my camera. Thank you! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

This is the first butterfly I've managed to photograph this year!

I believe this butterfly is a migrant that overwinters here. I don't know that it actually breeds here, but we have lots of their favorite Ceanothus host plants in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The whole concept of butterflies migrating over great distances is astounding!

File.Name: a213b_a213-011.jpg
Notes:
59270579 "Blah - I waited all winter to pupate for this???"

This unfortunate little Moth (Lepidoptera) has its extended leg caught in a stray spider web. It has beautiful antennae and stuffed-olive eyes. I don't think any spider was attending the web. It was like a "ghost net" that keeps on killing fish after it is lost. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

The moth was struggling when I found it, but it quieted down. When I came back an hour later, it was gone. I don't know if it got free or some predator got it.

File.Name: a213c_a213-031.jpg
Notes:
59269953 Tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on a blushing flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

I watched them for a while - they are like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend their time chasing each other away.

File.Name: a214a_a213-037.jpg
Notes:
59298326 Mating Convergent Lady Beetles - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. But that "fifth wheel" ladybug gets my sympathy. Is he left out of the party because he has no spots? (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

Julie and I are flying off to Portland, Oregon to visit the grandkids! I love flying, but I hate packing. I'll be glad when we're on our way at *gulp* 6:00 AM tomorrow morning. No new photos until we get back next Saturday.

File.Name: a214b_a213-028.jpg
Notes: 59297862 Striking charcoal-black Leptonia mushrooms in the pink-spored Entolomataceae family. Maybe they are Leptonia parva or kin? They're growing from the fallen leaves under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2010)

associatedTaxa: Leptonia; Entolomataceae; Leptonia parva; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: a214c_a214-053.jpg
Notes:
59297521 Beautiful flowers of a native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2010)

There was a single Black-chinned Hummingbird (Archilochus alexandri) guarding this prolific shrub. Talk about greedy!

File.Name: a220a_a219-148.jpg
Notes:
59508709 "Oh, baby, baby, it's a wild world..."

I think this is my grandchild Otto looking out the window - but it might be Kelvin. It's hard to tell the twins apart when they're by themselves! (Portland, Oregon, 19 February 2010)

We had a wonderful visit with our kids and grandkids this week in Portland, Oregon. But I'm glad to be back home with my beloved mushrooms and wildflowers and insects!

File.Name: a220b_a219-085.jpg
Notes: 59508236 My grandkid Kelvin helps "unload" the laundry - with perfect light. (Portland, Oregon, 19 February 2010)
